Annotation Review   101 tasks

#1 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Expert (5) nodes: 21 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_af50243e-3a60-4460-9536-f9a02c4f8eb8 ▼
Problem
Context: Generalized (or weakened) quantum measurements introduce qualitatively new features in fundamental quantum physics that do not appear with simple projective measurements. Moreover, sequences of weak measurements can be made effectively continuous, resulting in quantum state evolutions described by quantum stochastic processes. Crucially, continuous quantum monitoring makes feedback contro…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 0.5, Item: Defines the measurement operator \\( \\hat{\\cal M}\_{\\delta t} = (\\delta t / 2 \\pi \\tau)^{1/4} \\exp\\big\[-\\frac{\\delta t}{4 \\tau}(r-\\hat{\\sigma}\_3)^2\\big\]\\), where \\(\\tau = S_0/2\\Delta \\bar{\\mathcal{I}}^2\\) is the characteristic measureme…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires research-frontier knowledge spanning quantum measurement theory, stochastic Schrödinger equations, path integral formalism, and solid-state qubit physics simultaneously.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 Demands first-principles derivation of a stochastic path integral from scratch, extremization of action functionals, and derivation of canonical equations of motion in a doubled phase space — all novel reasoning chains.
conceptual_leaps
5/5 Multiple non-obvious insights required: doubling state space to form canonical phase space, identifying the conjugate momenta structure, recognizing postselection as boundary conditions, and connecting to the Zeno regime limit.
plan_length
5/5 The solution involves 10+ major derivation stages: defining measurement operators, writing joint PDFs, taking continuum limits, deriving master equations, constructing path integrals, extremizing the action, writing ODEs, solving QND case analytically, and characterizing jump events.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: the path integral representation depends on the discretized PDF, the ODEs depend on the stochastic Hamiltonian, the qubit-specific equations depend on the general framework — any error propagates through all downstream results.
Justification: —
Step graph — 21 nodes | bottlenecks: #5, #7, #9, #12, #13 The solution is computation-heavy, with the two deepest bottlenecks being the first-order expansion of ln P (node 7) and the action extremization yielding the general ODEs (node 12); all regime-specific analyses branch from these two nodes, making errors there globally propagating.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as stochastic path integral complexity: 4/5
Decide to construct a probability distribution over trajectories in quantum state space, analogous to classical rare-event path integrals, with postselection as boundary conditions.
#2 [strategic] Double quantum state space for canonical phase space complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Choose to introduce conjugate momenta alongside the quantum state coordinates to form a canonical phase space that enables extremization of a stochastic action.
#3 [conceptual] Define QPC measurement operator M_δt complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Specify the Gaussian measurement operator M_δt = (δt/2πτ)^{1/4} exp[−(δt/4τ)(r−σ̂_3)²] with τ = S_0/(2Δ Ī²) as the characteristic measurement time for the quantum point contact.
#4 [conceptual] Write solid-state qubit Hamiltonian complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify Ĥ = (ε/2)σ̂_3 + (−Δ/2)σ̂_1 with energy asymmetry ε and tunneling strength Δ as the system Hamiltonian for the qubit.
#5 [conceptual] Parametrize density matrix as Bloch-like vector q complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Expand the density operator ρ in the d = N²−1 generalized Gell-Mann operator basis so that the quantum state is represented as a real coordinate vector q with d components.
#6 [conceptual] Formulate joint PDF with preselection/postselection BCs complexity: 3/5 ← #3, #5
Write P = δ^d(q_0−q_I)δ^d(q_n−q_F) ∏_{k=0}^{n-1} P(q_{k+1},r_k|q_k) to encode pre- and postselected boundary conditions into the trajectory probability.
#7 [computational] Expand ln P(r|ρ̂) to first order in δt complexity: 4/5 ← #3, #5
Compute ln P(r|ρ̂) ≈ −(r²−2rz+1)δt/(2τ) + (1/2)ln(δt/2πτ) + O(δt²) by substituting the measurement operator and expanding the Gaussian trace to linear order in δt.
#8 [computational] Identify the log-probability functional F[q,r] complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Read off the leading δt coefficient to define F[q,r] = −(r²−2rz+1)/(2τ), which will serve as the measurement contribution to the stochastic action.
#9 [conceptual] Introduce canonical momenta and stochastic Hamiltonian structure complexity: 5/5 ← #2, #6, #8
Perform a Hubbard–Stratonovich-style Legendre transform on the discrete path sum to introduce conjugate momenta p and define H[q,p,r] = p·L[q,r] + F[q,r] plus δ-function boundary terms.
#10 [computational] Write full stochastic Hamiltonian with boundary terms complexity: 4/5 ← #4, #9
Assemble H[q,p,r] = p·L[q,r] + F[q,r] − p·(q−q_I)δ(t) − p·(q−q_F)δ(t−T) incorporating the qubit evolution superoperator L and the pre/postselection delta constraints.
#11 [computational] Write stochastic path integral ∫Dp e^S complexity: 3/5 ← #10
Express P = ∫Dp exp[∫_0^T dt(−p·q̇ + H[q,p,r])] as the continuum limit of the discrete trajectory sum after integrating over conjugate momenta.
#12 [computational] Extremize action to derive general optimal-path ODEs complexity: 5/5 ← #10
Take functional derivatives δS/δq = 0, δS/δp = 0, δS/δr = 0 to obtain the three equations −q̇ + L[q,r] = 0, ṗ + ∂_q(p·L + F) = 0, and ∂_r(p·L + F) = 0 with forced BCs.
#13 [computational] Substitute qubit Bloch vector, write 3+3 ODEs and constraint complexity: 4/5 ← #4, #12
Project the general optimal-path equations onto the qubit Bloch coordinates (x,y,z) and their conjugate momenta (p_x,p_y,p_z) to obtain the six explicit ODEs ẋ,ẏ,ż,ṗ_x,ṗ_y,ṗ_z and the measurement-record constraint r = z + p_z(1−z²) − p_x xz − p_y yz.
#14 [verification] Recover stochastic master equation without postselection complexity: 3/5 ← #7, #12
Set p = 0 (no postselection) and verify that the q̇ equation reproduces the known stochastic master equation ∂_t ρ̂ = −(i/ℏ)[Ĥ,ρ̂] + (r/2τ){σ̂_3,ρ̂} − (r/τ)⟨σ̂_3⟩ρ̂, confirming consistency.
#15 [conceptual] Specialize to QND regime by setting Δ = 0 complexity: 2/5 ← #13
Observe that with tunneling Δ = 0 the qubit undergoes quantum non-demolition measurement, simplifying the ODEs and allowing analytical progress for the most-likely trajectory.
#16 [computational] Solve QND most-likely paths x̄(t), ȳ(t), z̄(t) analytically complexity: 4/5 ← #15
Integrate the reduced ODEs with constant r̄ to obtain x̄(t) = (x_I cosεt − y_I sinεt)/(cosh r̄t/τ + z_I sinh r̄t/τ) and analogous expressions for ȳ, z̄, then fix r̄ from the boundary condition z̄(T) = z_F.
#17 [conceptual] Reduce to polar angle θ in quantum Zeno regime complexity: 3/5 ← #13
In the limit τ ≪ Δ⁻¹ the qubit is pinned near a measurement eigenstate; parametrize the pure-state Bloch vector by a single polar angle θ to collapse the six-dimensional phase space to (θ, p_θ).
#18 [computational] Write stochastic Hamiltonian for quantum jump dynamics complexity: 3/5 ← #17
Substitute the (θ, p_θ) parametrization to obtain H = p_θ(Δ − r sinθ/τ) − (r²−2r cosθ+1)/(2τ) as the reduced stochastic Hamiltonian governing rare quantum jumps.
#19 [computational] Derive 2 ODEs and constraint for quantum jump trajectory complexity: 4/5 ← #18
Extremize the reduced action to get θ̇ = Δ − r sinθ/τ, ṗ_θ = p_θ r cosθ/τ + r sinθ/τ, and the saddle-point condition r = cosθ − p_θ sinθ that determines the most-likely jump path.
#20 [computational] Estimate quantum jump timescale T ≈ 4τ ln(1/Δτ) complexity: 4/5 ← #19
Solve or estimate the transit time along the optimal jump trajectory between measurement eigenstates in the Zeno regime to obtain T ≈ 4τ ln(1/Δτ) as the characteristic jump duration.
#21 [verification] Verify Zeno ODEs recover known jump phenomenology complexity: 3/5 ← #19, #20
Check that the θ-space optimal paths and jump time are consistent with known quantum Zeno physics and that the constraint r = cosθ − p_θ sinθ reduces to r = cosθ on the measurement-eigenstate fixed points.
#2 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Hard (4) nodes: 8 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_4225f097-0cee-4e43-b5b9-6efbab4c3447 ▼
Problem
A uniformly thick, disk-shaped permanent magnet has a uniform magnetization within its body, with the direction of magnetization perpendicular to the top of the disk. (The "top" is one of the two flat surfaces of the disk, not a curved side.) The radius of the magnet is `\(2R\)`, and the thickness is `\( d \)`, with `\(d \ll R\)`. The magnetic field at the center of the magnet is `\(\vec{B}_{0}\)…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `\(\vec{B}_{1} = -\vec{B}_0\)` (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level magnetostatics: bound surface currents, equivalent solenoid model, and on-axis field integrals for finite solenoids.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solution requires chaining superposition, an equivalent-solenoid approximation, a thin-disk limiting case (d≪R), and a scaling comparison between two disk geometries.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious steps: (1) decompose the annular magnet via superposition (full = annular + small), (2) model each magnetized disk as a bound-surface-current solenoid, and (3) observe that the small-disk field equals 2B₀ from the d/a scaling, making B₁ = −B₀.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–8 meaningful steps: invoke superposition, map magnetization to surface currents, write on-axis solenoid field formula, apply d≪R limit, evaluate B for both radii, form the ratio, and combine.
interdependence
5/5 Each step is a prerequisite for the next — an error in the solenoid model or the thin-disk approximation propagates directly to an incorrect final field vector.
Justification: —
Step graph — 8 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #3, #6 The solution is dominated by computational steps after two critical conceptual pivots: recognizing the thin-disk bound-current model and applying superposition at the shared center point. An error in either conceptual node propagates through the entire chain.
#1 [strategic] Use superposition to decompose disk geometry complexity: 3/5
Frame the problem via linearity of magnetostatics: the original full disk (radius 2R) equals the annular remnant plus the small removed disk (radius R), so B_full = B_annular + B_small.
#2 [conceptual] Identify that hole center equals original disk center complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Recognize that the point where B_0 was measured (center of the 2R disk) is geometrically identical to the center of the hollowed region, so superposition is evaluated at the same spatial point.
#3 [conceptual] Model thin magnetized disk as rim current loop complexity: 4/5 ← #1
For d ≪ R, bound volume currents vanish and only the cylindrical side surface carries a bound surface current K_b = M × r̂, making each thin disk equivalent to a single circular current loop of radius a and current I = Md.
#4 [computational] Write superposition equation at the center point complexity: 1/5 ← #1, #2
Express the known field as B_0 = B_1 + B_small, where B_1 is the unknown field of the annular disk and B_small is the field contributed by the removed disk of radius R.
#5 [computational] Apply Biot–Savart for on-axis field of a current loop complexity: 2/5 ← #3
At the center of a circular loop of radius a carrying current I, the axial field is B = μ_0 I / (2a); combined with I = Md this gives B = μ_0 M d / (2a), establishing an inverse-radius dependence.
#6 [computational] Compute B_small relative to B_0 using radius ratio complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #5
Since B ∝ 1/a, the field from the radius-R disk is B_small = B_0 × (2R / R) = 2B_0, because the removed disk has half the radius of the full disk.
#7 [computational] Solve for B_1 via superposition equation complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Substitute B_small = 2B_0 into B_0 = B_1 + B_small and solve: B_1 = B_0 − 2B_0 = −B_0.
#8 [verification] Check sign and limiting-case consistency complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Confirm the negative sign is physical (the annular ring, which is farther from center, contributes a weaker field than what was removed, so the remainder field is reversed relative to B_0 at the interior point) and that the answer is independent of M, consistent with B_0 already encoding M.
#3 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Hard (4) nodes: 12 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_1e2f4cbe-4a38-4dd3-9378-14df40f7599f ▼
Problem
A thin-walled insulated container of mass `\(M\)` (ignoring the thickness of the container) floats in space (considered as a vacuum), initially at rest, filled with `\(N_0\)` molecules of nitrogen gas (`\( N_2 \)`), at an equilibrium temperature of `\(T_0\)`, and with a mass per molecule of nitrogen of `\(m\)`. In this case, we assume that `\(M\gg N_0m\)`. A small hole appears in the container, wh…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `\( E_{avg} = 3k_BT \)` (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level statistical mechanics: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, Knudsen effusion, and degrees of freedom for diatomic molecules.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Demands a non-trivial integral derivation: computing a flux-weighted energy average over the half-space Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, then adding internal DOF contributions.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three distinct insights needed: (1) escaping molecules are flux-weighted (not a random sample), giving translational KE of 2k_BT instead of 3k_BT/2; (2) correct evaluation of the flux-weighted integral; (3) adding rotational DOF (k_BT) for diatomic N2 to reach 3k_BT.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 6–8 distinct steps: frame the effusion flux integral, separate translational and rotational contributions, evaluate each integral, sum to final answer.
interdependence
4/5 Steps are tightly sequential — missing the flux-weighting insight at the start propagates a systematic error (wrong distribution) that corrupts every downstream calculation.
Justification: —
Step graph — 12 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #3, #4, #7, #10 The solution has two parallel conceptual threads — one developing the velocity-biased escape flux for translational energy, and one establishing that rotational energy is unaffected by that bias — that converge at the final summation. The dominant bottleneck is correctly modeling the v_x-proportional flux (node 3), since an error there corrupts the entire translational energy branch and thus the final answer.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as Knudsen effusion regime complexity: 4/5
Recognize that because the hole is much smaller than the mean free path, molecules escape independently without collisions at the orifice, and faster molecules strike (and escape through) the hole more frequently — so escaping molecules are velocity-biased.
#2 [conceptual] Identify N₂ internal degrees of freedom complexity: 2/5 ← #1
N₂ is a diatomic molecule with 3 translational and 2 rotational degrees of freedom (at moderate temperatures, vibrational modes are frozen out), giving total average energy (5/2)k_BT per molecule in equilibrium.
#3 [conceptual] Model escape flux proportional to normal velocity v_x complexity: 3/5 ← #1
The effusion flux through the hole depends on the number of molecules striking the hole per unit time, which for a molecule with velocity component v_x (perpendicular to the hole, v_x > 0) is proportional to v_x itself; this is the key velocity-selection bias.
#4 [conceptual] Set up flux-weighted energy expectation integral complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #3
Write the average energy of escaping molecules as E_avg = ∫ E_total · v_x · f(v) d³v / ∫ v_x · f(v) d³v (integrating over v_x > 0 only), then split translational and rotational contributions because the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution factorizes.
#5 [computational] Compute flux-weighted average of v_x² complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Evaluate ⟨v_x²⟩_escape = ∫₀^∞ v_x³ e^(−αv_x²) dv_x / ∫₀^∞ v_x e^(−αv_x²) dv_x with α = m/(2k_BT); numerator = 1/(2α²), denominator = 1/(2α), giving ⟨v_x²⟩_escape = 1/α = 2k_BT/m.
#6 [computational] Compute unweighted averages of v_y² and v_z² complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Since the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution factorizes and v_y, v_z are not selected by the escape criterion, their averages remain the equilibrium values: ⟨v_y²⟩ = ⟨v_z²⟩ = k_BT/m.
#7 [computational] Sum translational energy of escaping molecule complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6
Combine the three translational contributions: ⟨½mv_x²⟩_escape = k_BT, ⟨½mv_y²⟩ = ½k_BT, ⟨½mv_z²⟩ = ½k_BT, giving total average translational energy ⟨E_trans⟩_escape = 2k_BT.
#8 [conceptual] Recognize rotational DOF are uncorrelated with escape selection complexity: 3/5 ← #2, #3
The translational and rotational degrees of freedom are statistically independent in the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, so the velocity-bias applied at the hole does not alter the distribution of rotational energies among escaping molecules.
#9 [computational] Apply equipartition to rotational energy of escapees complexity: 1/5 ← #8
Because rotational energy is uncorrelated with escape selection, escaping N₂ molecules carry the same average rotational energy as bulk molecules: ⟨E_rot⟩_escape = 2 × (½k_BT) = k_BT.
#10 [computational] Add translational and rotational contributions complexity: 1/5 ← #7, #9
Sum the flux-weighted translational energy and the unbiased rotational energy: E_avg = 2k_BT + k_BT = 3k_BT.
#11 [verification] Check dimensional consistency of result complexity: 1/5 ← #10
Confirm that 3k_BT has units of energy (Joules) and that the numerical prefactor 3 is dimensionless, consistent with the expression being an energy per molecule.
#12 [verification] Sanity-check against monoatomic limiting case complexity: 2/5 ← #10
For a monoatomic ideal gas (0 rotational DOF), the same derivation yields E_avg = 2k_BT; adding the 2 rotational DOF of a diatomic gas gives +k_BT → 3k_BT, confirming the answer increases correctly with internal degrees of freedom.
#4 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Expert (5) nodes: 18 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_60ff38cc-fb55-4c3c-b75c-f59dde5de0c3 ▼
Problem
Context: IceCube neutrino scientists usually calculate confidence intervals on their results by constructing contours based on Wilks' Theorem. For time-integrated neutrino searches, the TS of the neutrino background generally follows Wilks' Theorem with two degrees of freedom. For time-dependent neutrino searches, the TS of the neutrino background generally follows Wilks' Theorem with four degrees…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: Correctly explains that the S/B threshold is defined as the event signal over the event background and is used to reduce computational time by not considering events under a specified S/B threshold. No points if solution does not correctly define the S/B thres…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires research-level familiarity with IceCube analysis pipelines, Feldman-Cousins unified intervals, Wilks' theorem boundary conditions, and maximum likelihood methods in high-energy astrophysics.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 Solver must chain together multiple non-trivial statistical arguments: boundary invalidation of Wilks', FC construction procedure, minimizer pathology, S/B thresholding, and sign of ΔTS under different injection regimes.
conceptual_leaps
5/5 At least four non-obvious insights are required: (1) boundary saturation invalidates Wilks', (2) the minimizer's tendency to prefer long flares drives positive ΔTS, (3) the role of the S/B threshold in pruning events, and (4) signal-injected trials are necessary (not background-only) to map the FC belt.
plan_length
4/5 The answer must address ~12 distinct sub-tasks: method selection, rationale, trial design (4-parameter grid), TS_best-fit construction, TS_truth construction, pseudo-trial ΔTS, true-data ΔTS, 90% contour extraction, sign analysis of ΔTS, minimizer description with equation, S/B threshold definition, and minimizer improvement.
interdependence
4/5 Understanding why FC is chosen underpins how trials must be constructed, which in turn dictates how ΔTS is built and how the contour is extracted — errors early in the chain propagate through all downstream steps.
Justification: —
Step graph — 18 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #3, #6, #7, #8 The solution is dominated by conceptual steps because the core challenge is understanding why FC applies and how ΔTS is constructed; the single strategic choice (node 1) to use FC is the root bottleneck from which all reasoning flows, with node 8 (ΔTS computation) being a second major bottleneck gating the contour and interpretive branches.
#1 [strategic] Choose Feldman-Cousins over Wilks' method complexity: 3/5
Decide that because the best-fit dt hits the physical boundary (max allowed 1960 days), Wilks' regularity conditions are violated and Feldman-Cousins (FC) is required.
#2 [conceptual] Identify why Wilks' Theorem fails here complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Recognize that Wilks' Theorem assumes the true parameter is in the interior of the allowed space; a best-fit at the boundary breaks this regularity condition, making the chi-squared TS distribution invalid.
#3 [conceptual] Frame FC as ordering-principle confidence construction complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Understand that FC constructs confidence intervals by comparing the true-data ΔTS to a ΔTS distribution built from pseudo-experiments injected at each true (ns, dt) point, guaranteeing coverage at physical boundaries.
#4 [conceptual] Identify signal trials as required pseudo-experiments complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Recognize that FC requires signal trials (scrambled data plus injected signal), not background-only trials, because we need ΔTS distributions conditional on specific true-parameter values across the 2D grid.
#5 [computational] Specify signal injection grid over all four parameters complexity: 4/5 ← #4
Define a grid of injection combinations spanning all four free parameters (γ, T0, dt, ns) at sufficiently fine resolution, since FC contours must be built at every (ns, dt) point tested and γ and T0 must also be varied to avoid bias.
#6 [conceptual] Define TS_best-fit via likelihood minimizer over all four params complexity: 3/5 ← #3
For both pseudo-experiments and true data, TS_best-fit is obtained by running the full four-parameter likelihood minimizer (γ, ns, T0, dt free) to find the global maximum likelihood, not by scanning.
#7 [conceptual] Define TS_truth via likelihood scan at injected values complexity: 3/5 ← #3
For pseudo-experiments, TS_truth is evaluated by fixing all four parameters to the injected truth values and reading off the likelihood scan (no re-fit), so that ΔTS measures the gain over the truth point.
#8 [computational] Compute ΔTS for each pseudo-trial at each grid point complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6, #7
For every signal trial at every (γ, T0, dt, ns) injection point, calculate ΔTS = TS_best-fit − TS_truth, accumulating a ΔTS distribution per grid cell.
#9 [computational] Compute true-data ΔTS at each (dt, ns) grid point complexity: 2/5 ← #6, #7
For the real dataset, evaluate ΔTS = TS_best-fit (from the minimizer) − TS_truth (from the likelihood scan fixed at that grid point's parameter values), separately for each (dt, ns) cell.
#10 [computational] Build 2D ΔTS histogram over (dt, ns) parameter space complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Assemble the pseudo-trial ΔTS distributions into a 2D histogram with x-axis dt, y-axis ns, and colorbar ΔTS, one distribution per grid cell, representing the FC ordering statistic landscape.
#11 [computational] Find 90% contour by comparing true ΔTS to trial distribution complexity: 3/5 ← #9, #10
At each (dt, ns) grid point, find the ΔTS value below which 90% of the pseudo-trial ΔTS values fall; the set of grid points where the true-data ΔTS is below this threshold defines the 90% FC confidence contour.
#12 [verification] Verify true ΔTS is compared to trial ΔTS distribution complexity: 2/5 ← #11
Confirm that the comparison is always between the true-data ΔTS and the pseudo-trial ΔTS distribution at the same grid point, not between raw TS values or other parameters.
#13 [conceptual] Explain positive ΔTS: minimizer favors long flares complexity: 4/5 ← #8
When the injected truth is at short dt but the global likelihood landscape has its minimum at large dt, the minimizer's TS_best-fit picks a long flare, exceeding TS_truth and yielding positive ΔTS.
#14 [conceptual] Explain negative ΔTS: truth near global minimum complexity: 3/5 ← #8
When the injected truth parameters are near the global likelihood minimum (large dt), TS_truth ≈ TS_best-fit or even lower, yielding ΔTS ≈ 0 or negative due to minimizer imprecision.
#15 [conceptual] Define S/B threshold and its computational role complexity: 3/5 ← #6
The S/B threshold is the ratio of per-event signal probability to background probability; events below the threshold are excluded from the likelihood sum to reduce computational time, since they contribute negligibly to the signal hypothesis.
#16 [computational] Give math equation for minimizer initial parameter seeding complexity: 3/5 ← #15
Express how the minimizer seeds initial guesses, e.g. using the highest-S/B events to estimate T0 and dt: T0_init = (Σ_i S_i·t_i)/(Σ_i S_i), so events with high signal-to-background weight the initial time estimate, focusing the minimizer near likely signal regions.
#17 [conceptual] Suggest improvement for minimizer global-minimum finding complexity: 3/5 ← #15, #16
Propose running the minimizer from multiple random initial seeds across the parameter space (multi-start optimization) to reduce the chance of converging to a local minimum rather than the global one, balancing accuracy against computational cost.
#18 [verification] Verify all four parameters included in TS constructions complexity: 2/5 ← #6, #7
Check that both TS_best-fit (minimizer) and TS_truth (scan) account for all four parameters (γ, ns, T0, dt) in both pseudo-experiments and true data, so no parameter is inadvertently held fixed and the FC construction is fully four-dimensional.
#5 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Expert (5) nodes: 14 | bottlenecks: 4 fs_f3ba1aae-2fc3-4d9b-a5a3-42bb91de4d7d ▼
Problem
Consider a uniform three-dimensional object of elliptical cross section with mass `\(m\)`, the semi-major axis and semi-minor axis of the cross-section, `\(A\)` and `\(B\)`, respectively. It is placed on a horizontal ground, with stable equilibrium established. A perturbation is applied to the object to make a small pure rolling oscillation on the ground (without slipping). Find the period of this…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `\( \omega = \pi\sqrt{\frac{\left(A^{2}+5B^{2}\right)B}{g\left(A^{2}-B^{2}\right)}}\)` (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level classical mechanics: moment of inertia for elliptical solids, rolling constraints, Lagrangian or energy methods for oscillation.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 The derivation demands first-principles construction of the effective potential for a rolling ellipse, identifying the contact point geometry, and linearizing about equilibrium — none of which follows a standard textbook recipe.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 At least three non-trivial insights: (1) computing the instantaneous radius of curvature at the contact point, (2) correctly accounting for the shift of the center of mass height as the ellipse rolls, (3) combining these into a restoring torque and applying the small-angle approximation.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: set up geometry, find center-of-mass height as function of roll angle, compute moment of inertia, write energy/Lagrangian, derive equation of motion, linearize, extract ω.
interdependence
5/5 Each step feeds directly into the next; an error in the contact-point curvature or the CM height expression propagates and corrupts the final frequency formula.
Justification: —
Step graph — 14 nodes | bottlenecks: #4, #6, #9, #11 The solution is dominated by computational steps but hinges on two non-trivial conceptual leaps — deriving the CM height change from rolling geometry (node 5) and recognising the instantaneous contact axis (node 7); errors at the curvature computation (node 4) or the parallel-axis result (node 9) corrupt all downstream results.
#1 [strategic] Use Lagrangian energy method for rolling oscillation complexity: 2/5
Frame the problem via conservation of energy / Lagrangian mechanics, recognising that for a no-slip rolling constraint the system has one degree of freedom (tilt angle φ).
#2 [conceptual] Identify stable equilibrium configuration complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Determine that stable equilibrium requires the semi-minor axis B to be vertical (contact at the tip of B), since the smaller curvature there produces a restoring force when A > B.
#3 [conceptual] Invoke curvature formula for ellipse at minor-axis tip complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recall that the radius of curvature of an ellipse x²/A² + y²/B² = 1 at the end of the semi-minor axis (y = −B) is ρ = A²/B, needed to describe rolling geometry.
#4 [computational] Compute ρ = A²/B at equilibrium contact point complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Substitute A and B into the curvature formula to obtain ρ = A²/B as the radius of curvature of the ellipse at the resting contact point.
#5 [conceptual] Model CM height change under small tilt φ complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #4
Using the rolling-on-flat-surface geometry, show that when the ellipse tilts by small angle φ the centre of mass rises by Δh ≈ (ρ − B)φ²/2 = (A² − B²)φ²/(2B), providing the restoring potential.
#6 [computational] Write potential energy V = mg(A²−B²)φ²/(2B) complexity: 2/5 ← #5
Multiply mass and gravity by the height change to get the quadratic potential V = mg(A²−B²)φ²/(2B), confirming it is positive (restoring) for A > B.
#7 [conceptual] Identify instantaneous rotation axis at contact point complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #2
Recognise that for pure rolling the body rotates instantaneously about the contact point, so the kinetic energy is (1/2)I_contact φ̇², where I_contact is the moment of inertia about that axis.
#8 [computational] Calculate I_cm = m(A²+B²)/4 for elliptic cylinder complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Apply the standard formula for a solid elliptic cylinder rotating about its central longitudinal axis: I_cm = m(A²+B²)/4.
#9 [computational] Apply parallel-axis theorem to get I_contact complexity: 2/5 ← #7, #8
Shift the axis from the CM to the contact point (distance B) via I_contact = I_cm + mB² = m(A²+B²)/4 + mB² = m(A²+5B²)/4.
#10 [conceptual] Assemble Lagrangian and derive SHO equation complexity: 3/5 ← #6, #9
Write L = T − V = (1/2)·m(A²+5B²)/4·φ̇² − mg(A²−B²)/(2B)·φ², then apply the Euler–Lagrange equation to obtain the simple harmonic oscillator equation for φ.
#11 [computational] Extract angular frequency ω_freq² complexity: 2/5 ← #10
Read off ω_freq² = (stiffness)/(inertia) = [mg(A²−B²)/B] / [m(A²+5B²)/4] = 4g(A²−B²) / [B(A²+5B²)].
#12 [computational] Compute period T = 2π/ω_freq complexity: 2/5 ← #11
Take the reciprocal square root and multiply by 2π: T = 2π/ω_freq = π√[B(A²+5B²) / (g(A²−B²))], which is the answer labelled ω in the problem.
#13 [verification] Check dimensional consistency of period formula complexity: 1/5 ← #12
Confirm that √[B(A²+5B²)/(g(A²−B²))] = √[m³/(m·s⁻²·m²)] = √[s²] = s, consistent with a period.
#14 [verification] Verify limiting cases (A→B and A≫B) complexity: 2/5 ← #12
Check that T→∞ as A→B (ellipse becomes a circle, no restoring force) and that T decreases with increasing A/B ratio, both physically expected behaviours.
#6 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Medium (3) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_1dba0086-bca8-4b25-8843-e6e428f9284d ▼
Problem
Assuming we conducted an experiment to determine a physical quantity \\(X\\). We measure the physical quantity \\(X\\), `\(N\)` times independently, with the results being `\(x_{1}, x_{2}, ..., x_{N}\)`, each random variable \\(X_i\\) follows a Gaussian distribution. An experiment is a sampling process, and our goal is to use `\(X_{1},X_{2},...,X_{N}\)` to find the best estimates for the paramete…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `\( \sqrt{\frac{s^2+\frac{a^2}{12}}{N}} \) ` (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid undergraduate-level knowledge of statistics: Gaussian distributions, unbiased estimators, variance of a uniform distribution, and error propagation.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 The solver must combine two independent variance sources (Type A and Type B), requiring several conceptual steps but following a tractable recipe once the framework is clear.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two moderate non-obvious steps: recognizing that Var(Uniform[-a/2, a/2]) = a²/12, and understanding that independent variances add so the combined uncertainty is sqrt((s²+a²/12)/N).
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 4–5 distinct steps: set up the shifted mean, compute Var(X_B), compute its contribution to the mean's variance, add the two variance terms, and take the square root.
interdependence
4/5 Each step feeds directly into the next — misidentifying Var(X_B) or the scaling by 1/N propagates an error all the way through to the final expression.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #4, #6 The solution is dominated by computational steps once the key modelling choice (independent X_Bi per measurement) is made; node 2 is the critical bottleneck since misreading X_B as a single shared offset yields a different and incorrect answer.
#1 [strategic] Frame as independent-error variance combination problem complexity: 3/5
Recognize that the total uncertainty of μ̂ requires combining Type A sampling variance with Type B instrument variance, using the law of total variance.
#2 [conceptual] Model each measurement as Xi + X_Bi (i.i.d. errors) complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Interpret the problem statement as each measurement having its own independent X_Bi ~ Uniform(-a/2, a/2), so the observed value is Xi + X_Bi.
#3 [conceptual] Express μ̂ as average of (Xi + X_Bi) complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Write μ̂ = (1/N)Σ(Xi + X_Bi) using the standard unbiased mean estimator applied to the augmented measurements.
#4 [conceptual] Apply variance propagation to μ̂ complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Use Var(μ̂) = (1/N²)Σ[Var(Xi) + Var(X_Bi)] by independence of Xi and X_Bi and mutual independence across i.
#5 [computational] Compute variance of Uniform(-a/2, a/2) complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Apply the standard result Var(Uniform(-a/2, a/2)) = a²/12 from the uniform distribution variance formula.
#6 [computational] Substitute Var(Xi) = s² and Var(X_Bi) = a²/12 complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #5
Replace σ² with its unbiased estimate s² and substitute a²/12 to get Var(μ̂) = (1/N²)[N·s² + N·a²/12].
#7 [computational] Simplify variance to (s² + a²/12) / N complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Cancel the factor of N from numerator and denominator: Var(μ̂) = (s² + a²/12)/N.
#8 [computational] Take square root for final uncertainty expression complexity: 1/5 ← #7
The uncertainty of μ̂ is the standard deviation, so compute √((s² + a²/12)/N) as the final answer.
#9 [verification] Check limiting case a → 0 recovers Type A result complexity: 1/5 ← #8
When a → 0 (no instrument error), the expression reduces to s/√N, confirming consistency with the given Type A uncertainty formula.
#7 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Hard (4) nodes: 12 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_27c865e6-1c87-489b-b7ea-b197fe3356ba ▼
Problem
Consider a dielectric block with polarization `\(P\) and internal energy \(U\) which obeys` `\[ U(T, P) = CT + f(P)\,,\]`where 𝑓(𝑃) is the polarization-dependent part of the internal energy. where `\(CT\)` is the internal energy of the unpolarized dielectric block, `\(C\)` is the heat capacity of the dielectric block, and `\(T\)` is the absolute temperature. For convenience, any possible volume …
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: \(U=CT\)' (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level thermodynamics: Carnot cycles, dielectric polarization, Curie law, and Maxwell relations.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must construct an infinitesimal Carnot cycle for a dielectric medium, apply the first law with non-standard work term, and derive a constraint on f(P) via thermodynamic identities.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-trivial insights required: (1) translating the Carnot cycle efficiency condition to a differential relation between E, T, and P; (2) using χ(T) = nα = nK/T to express E in terms of P and T; (3) recognizing that the resulting constraint forces f(P) = const, leaving U = CT.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 6–8 distinct steps: set up Carnot cycle, compute heat exchanges, equate efficiency, apply Maxwell-type relation, substitute Curie law, solve for f(P), write final U.
interdependence
5/5 Every step feeds directly into the next; an error in the Carnot cycle setup or the Maxwell relation propagates and invalidates the final expression for U.
Justification: —
Step graph — 12 nodes | bottlenecks: #5, #9, #10 The solution is dominated by conceptual and computational steps of moderate difficulty; the critical bottleneck is the Maxwell-relation identity (node 5) combined with the Curie-law evaluation (node 9), whose cancellation is the non-obvious core insight that collapses f(P) to zero.
#1 [strategic] Use Carnot cycle to derive thermodynamic identity complexity: 4/5
Choose the infinitesimal Carnot cycle approach to extract a constraint on (∂U/∂P)_T, which will determine f(P).
#2 [conceptual] Identify dielectric thermodynamic state variables complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that T and P (or p=PV) are the independent state variables and dU = TdS + Edp is the fundamental relation for the dielectric.
#3 [conceptual] Construct Helmholtz free energy for dielectric complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Define F = U − TS so that dF = −SdT + Edp, enabling derivation of Maxwell relations between entropy and electric field.
#4 [conceptual] Derive Maxwell relation (∂S/∂p)_T = −(∂E/∂T)_p complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Apply equality of mixed second partial derivatives of F with respect to T and p to obtain the Maxwell relation linking entropy and field changes.
#5 [computational] Express (∂U/∂p)_T via Maxwell relation complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Substitute (∂S/∂p)_T = −(∂E/∂T)_p into the first law to obtain (∂U/∂p)_T = E − T(∂E/∂T)_p.
#6 [conceptual] Apply Curie law to find susceptibility χ(T) complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Use α = K/T and the relation χ(T) = nα = nK/T to express the temperature-dependent susceptibility of the dielectric.
#7 [computational] Solve for E in terms of T and P using Curie law complexity: 2/5 ← #6
Invert P = χ(T)ε₀E = (nKε₀/T)E to get E = PT/(nKε₀), making E explicitly linear in both T and P.
#8 [computational] Compute ∂E/∂T at constant P complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Differentiate E = PT/(nKε₀) with respect to T holding P fixed, giving (∂E/∂T)_P = P/(nKε₀).
#9 [computational] Evaluate E − T(∂E/∂T)_P to find (∂U/∂P)_T complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #8
Substitute into the identity from step 5: E − T·(∂E/∂T)_P = PT/(nKε₀) − T·P/(nKε₀) = 0, so (∂U/∂P)_T = 0.
#10 [conceptual] Interpret zero derivative to determine f(P) complexity: 2/5 ← #9
Since (∂U/∂P)_T = f'(P) = 0 for all P, f(P) must be a constant; by convention f(0) = 0 so f(P) = 0 identically.
#11 [computational] State final result U = CT complexity: 1/5 ← #10
Substitute f(P) = 0 into U(T,P) = CT + f(P) to conclude U = CT, independent of polarization.
#12 [verification] Check P→0 limit and heat capacity consistency complexity: 1/5 ← #11
Confirm that U = CT recovers the correct unpolarized limit and that ∂U/∂T = C matches the given heat capacity, validating the result.
#8 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Expert (5) nodes: 0 | bottlenecks: 0 fs_d3acd2cb-8477-4630-b8bb-b4bc22670dc8 ▼
Problem
Context: For few-body system problems, a typical problem is to find the energy spectrum of a given quantum Hamiltonian. For example, let us consider the three-dimensional lattice Hamiltonian for a spin-1/2 non-relativistic nucleon. The Hamiltonian reads \\\[H = t\\sum\_{r,\\alpha, \\sigma} (2N^\\dagger\_{r,\\sigma}N\_{r,\\sigma} - N^\\dagger\_{r,\\sigma} N\_{r+e\_\\alpha,\\sigma} - N^\\dagger\_{…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: Correctly identify that there are three spin-singlet states since we are working with nucleons, which can either be a neutron or a proton Points: 0.5, Item: Find the value of a single particle energy based on given values of input parameter as\\\[E_0=\\frac{4\…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires graduate-to-research-level mastery of second quantization, nuclear lattice EFT, Fock space formalism, and few-body scattering theory.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 Demands full first-principles derivations: lattice dispersion relations, resolvent/pole conditions for bound states, spin-isospin algebra, and thermodynamic limit analysis.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Multiple non-obvious insights required: identifying all three spin-singlet channels (including isospin), reducing the two-body problem to a relative-coordinate single-body problem, and using the Green's function pole to extract the coupling constant.
plan_length
5/5 Five sub-parts each with several distinct steps (basis construction, dispersion matching, numerical evaluation, T-matrix pole calculation, excited-state degeneracy counting) — well over 21 meaningful actions.
interdependence
4/5 Parts build sequentially: t from (b) feeds (c), the spin-singlet basis from (a) feeds (d), and C from (d) is needed for (e); errors propagate strongly downstream.
Justification: —
Step graph — 0 nodes No task or solution was provided in the block; the graph is empty.
—
#9 FrontierScience-Olympiad physics Hard (4) nodes: 13 | bottlenecks: 2 fs_422512ca-5a0c-4a23-b987-7fa2eb2024e6 ▼
Problem
Suppose the Moon is 3 degrees above the horizon and we observe it over the ocean that has small ripples on its surface. We are located such that we can see the reflection of the moon in the ocean. Assume we are very far away from the ocean. Derive a value for the quantity `\( r \)` defined as a ratio of the perceived maximum vertical displacement over the perceived maximum horizontal displacement …
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: \\(r = 19\\) (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires solid command of 3D specular reflection geometry and vector optics beyond standard introductory physics.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Full derivation demands a multi-step mathematical chain: establish observer/moon geometry, perturb the surface normal, project reflected-ray deviations onto the observer's local vertical and horizontal axes, then form the ratio.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 At least three non-obvious insights are needed: (1) the observer's grazing angle must equal θ for the flat-surface baseline; (2) sagittal tilts produce a vertical shift of 2δα independent of θ; (3) transverse tilts produce a horizontal shift of 2 sin θ δα, making the ratio 1/sin θ.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: set up coordinates, define direction vectors, apply reflection law, perturb the normal, decompose the deviation into observer frame, extract max shifts, take ratio, evaluate numerically.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential — an error in the coordinate setup or the reflection perturbation calculation propagates irreversibly into the final ratio.
Justification: —
Step graph — 13 nodes | bottlenecks: #4, #10 The solution is dominated by computational steps once the key reflection formula (node 4) is established; the critical algebraic insight is the cancellation of the unknown ripple amplitude Φ in node 10, yielding the clean result r = 1/θ ≈ 19.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as ray-reflection shape analysis complexity: 3/5
Decide to model the spread of the Moon's reflection as determined by which tilted surface elements can specularly reflect moonlight toward the observer, using the small-ripple limit.
#2 [conceptual] Model ripples as locally flat tilted facets complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Treat the rippled ocean surface as a collection of locally flat planar elements each characterized by two independent tilt angles (φ_x in the vertical plane, φ_y perpendicular), bounded by maximum slope Φ.
#3 [conceptual] Establish coordinate system and ray directions complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Define x as horizontal toward the observer, z as vertical; Moon direction from surface is û_M ≈ (1, 0, θ) and observer direction is û_O ≈ (1, 0, 0) under the small-angle, far-field approximation.
#4 [conceptual] Apply law of reflection to tilted surface element complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #3
For a surface element with normal n̂ ≈ (φ_x, φ_y, 1), compute the reflected ray û_r = û_M − 2(û_M · n̂)n̂ and read off its elevation and azimuth components to first order in the tilt angles.
#5 [computational] Derive apparent elevation α of reflected image complexity: 3/5 ← #4
From the z-component of û_r, extract α = θ + 2φ_x, showing that only vertical tilts φ_x shift the apparent elevation of the reflection.
#6 [computational] Derive apparent azimuth β of reflected image complexity: 3/5 ← #4
From the y-component of û_r, extract β = −2φ_y(φ_x + θ), showing that the horizontal spread depends on both φ_y and the sum (φ_x + θ).
#7 [conceptual] Apply small-ripple approximation φ_x << θ complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6
Since ripples are small (Φ << θ), approximate φ_x + θ ≈ θ in the expression for β, decoupling the horizontal spread from φ_x.
#8 [computational] Compute maximum vertical extent of reflection complexity: 2/5 ← #5
With φ_x ranging over [−Φ, Φ], the elevation α spans [θ − 2Φ, θ + 2Φ], giving total vertical angular extent Δα = 4Φ.
#9 [computational] Compute maximum horizontal extent of reflection complexity: 2/5 ← #6, #7
With φ_y ∈ [−Φ, Φ] and using β ≈ −2φ_y θ, the azimuth spans [−2Φθ, 2Φθ], giving total horizontal angular extent Δβ = 4Φθ.
#10 [computational] Form ratio r = Δα / Δβ and cancel Φ complexity: 3/5 ← #8, #9
Compute r = 4Φ / (4Φθ) = 1/θ; the maximum slope Φ cancels, leaving the ratio dependent only on the Moon's elevation angle θ.
#11 [computational] Convert θ = 3° to radians and evaluate r complexity: 1/5 ← #10
θ = 3° × π/180 = π/60 rad ≈ 0.05236 rad, so r = 1/θ = 60/π ≈ 19.099.
#12 [computational] Round result to nearest whole number complexity: 1/5 ← #11
Round 19.099 to r = 19 as the problem requests.
#13 [verification] Check limiting-case behavior of r = 1/θ complexity: 2/5 ← #10
Verify plausibility: as θ → 0° (Moon at horizon), r → ∞ (reflection infinitely elongated vertically, consistent with glitter-path intuition); as θ → 90° (Moon overhead), r → 1 (circular reflection shape, consistent with symmetry).
#10 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 13 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_ecb58594-7fd9-4a94-b799-0f1c13acb752 ▼
Problem
At high temperatures, element A reacts with water to make a binary compound B. Compound B reacts with a mixture of concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and bromine (Br2) to yield a ternary compound C. Compound C is a strong oxidiser, contains two metal elements in 1:1 ratio and does not contain any water of crystallisation. Compound C contains 74.6% of element A by mass. The relative formu…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Sodium bismuthate (NaBiO3) (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires advanced inorganic chemistry knowledge: bismuth's unusual oxidation states (Bi³⁺→Bi⁵⁺), its high-temperature water reaction forming Bi₂O₃, and the specific oxidizing action of Br₂ in alkaline solution.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must integrate multiple simultaneous constraints (binary/ternary classification, 1:1 metal ratio, mass percentage, formula mass threshold, strong oxidizer property) into a single consistent identification.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious insights required: (1) recognizing Na from NaOH becomes one of the two metals in C, (2) using the 74.6% mass fraction to back-calculate element A's atomic mass (~209 → Bi), and (3) knowing Bi can be oxidized to the +5 state under these conditions.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–8 steps: classify C's formula type, assign Na as one metal, set up and solve the mass percentage equation, identify Bi, verify formula mass >230, verify binary compound B (Bi₂O₃), confirm reaction pathway, write final answer.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: misidentifying Na as a component, or failing to solve the atomic-mass equation correctly, invalidates every subsequent verification step.
Justification: —
Step graph — 13 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #4, #5, #6, #7 The solution is a linear identification pipeline — a chain of constraint-narrowing steps (formula assignment → mass equation → element lookup) feeding a parallel verification fan-out — with the algebraic mass-percentage computation (nodes 5–7) acting as the decisive bottleneck that converts all qualitative clues into a unique answer.
#1 [strategic] Frame as multi-constraint element identification complexity: 2/5
Recognise that the problem chains reaction clues (binary product, ternary oxidiser, two-metal 1:1 ratio, mass %, formula mass) into a system that uniquely identifies element A and compound C.
#2 [conceptual] Identify both metals in C as A and Na complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Since C is ternary with exactly two metal elements in 1:1 ratio and the only metal source in the reaction medium is NaOH, the two metals must be Na (from NaOH) and element A.
#3 [conceptual] Assign general formula NaAOₓ to compound C complexity: 2/5 ← #2
With Na and A in 1:1 ratio, no water of crystallisation, and oxygen as the remaining element in a ternary compound, C must have the formula NaAOₓ where x is determined by oxidation state.
#4 [conceptual] Infer high oxidation state from strong-oxidiser clue complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Strong oxidisers of the form NaAOₓ typically have A in a high, unusual oxidation state; combined with Br₂ as the oxidising agent in synthesis, this points to x = 3 (i.e., A in the +5 state, giving NaAO₃).
#5 [computational] Set up mass-percentage equation for element A complexity: 2/5 ← #3, #4
Express the 74.6 % mass constraint as Mₐ / (23 + Mₐ + 16x) = 0.746, substituting x = 3 to get Mₐ / (71 + Mₐ) = 0.746.
#6 [computational] Solve equation for molar mass of A complexity: 2/5 ← #5
Rearranging Mₐ = 0.746(71 + Mₐ) gives 0.254 Mₐ = 52.966, so Mₐ ≈ 208.5 g mol⁻¹.
#7 [conceptual] Identify element A as bismuth (Bi, M = 209) complexity: 2/5 ← #6
The only stable element with a standard atomic mass of ~209 g mol⁻¹ is bismuth (Bi, Z = 83), uniquely matching the computed value.
#8 [computational] Calculate formula mass of NaBiO₃ complexity: 1/5 ← #7
M(NaBiO₃) = 22.99 + 208.98 + 3 × 16.00 = 279.97 ≈ 280 g mol⁻¹.
#9 [verification] Verify formula mass exceeds 230 complexity: 1/5 ← #8
280 > 230, so the formula-mass constraint is satisfied; this rules out lighter analogues such as NaSbO₃ (M ≈ 193).
#10 [verification] Back-check mass percentage of Bi in NaBiO₃ complexity: 1/5 ← #8
208.98 / 279.97 = 74.6 %, confirming the mass-percentage constraint is exactly met.
#11 [verification] Confirm Bi reacts with steam to give binary compound complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Bismuth reacts with water vapour at high temperatures to form the binary oxide Bi₂O₃, consistent with the description of compound B as a binary compound of element A.
#12 [verification] Confirm NaBiO₃ is a known strong oxidiser complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Sodium bismuthate is a well-established strong oxidising agent (used, e.g., to oxidise Mn²⁺ to permanganate), confirming all qualitative clues are satisfied.
#13 [computational] State compound C as sodium bismuthate NaBiO₃ complexity: 1/5 ← #9, #10, #11, #12
All constraints (two metals 1:1, ternary, no water of crystallisation, 74.6 % Bi, M > 230, strong oxidiser) are satisfied simultaneously only by NaBiO₃.
#11 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Medium (3) nodes: 24 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_1875e781-bd89-4528-8bc3-94b7b436402f ▼
Problem
Context: Specific activity and yield of proteins are crucial in determining the purification scheme of an enzyme Question: Fill in the missing numbers in the missing purification scheme. Note that each step has 5 columns (Volume; Protein concentration; Activity; Specific activity; Yield) in the order listed. Define the required formulas to determine the missing numbers. Step 1 1450; A; 9; 0.8; B…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 0.5, Item: Recognizes that starting yield is always the maximum hence B = 100.0 Points: 0.5, Item: The answer correctly calculate A = 11.3 Points: 1.0, Item: The answer correctly calculate C = 7.9 Points: 0.5, Item: The answer correctly calculate D = 1.0 Points: 0.5, It…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with enzyme purification concepts (specific activity, yield, total vs. concentration-based activity) at an intermediate undergraduate biochemistry level.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must identify and apply 4 interrelated formulas systematically across 6 steps, with both forward and back-calculation required.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 The primary non-obvious insight is distinguishing 'Activity' (per-volume concentration) from Total Activity (Volume × Activity), which anchors all yield calculations; the rest follows procedurally.
plan_length
4/5 Solving all 12 unknowns across 6 steps involves roughly 15–18 distinct arithmetic or formula-application actions.
interdependence
4/5 Total Activity at Step 1 (1450 × 9 = 13050) is a single shared anchor that all yield calculations depend on; an error there propagates to C, F, G, I, L.
Justification: —
Step graph — 24 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #8 The solution is dominated by low-complexity atomic computations; the single critical bottleneck is node 8 (Total Activity Step 1 = 13050), which feeds every yield calculation and thus all volume/activity back-computations in steps 2, 4, 5, and 6. Errors in formula choice (nodes 2–4) or in the Step 1 total activity propagate to the majority of downstream answers.
#1 [strategic] Map table columns and identify all unknowns complexity: 2/5
Recognize the five-column structure (Volume, Protein concentration, Activity, Specific activity, Yield) and catalog every lettered unknown across all six steps before attempting any calculation.
#2 [conceptual] Define Specific Activity formula complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Establish that Specific Activity = Activity (units/mL) ÷ Protein concentration (mg/mL), giving units/mg; this is distinct from activity per se.
#3 [conceptual] Define Total Activity formula complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Establish that Total Activity at any step = Volume × Activity (units/mL), yielding total enzyme units; this is the quantity that decreases with purification losses.
#4 [conceptual] Define Yield percentage formula complexity: 2/5 ← #1, #3
Establish that Yield (%) = (Total Activity at current step ÷ Total Activity at Step 1) × 100, making Step 1 the reference denominator for all subsequent yields.
#5 [conceptual] Recognize Step 1 yield is always 100% complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Because Yield is defined relative to Step 1's total activity, the starting step's yield is by definition 100%, so B = 100.0 without calculation.
#6 [computational] Compute A: protein concentration in Step 1 complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Rearrange Specific Activity = Activity / Protein concentration to get A = Activity / Specific Activity = 9 / 0.8 = 11.25 ≈ 11.3 mg/mL.
#7 [computational] Set B = 100.0 for Step 1 yield complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Apply the baseline rule: B = 100.0%.
#8 [computational] Compute Total Activity for Step 1 complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Total Activity₁ = Volume × Activity = 1450 × 9 = 13050 units; this value is the universal denominator for all yield calculations in later steps.
#9 [computational] Compute C: activity per mL in Step 2 complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #8
Rearrange Yield formula: Total Activity₂ = (87.5/100) × 13050 = 11418.75; then C = 11418.75 / 1450 = 7.875 ≈ 7.9 units/mL.
#10 [computational] Compute D: specific activity in Step 2 complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #9
D = C / Protein concentration₂ = 7.875 / 8 = 0.984 ≈ 1.0 units/mg.
#11 [computational] Compute E: protein concentration in Step 3 complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Rearrange Specific Activity: E = Activity / Specific Activity = 30 / 11 = 2.727 ≈ 2.7 mg/mL.
#12 [computational] Compute Total Activity for Step 3 complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Total Activity₃ = 280 × 30 = 8400 units; needed to derive yield F.
#13 [computational] Compute F: yield for Step 3 complexity: 1/5 ← #4, #8, #12
F = (8400 / 13050) × 100 = 64.37 ≈ 64.4%.
#14 [computational] Compute Total Activity for Step 4 from yield complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #8
Total Activity₄ = (37/100) × 13050 = 4828.5 units; the yield is given so total activity is derived, then used to find volume.
#15 [computational] Compute G: volume in Step 4 complexity: 1/5 ← #3, #14
G = Total Activity₄ / Activity₄ = 4828.5 / 900 = 5.365 ≈ 5.4 mL.
#16 [computational] Compute H: protein concentration in Step 4 complexity: 1/5 ← #2
H = Activity / Specific Activity = 900 / 150 = 6.0 mg/mL.
#17 [computational] Compute Total Activity for Step 5 from yield complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #8
Total Activity₅ = (32/100) × 13050 = 4176 units; yield is given, so back-calculate total activity.
#18 [computational] Compute I: activity per mL in Step 5 complexity: 1/5 ← #3, #17
I = Total Activity₅ / Volume₅ = 4176 / 10 = 417.6 units/mL.
#19 [computational] Compute J: specific activity in Step 5 complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #18
J = I / Protein concentration₅ = 417.6 / 2 = 208.8 units/mg.
#20 [computational] Compute K: protein concentration in Step 6 complexity: 1/5 ← #2
K = Activity / Specific Activity = 700 / 500 = 1.4 mg/mL.
#21 [computational] Compute Total Activity for Step 6 complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Total Activity₆ = Volume₆ × Activity₆ = 5 × 700 = 3500 units.
#22 [computational] Compute L: yield for Step 6 complexity: 1/5 ← #4, #8, #21
L = (3500 / 13050) × 100 = 26.82 ≈ 26.8%.
#23 [verification] Verify yields decrease monotonically across steps complexity: 1/5 ← #7, #9, #13, #14, #17, #22
Confirm 100 > 87.5 > 64.4 > 37 > 32 > 26.8 — a valid purification must lose total activity at each step, so yields must be non-increasing.
#24 [verification] Verify specific activity increases across steps complexity: 2/5 ← #6, #10, #11, #15, #16, #18, #19, #20
Confirm 0.8 < 1.0 < 11 < 150 < 208.8 < 500 — specific activity must rise with each purification step as contaminant protein is removed relative to enzyme activity.
#12 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 18 | bottlenecks: 8 fs_7c94f50a-0896-450f-8d55-00c98aca5158 ▼
Problem
Context: Genexol-PM is a polymeric micellar system of paclitaxel. The original procedure states "Briefly, paclitaxel (30 mg) and mPEG-PDLLA (150 mg) were dissolved in 2.0 mL of acetonitrile. After 5 min of stirring, the organic solvent was evaporated on a rotary evaporator under reduced pressure at 60 °C to obtain a transparent gel matrix. The resulting transparent gel matrix was dissolved by the …
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: Correctly answers that there is no consequence of adding 5% sucrose instead of water Points: 1.0, Item: Correctly answers that there is no consequence of adding saline instead of water Points: 1.0, Item: Correctly answers that there is no consequence when redu…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level knowledge of polymer micelle formulation, colloidal stability, lyophilization, and DLS characterization.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Each sub-question demands applying multiple domain concepts (solubility kinetics, electrolyte-micelle interactions, cryoprotection, DLS peak interpretation) without a single prescribed path.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Several non-obvious insights are needed: that both components dissolve in acetonitrile in <60 s (so 1 min ≈ 5 min), that saline and 5% sucrose do not destabilize these particular micelles, and that the DLS intensity plot shows a bimodal distribution (~25 nm + ~1000 nm) prior to filtration.
plan_length
3/5 Five distinct sub-questions each requiring independent analysis yields roughly 8–12 meaningful reasoning/writing steps in total.
interdependence
1/5 The five sub-questions are largely independent; an error in one does not propagate to the others.
Justification: —
Step graph — 18 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #2, #3, #4, #5, #6, #7, #11 The solution is a parallel fan-out from two strategic framing nodes into five independent conceptual branches, each terminating in a computational conclusion and a verification check; the DLS branch has one extra depth level because peak count must precede volume assignment. Conceptual nodes are the dominant bottlenecks because errors in domain knowledge about solubility, micelle stability, or DLS physics cascade directly into wrong final answers.
#1 [strategic] Frame task as multi-part micelle formulation analysis complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the problem requires independent analysis of process parameter changes, colloidal characterization, and protocol completeness for a polymeric micelle drug delivery system.
#2 [strategic] Decompose into five independent sub-questions complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify the five answerable sub-questions—stirring time, saline substitution, sucrose substitution, DLS peak count/volumes, and missing protocol steps—each requiring a distinct knowledge domain.
#3 [conceptual] Assess solubility of drug and polymer in acetonitrile complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Establish that both paclitaxel and mPEG-PDLLA are highly soluble in acetonitrile and dissolve nearly instantaneously, so dissolution is complete well within 60 seconds.
#4 [conceptual] Identify principles of micelle stability under ionic conditions complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recall that high salt can destabilize micelles via screening of electrostatic interactions, but also that PEG-based non-ionic micelles are largely insensitive to physiological-level ionic strength such as saline.
#5 [conceptual] Identify principles of micelle stability under osmotic/sugar conditions complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recall that sugars can in principle alter micelle hydration and stability but that low concentrations like 5% sucrose do not measurably perturb PEG-shell micelles, which are stabilized sterically rather than electrostatically.
#6 [conceptual] Understand DLS intensity-weighted size distribution for micelle suspensions complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that DLS intensity weighting strongly favors large scatterers (I ∝ r⁶), so even a small population of aggregates or dust particles above ~100 nm will appear as a distinct peak alongside the main micelle population.
#7 [conceptual] Identify elements required for a complete lyophilization micelle protocol complexity: 4/5 ← #2
Draw on pharmaceutical formulation knowledge to enumerate the critical parameters a reproducible protocol must specify: solvent removal extent/duration, reconstitution volume, reconstitution duration, solution temperature at filtration, and lyoprotectant identity and timing.
#8 [computational] Conclude no consequence from 1-min stirring reduction complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Because paclitaxel and mPEG-PDLLA dissolve in acetonitrile in under 60 seconds given the specified masses (30 mg + 150 mg in 2 mL), reducing stirring from 5 min to 1 min does not affect dissolution completeness.
#9 [computational] Conclude no consequence from saline substitution complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Although elevated ionic strength can destabilize charge-stabilized micelles, PEG-PDLLA micelles rely on steric stabilization and the ionic strength of physiological saline is insufficient to disrupt them in this protocol.
#10 [computational] Conclude no consequence from 5% sucrose substitution complexity: 3/5 ← #5
5% sucrose does not destabilize PEG-shell micelles because the steric PEG corona is unaffected by the mild osmolyte at this concentration, leaving micelle size and drug loading unchanged.
#11 [computational] Determine two DLS peaks are present prior to filtration complexity: 3/5 ← #6
Before 0.22 µm filtration, the unfiltered Genexol-PM suspension contains both intact micelles and a small population of large aggregates/dust; DLS intensity weighting resolves these as exactly two distinct peaks.
#12 [computational] Assign hydrodynamic sizes: ~25 nm main peak, ~1000 nm minor peak complexity: 3/5 ← #6, #11
The dominant peak at ~25 nm corresponds to the core–shell mPEG-PDLLA/paclitaxel micelle; the minor peak at ~1000 nm corresponds to large aggregates or particulate contaminants removed by subsequent filtration.
#13 [computational] Enumerate all missing protocol steps complexity: 4/5 ← #7
Compare the published procedure against the required-element checklist to identify five omissions: (1) no lyoprotectant specified, (2) no duration or endpoint for acetonitrile evaporation, (3) no volume of water to add, (4) no duration for water reconstitution, (5) no instruction on whether to cool to room temperature before filtration.
#14 [verification] Cross-check stirring conclusion against dissolution kinetics literature complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Confirm that reported dissolution rates of paclitaxel and PEG-block copolymers in acetonitrile support complete solvation within seconds, validating the no-consequence conclusion.
#15 [verification] Cross-check saline conclusion against micelle stability data complexity: 2/5 ← #9
Verify against published stability studies that Genexol-PM or analogous PEG-PDLLA micelles maintain size and polydispersity in normal saline, confirming no adverse effect.
#16 [verification] Cross-check sucrose conclusion against cryoprotectant literature complexity: 2/5 ← #10
Confirm that 5% sucrose is commonly used as a lyoprotectant with PEG-based micelles without destabilizing them, consistent with the no-consequence conclusion.
#17 [verification] Verify DLS peak assignments against Genexol-PM characterization data complexity: 3/5 ← #11, #12
Cross-reference the ~25 nm and ~1000 nm peak positions with published DLS characterization of Genexol-PM (Kim et al. and related sources) to confirm consistency.
#18 [verification] Confirm missing steps list is complete and non-redundant complexity: 3/5 ← #13
Re-read the published protocol sentence by sentence to ensure no additional ambiguities were missed and that each identified omission is genuinely absent rather than implied.
#13 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_b75893ef-5c56-4d43-9ae1-8c9caaf32ee2 ▼
Problem
The molar mass of fully saturated unknown molecule X containing no oxygen atoms is 129 g/mol. Furthermore, its C-13 NMR spectrum indicates that there are 4 unique carbons, and its 1H-NMR spectrum in a deuterated protic solvent indicates 4 unique hydrogens. Identify molecule X. Think step by step and solve the problem below. At the end of your response, write your final answer on a new line starti…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Molecule X is <INCHI>InChI=1S/C8H19N/c1-3-5-7-9-8-6-4-2/h9H,3-8H2,1-2H3</INCHI>, <SMILES>CCCCNCCCC</SMILES>, <IUPAC>N-butylbutan-1-amine</IUPAC> (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure a…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level organic chemistry: degree of unsaturation, nitrogen rule, NMR symmetry analysis, and solvent effects on exchangeable protons.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must chain: odd MW → nitrogen rule → molecular formula → symmetry argument → NMR cross-validation, each step gating the next.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious insights: (1) odd MW implies nitrogen, (2) the symmetric dibutylamine structure collapses 8 carbons to 4 unique, (3) NH proton vanishes in protic deuterated solvent leaving exactly 4 1H signals.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 4–6 discrete steps: MW parity check, formula derivation, unsaturation degree, symmetry reasoning, NMR validation.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: an error in the MW/formula step propagates through every downstream constraint check, making recovery impossible without backtracking.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #5, #6 The solution is a sequential constraint-narrowing pipeline: molecular formula derivation (node 3) and the two NMR interpretations (nodes 5 and 6) are parallel bottlenecks that all feed the enumeration step, making errors at those nodes fatal to the correct identification.
#1 [strategic] Catalog all constraints from the problem complexity: 1/5
List every piece of data: MW=129 g/mol, no oxygen, fully saturated (zero degrees of unsaturation), 4 unique ¹³C signals, 4 unique ¹H signals in deuterated protic solvent.
#2 [conceptual] Identify candidate heteroatoms given no oxygen complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Since oxygen is excluded and molecule is organic, candidate heteroatoms are N, S, or halogens; nitrogen is the most common in stable, saturated organic molecules of this MW range.
#3 [computational] Derive molecular formula from MW 129 complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Test formula C₈H₁₉N: 8×12 + 19×1 + 14 = 96 + 19 + 14 = 129 g/mol, confirming a single nitrogen atom with eight carbons and nineteen hydrogens.
#4 [computational] Verify degree of unsaturation equals zero complexity: 2/5 ← #3
For C₈H₁₉N, DoU = (2×8 + 2 − 19 + 1)/2 = 0, consistent with the 'fully saturated' constraint and ruling out rings or double bonds.
#5 [conceptual] Interpret 4 unique ¹³C signals as molecular symmetry complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #4
A molecule with 8 carbons showing only 4 unique ¹³C peaks must have a C₂ symmetry element (or mirror plane) that makes each half of the molecule equivalent.
#6 [conceptual] Account for NH exchange in protic deuterated solvent complexity: 3/5 ← #1
In a deuterated protic solvent (e.g., D₂O or MeOD), the N–H proton undergoes H/D exchange and disappears from the ¹H-NMR spectrum, so the 4 unique ¹H signals arise only from C–H environments.
#7 [computational] Enumerate symmetric C₈H₁₉N isomers with one nitrogen complexity: 4/5 ← #4, #5, #6
Systematic enumeration of acyclic, saturated C₈H₁₉N structures with C₂ symmetry narrows to secondary amines of the form R₂NH where both R groups are identical n-butyl or branched C₄ chains.
#8 [computational] Confirm dibutylamine gives exactly 4 unique C and 4 unique H complexity: 3/5 ← #7
In CCCCNCCCC (di-n-butylamine), the two n-butyl chains are equivalent; within each chain the four CH₂/CH₃ groups are all distinct, yielding exactly 4 unique ¹³C signals and 4 unique C–H ¹H environments after NH exchange.
#9 [verification] Cross-check all constraints against dibutylamine complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Verify MW=129 ✓, no oxygen ✓, DoU=0 ✓, 4 unique ¹³C ✓, 4 unique C–H ¹H in protic solvent ✓, confirming N-butylbutan-1-amine as the unique solution.
#14 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Medium (3) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_a74ccc5b-fd37-49fa-b784-871dbcc41e9e ▼
Problem
How many distinct constitutional isomers exist for the chemical formula C4H6? Think step by step and solve the problem below. At the end of your response, write your final answer on a new line starting with “FINAL ANSWER”. It should be an answer to the question such as providing a number, mathematical expression, formula, or entity name, without any extra commentary or providing multiple answer a…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: 9 (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid understanding of organic chemistry: degrees of unsaturation, ring structures, alkenes, alkynes, and isomer classification rules.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must systematically enumerate all structural arrangements (chains, branches, rings, double bonds, triple bonds) without double-counting or omission.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight is recognizing that C4H6 has two degrees of unsaturation, which can be distributed as two double bonds, one triple bond, or one ring plus one double bond.
plan_length
3/5 Requires ~6–9 discrete steps: compute degrees of unsaturation, enumerate open-chain alkynes, open-chain dienes (conjugated/isolated/cumulated), cyclic structures with a double bond, and verify distinctness of each.
interdependence
3/5 The enumeration strategy (organized by unsaturation class) creates moderate coupling — missing a structural class early leads to an incomplete final count.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3, #6 The solution is dominated by computational enumeration steps that fan out from a single conceptual partitioning node (DoU category split); node 6 is the hardest bottleneck because monocyclic isomers are the most numerous and easiest to miss.
#1 [strategic] Plan systematic enumeration by structural class complexity: 2/5
Decide to enumerate constitutional isomers by grouping candidates according to their degree of unsaturation breakdown (double bonds, triple bond, rings), ensuring completeness.
#2 [computational] Calculate degree of unsaturation for C4H6 complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Apply DoU = (2C + 2 - H) / 2 = (8 + 2 - 6) / 2 = 2, establishing that every valid isomer must carry exactly 2 degrees of unsaturation.
#3 [conceptual] Map DoU=2 to exhaustive structural categories complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Identify all ways DoU=2 can be realized: (a) two C=C double bonds, (b) one C≡C triple bond, (c) one C=C double bond + one ring, and (d) two rings — forming the partition that drives the rest of the enumeration.
#4 [computational] Enumerate acyclic dienes (two C=C bonds) complexity: 3/5 ← #3
List all acyclic C4H6 skeletons bearing two double bonds: buta-1,2-diene (cumulated, CH2=C=CH–CH3) and buta-1,3-diene (conjugated, CH2=CH–CH=CH2) — yielding 2 isomers.
#5 [computational] Enumerate acyclic alkynes (one C≡C bond) complexity: 2/5 ← #3
List all acyclic C4H6 structures with a triple bond: but-1-yne (HC≡C–CH2–CH3) and but-2-yne (CH3–C≡C–CH3) — yielding 2 isomers.
#6 [computational] Enumerate monocyclic structures with one double bond complexity: 4/5 ← #3
Systematically build all C4H6 monocyclic structures (ring + one C=C): cyclobutene (4-membered ring), 1-methylcycloprop-1-ene (methyl on sp2 carbon), 3-methylcycloprop-1-ene (methyl on sp3 carbon), and methylenecyclopropane (exocyclic double bond on cyclopropane) — yielding 4 isomers.
#7 [computational] Enumerate bicyclic structures (two rings) complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Identify C4H6 bicyclic frameworks: bicyclo[1.1.0]butane (two fused cyclopropane rings sharing an edge) is the only possibility — yielding 1 isomer.
#8 [verification] Check for duplicates and missed structures complexity: 3/5 ← #4, #5, #6, #7
Verify that no structure appears in more than one category, that all ring sizes and branching patterns have been considered, and that each candidate satisfies C4H6 with DoU=2.
#9 [computational] Sum isomers across all categories complexity: 1/5 ← #8
Add the counts from each structural class: 2 (dienes) + 2 (alkynes) + 4 (monocyclic with C=C) + 1 (bicyclic) = 9 distinct constitutional isomers.
#15 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 11 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_5ef8b29d-7665-45e6-a26a-bd4a27f450ad ▼
Problem
Unknown molecule X reacts with <INCHI>InChI=1S/C7H8O3S/c1-6-2-4-7(5-3-6)11(8,9)10/h2-5H,1H3,(H,8,9,10)</INCHI>, <SMILES>CC1=CC=C(C=C1)S(=O)(=O)O</SMILES>, <IUPAC>4-methylbenzenesulfonic acid</IUPAC> to produce <INCHI>InChI=1S/C8H14O2/c1-7-4-3-5-8(2,10-7)9-6-7/h3-6H2,1-2H3/t7-,8+/m0/s1</INCHI>, <SMILES>C\[C@@\]12CCC\[C@@\](O1)(OC2)C</SMILES&g…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Molecule X is <INCHI>InChI=1S/C10H22O4/c1-9(12,8-11)6-5-7-10(2,13-3)14-4/h11-12H,5-8H2,1-4H3</INCHI>, <SMILES>CC(CCCC(C)(OC)OC)(CO)O</SMILES>, <IUPAC>6,6-dimethoxy-2-methylheptane-1,2-diol</IUPAC>. (Full derivation not provided; annotator sh…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level organic chemistry: acetal chemistry, stereochemistry, retrosynthetic analysis of bicyclic systems, and acid-catalyzed cyclization mechanisms.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must work retrosynthetically from a bicyclic acetal product, identify the bond-forming/breaking events under acid catalysis, and reconstruct a linear precursor that satisfies all constraints (formula, functional groups, stereochemistry).
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious insights are needed: recognizing TsOH drives intramolecular acetal exchange (not simple formation), mentally 'unzipping' the bicyclic ring system to a linear diol-acetal, and correctly assigning connectivity to match C10H22O4.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 steps: decode product structure, identify acid catalyst role, perform retrosynthetic ring-opening of each C–O bond, enumerate plausible linear precursors, check molecular formula, confirm acetal motif, verify stereochemical outcome.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: an error in reading the product's bicyclic connectivity propagates through every downstream retrosynthetic step, making the final answer unrecoverable without backtracking to the start.
Justification: —
Step graph — 11 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #5, #7 The solution is dominated by retrosynthetic reasoning: decoding the bicyclic product structure (node 3) and identifying the transacetalization mechanism (node 5) are the two critical bottlenecks from which all downstream reconstruction and verification steps follow. Computational steps are mostly bookkeeping once the mechanistic insight is established.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as retrosynthetic acetal analysis complexity: 2/5
Decide to work backward from the bicyclic acetal product to identify precursor X, using TsOH's role as acid catalyst as a mechanistic clue.
#2 [conceptual] Identify TsOH as acid catalyst for acetal exchange complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that p-toluenesulfonic acid promotes transacetalization reactions, enabling acetal formation or interconversion under thermodynamic control.
#3 [conceptual] Decode bicyclic acetal product structure complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Parse the product SMILES C[C@@]12CCC[C@@](O1)(OC2)C to establish that both oxygens bridge two quaternary carbons in a bicyclo[3.2.1]octane scaffold, confirming a bicyclic ketal.
#4 [computational] Compute molecular formula difference X minus product complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Subtract the product formula C8H14O2 from X's given formula C10H22O4 to obtain a difference of C2H8O2, which corresponds exactly to two molecules of methanol (2 × CH3OH).
#5 [conceptual] Identify transacetalization: dimethyl acetal plus diol complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #4
Conclude that X bears a dimethyl acetal and a 1,2-diol (or similar diol), and under acid catalysis undergoes intramolecular transacetalization, expelling two MeOH equivalents to close the bicyclic ring.
#6 [computational] Map bridgehead atoms and chain length in product complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Count atoms in each bridge of bicyclo[3.2.1]octane (3-carbon bridge, 2-carbon bridge, 1-carbon bridge via oxygen) to determine the number of methylenes separating the two acetal oxygens in the open-chain precursor.
#7 [computational] Reconstruct X by opening bicyclic acetal with 2 MeOH complexity: 4/5 ← #5, #6
Replace both ring C–O bonds at the dimethyl-substituted acetal carbon with –OMe groups and restore –OH on the other bridgehead carbon, yielding the open-chain precursor with a dimethyl acetal and a gem-diol functionality.
#8 [computational] Write SMILES and IUPAC name of X complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Record the reconstructed structure as CC(CCCC(C)(OC)OC)(CO)O, corresponding to 6,6-dimethoxy-2-methylheptane-1,2-diol.
