#9 [verification] Verify molecular formula of X is C10H22O4 complexity: 1/5 ← #8
Atom-count the SMILES CC(CCCC(C)(OC)OC)(CO)O: 10 carbons, 22 hydrogens, 4 oxygens — confirms the given formula C10H22O4.
#10 [verification] Confirm X contains an acetal as required complexity: 1/5 ← #8
Check that the –C(OMe)2– group in X satisfies the problem's explicit constraint that X contains an acetal (two OR groups on the same carbon).
#11 [verification] Confirm cyclization mechanism yields correct stereochemistry complexity: 4/5 ← #5, #8
Verify that acid-catalyzed intramolecular transacetalization of X selectively produces the (1S,5R) bicyclic ketal via the preferred chair-like transition state, consistent with the reported stereodescriptors.
#16 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Medium (3) nodes: 15 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_0f13c295-7991-43dc-a79f-d8f3d3fc0d57 ▼
Problem
Context: A research paper discusses the interaction of plasmonic silver (Ag) nanocubes with methylene blue (MB) under different laser wavelengths and photon intensities. Consider a plasmonic system where Ag nanocubes of edge length ( a = 75 ) nm are dispersed on a silicon substrate. The extinction spectrum of these nanocubes shows broad plasmon peaks, particularly at 532 nm and 785 nm. The quantum…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: 1. **Square Root of Enhancement Factor**   - **Full point:** Correctly calculates the square root of the electromagnetic enhancement to find ( \\eta ).      - Solution: (\\eta = \\Sigma^{1/2} = (4 \\times 10^4)^{1/2} = 200) Points: 1.0, Item: 10. **Rate Com…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level familiarity with plasmonics, SERS-like enhancement factors, and photochemical charge-transfer mechanisms.
reasoning_complexity
2/5 Once the framework is established, the solution is a straightforward multi-step arithmetic recipe with no branching.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 The one non-obvious step is recognizing that the electromagnetic enhancement factor Σ enters as its square root (η = Σ^{1/2}) to obtain the field enhancement, which then squares to give the intensity enhancement.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 9–10 distinct computational steps are required (η, I_eff × 2 wavelengths, excitation rates × 2, decomp rates × 2, ratio).
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: η feeds I_eff, which feeds excitation rates, which feed decomposition rates, which feed the final ratio — any error cascades through all downstream values.
Justification: —
Step graph — 15 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #7, #10, #13 The solution is a single deep chain with two parallel branches (one per wavelength) that merge at each aggregation step; computational steps dominate but the three conceptual formula-identification steps are the true bottlenecks because errors there invalidate all downstream arithmetic.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem using plasmonic enhancement model complexity: 2/5
Decide to compute MB decomposition rates by chaining: field enhancement → effective photon intensity → charge excitation rate → decomposition rate, then compare wavelengths.
#2 [conceptual] Relate enhancement factor Σ to field amplitude η complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that the electromagnetic enhancement factor Σ is an intensity ratio, so the field amplitude enhancement η equals the square root of Σ.
#3 [computational] Calculate η from Σ complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Compute η = √(4×10⁴) = 200, giving the local field amplitude enhancement factor used to boost photon intensity.
#4 [conceptual] State effective photon intensity formula complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Establish I_eff = I_photon × η², because the plasmonic field enhances the local photon density by η² (the intensity enhancement factor).
#5 [computational] Calculate I_eff at 785 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Compute I_eff,785 = 2.0×10²⁰ × (200)² = 8.0×10²⁴ photons/cm²/s.
#6 [computational] Calculate I_eff at 532 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Compute I_eff,532 = 2.0×10²⁰ × (200)² = 8.0×10²⁴ photons/cm²/s; same result because Σ and I_photon are identical at both wavelengths.
#7 [conceptual] State charge excitation rate formula complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6
Identify that the charge excitation rate equals the effective photon intensity multiplied by the wavelength-dependent charge excitation cross-section σ: Rate_exc = I_eff × σ.
#8 [computational] Calculate excitation rate at 785 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Compute Rate_exc,785 = 8.0×10²⁴ × 3×10⁻¹⁸ = 2.4×10⁷ electrons/s.
#9 [computational] Calculate excitation rate at 532 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Compute Rate_exc,532 = 8.0×10²⁴ × 1×10⁻¹⁸ = 8.0×10⁶ electrons/s.
#10 [conceptual] Identify decomposition rate formula complexity: 3/5 ← #8, #9
Recognize that the photochemical decomposition rate equals the charge excitation rate multiplied by the baseline (no-plasmon) decomposition rate, which encodes quantum efficiency for MB breakdown per excited electron.
#11 [computational] Calculate decomposition rate at 785 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #10
Compute Decomp_785 = 2.4×10⁷ × 0.1 = 2.4×10⁶ molecules/s.
#12 [computational] Calculate decomposition rate at 532 nm complexity: 1/5 ← #10
Compute Decomp_532 = 8.0×10⁶ × 0.02 = 1.6×10⁵ molecules/s.
#13 [conceptual] State rate comparison ratio formula complexity: 1/5 ← #11, #12
Define the comparison metric as Ratio = Decomp_785 / Decomp_532, which quantifies how much more effective 785 nm illumination is for MB decomposition.
#14 [computational] Compute wavelength decomposition rate ratio complexity: 1/5 ← #13
Calculate Ratio = 2.4×10⁶ / 1.6×10⁵ = 15, meaning 785 nm illumination produces MB decomposition 15× faster than 532 nm.
#15 [verification] Check units and physical reasonableness complexity: 2/5 ← #14
Confirm that all intermediate quantities carry consistent units (photons/cm²/s × cm² = electrons/s, then electrons/s × molecules/electron = molecules/s) and that the ratio >1 is physically sensible given the larger σ and higher baseline rate at 785 nm.
#17 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 11 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_f12a8706-3ddd-4255-9234-0655babceb8e ▼
Problem
Show your work when answering the following questions for the Latimer diagram of sulfur under acidic conditions: `\(SO_4^{2-}\)` to `\(SO_2\)` is 0.16V `\( SO_2 \)` to `\( S_4O_6^{2-} \)` is 0.54V `\( S_4O_6^{2-} \)` to `\( S_2O_3^{2-} \)` is 0.02V `\(S_2O_3^{2-}\)` to `\(S\)` is 0.49V `\(S\)` to `\(H_2S\)` is 0.17V Show whether thiosulfate, `\(S_2O_3^{2-}\)`, will form from comproportionati…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `\( S_2O_3^{2-} \)` will disproportionate, with E = 0.08V. (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level inorganic chemistry: Latimer diagram conventions, non-integer oxidation states in polythionate species (S₄O₆²⁻ at +2.5), and the ΔG-based method for combining non-adjacent half-cell potentials.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 The solver must chain several calculations — identify oxidation states, count electrons carefully for fractional-OS intermediates, apply ΔG additivity to derive a non-adjacent E°, then evaluate the sign of E°cell to draw the thermodynamic conclusion.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious insights are required: (1) that combining Latimer potentials demands ΔG/n weighting rather than simple averaging, and (2) that testing comproportionation vs. disproportionation means computing E°(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻) − E°(S₂O₃²⁻/S) and checking the sign.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 5–7 distinct steps: assign oxidation states, determine electron counts per step, apply ΔG additivity to get E°(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻), compute E°cell, interpret sign — fits comfortably in the 3–5 action band.
interdependence
4/5 Each calculation feeds the next: a wrong electron count for the S₄O₆²⁻ step propagates into the derived E°(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻), which corrupts the final E°cell and the comproportionation/disproportionation conclusion.
Justification: —
Step graph — 11 nodes | bottlenecks: #4, #8, #10 The solution is dominated by a single critical chain: assign oxidation states → compute n values → apply ΔG additivity to derive the non-adjacent potential → subtract to get E_cell. A error in the oxidation-state assignment (node 4) or the weighted-average formula (node 8) propagates directly to the final answer.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as comproportionation vs. disproportionation complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the question asks whether S₂O₃²⁻ is the thermodynamic sink (comproportionation favored) or source (disproportionation favored) relative to SO₂ and S, requiring comparison of two reduction potentials flanking S₂O₃²⁻.
#2 [conceptual] Identify relevant Latimer couples for S₂O₃²⁻ complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Locate the two couples adjacent to S₂O₃²⁻ in the context of the question: E(S₂O₃²⁻/S) = 0.49 V is given directly, while E(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻) spans two steps in the diagram (SO₂→S₄O₆²⁻→S₂O₃²⁻) and must be computed.
#3 [conceptual] Recognize non-adjacent potential requires ΔG additivity complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Because E(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻) spans two Latimer steps, it cannot be obtained by simple averaging; instead the ΔG = −nFE relation must be applied: n_total·E_total = Σ nᵢEᵢ.
#4 [computational] Assign S oxidation states in each species complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Calculate: S in SO₂ = +4; S in S₄O₆²⁻ = +2.5 (from 4x − 12 = −2); S in S₂O₃²⁻ = +2 (from 2x − 6 = −2); S in S = 0, establishing electron-transfer counts for each step.
#5 [computational] Calculate n per S for SO₂ → S₄O₆²⁻ complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Each S atom gains 4 − 2.5 = 1.5 electrons in the SO₂→S₄O₆²⁻ half-reaction, giving n₁ = 1.5 per S atom.
#6 [computational] Calculate n per S for S₄O₆²⁻ → S₂O₃²⁻ complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Each S atom gains 2.5 − 2 = 0.5 electrons in the S₄O₆²⁻→S₂O₃²⁻ half-reaction, giving n₂ = 0.5 per S atom.
#7 [computational] Confirm total n for SO₂ → S₂O₃²⁻ is 2 complexity: 1/5 ← #5, #6
Sum the partial electron counts: n_total = n₁ + n₂ = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2 per S atom, consistent with the direct change +4 → +2.
#8 [computational] Compute E(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻) via weighted average complexity: 2/5 ← #3, #7
Apply n_total·E = n₁·E₁ + n₂·E₂: E = (1.5 × 0.54 + 0.5 × 0.02) / 2 = (0.81 + 0.01) / 2 = 0.41 V.
#9 [conceptual] Set up disproportionation cell half-reactions complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #8
For disproportionation S₂O₃²⁻ → SO₂ + S, the cathode is S₂O₃²⁻→S (E_red = 0.49 V) and the anode is S₂O₃²⁻→SO₂ (E_red = 0.41 V, reverse for oxidation), so E_cell = E_cathode − E_anode.
#10 [computational] Calculate E_cell for disproportionation complexity: 1/5 ← #9
E_cell = E(S₂O₃²⁻/S) − E(SO₂/S₂O₃²⁻) = 0.49 − 0.41 = 0.08 V.
#11 [verification] Confirm sign and conclude spontaneity complexity: 2/5 ← #10
Since E_cell = +0.08 V > 0, ΔG < 0, confirming disproportionation is spontaneous; comproportionation would require E_cell < 0, which is not the case.
#18 FrontierScience-Olympiad chemistry Hard (4) nodes: 10 | bottlenecks: 4 fs_eec8840a-2d00-4e70-b043-0da51bd1b288 ▼
Problem
Colorless gas `\( A \)` reacts with pungent-smelling, colored gas `\( B \)` when exposed to radiation. From this, poisonous liquid `\( X \)` is extracted. `\( X \)` reacts with water (`\( H_2O \)`) to form two strong acids in a 2:1 ratio. When barium chloride (`\( BaCl_2 \)`) is added to the resulting solution, a precipitate of `\( C \)` is obtained. Liquid `\( Y \)`, which contains the same eleme…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: `Sulfuryl chloride (\(SO_2Cl_2)\) ` (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires knowledge of inorganic gas properties, photochemical reactions (SO₂ + Cl₂ → SO₂Cl₂ under UV), hydrolysis of sulfuryl chloride, and recognizing BaSO₄ as the precipitate.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must chain multiple deductions: gas identification from physical clues → photochemical reaction → hydrolysis stoichiometry → precipitate identity → dual molar-mass ratio verification.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious jumps: (1) recognizing Cl₂ as the colored pungent gas and SO₂ as colorless, (2) knowing the UV-initiated SO₂ + Cl₂ reaction, (3) identifying Y as SOCl₂ (thionyl chloride) via the 1.134 molar mass ratio (135/119).
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: identify B, identify A, write photochemical reaction, write hydrolysis equation, check 2:1 acid ratio, identify precipitate C, compute B/A molar mass ratio, compute X/Y molar mass ratio, confirm X = SO₂Cl₂.
interdependence
5/5 Fully sequential: misidentifying either gas immediately derails the hydrolysis reasoning and makes the molar mass ratio checks meaningless.
Justification: —
Step graph — 10 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #4, #5, #9 The solution is dominated by conceptual reasoning — identifying substances from qualitative clues — with computational steps serving as quantitative checkpoints. The critical bottleneck is node 5 (identifying X as SO2Cl2), since all downstream verification branches from it.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem using clues and molar mass ratios complexity: 2/5
Decide to identify A, B, X, Y by combining qualitative descriptors (color, smell, toxicity) with the two molar mass ratio constraints.
#2 [conceptual] Identify colored, pungent gas B as Cl2 complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Chlorine gas (Cl2) is the only common colored (yellow-green) and pungent-smelling diatomic gas that undergoes radical reactions under radiation.
#3 [conceptual] Hypothesize colorless gas A as SO2 complexity: 3/5 ← #1
SO2 is a colorless gas known to react with Cl2 under UV radiation to form a liquid product, making it a strong candidate for A.
#4 [computational] Verify B/A molar mass ratio with Cl2 and SO2 complexity: 2/5 ← #2, #3
Compute M(Cl2)/M(SO2) = 71/64 ≈ 1.109, which matches the given ratio of 1.107, confirming A = SO2 and B = Cl2.
#5 [conceptual] Derive X from SO2 + Cl2 radical reaction complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Under radiation, SO2 + Cl2 → SO2Cl2 (sulfuryl chloride), a poisonous, colorless liquid consistent with the description of X.
#6 [conceptual] Verify X hydrolyzes to two strong acids in 2:1 ratio complexity: 3/5 ← #5
SO2Cl2 + 2H2O → H2SO4 + 2HCl produces two strong acids (HCl and H2SO4) where HCl:H2SO4 = 2:1, matching the problem statement.
#7 [computational] Identify precipitate C as BaSO4 complexity: 1/5 ← #6
BaCl2 reacts with H2SO4 in the hydrolysis solution to form BaSO4↓, an insoluble white precipitate, confirming C = BaSO4.
#8 [conceptual] Identify liquid Y with same elements as X complexity: 3/5 ← #5
Y must be a liquid containing S, O, and Cl; thionyl chloride (SOCl2) is the known analogue differing only in one fewer oxygen atom.
#9 [computational] Verify X/Y molar mass ratio with SO2Cl2 and SOCl2 complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #8
Compute M(SO2Cl2)/M(SOCl2) = 135/119 ≈ 1.134, exactly matching the given ratio, confirming Y = SOCl2 and X = SO2Cl2.
#10 [verification] Confirm all clues satisfied; conclude X = SO2Cl2 complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #6, #7, #9
Cross-check that SO2Cl2 is poisonous, liquid, yields two strong acids in 2:1 ratio, produces BaSO4 with BaCl2, and both molar mass ratios are satisfied.
#19 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Hard (4) nodes: 17 | bottlenecks: 6 fs_0b946e4d-8fc8-4f65-85dc-8fb7fd227a96 ▼
Problem
Context: Climate change is increasing the demand for stress resilient crops. Chenopodium pallidicaule, native to the Andes, is an understudied crop with high potential for supporting food security. While Chenopodium pallidicaule may adapt fast to new environments, the plants will encounter new biotic pests and diseases and therefore research into the crop's immune system and disease resistance pro…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 2.0, Item: Describes an appropriate series of experiments to perform for investigating differential virulence between the candidate strains, where a suitable time period for experiments should be 3 days or less (0.5). Conduct a preliminary screen to evaluate the virulenc…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level plant pathology: knowledge of pathosystems, bacterial virulence strategies, infection protocols, CFU-based inoculation, and the genetic toolkit landscape around model pathogens like Pseudomonas syringae.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must integrate constraints from the novel, genetically uncharacterized host side with pathogen selection criteria, then design a two-phase infection screen and interpret five distinct quantitative/visual outcome combinations.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious insights are required: (1) the crop's lack of genetic tools forces the pathogen to compensate with rich pre-existing genomic resources; (2) a dose-escalation inoculum strategy starting at 10^5 CFU/ml; (3) distinguishing five biologically distinct outcome scenarios to classify virulence vs. resistance vs. latent growth.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct experimental steps: pathogen selection + justification, preliminary visual screen, inoculum concentration gradient, quantitative colony-count assay, outcome interpretation per scenario, replication requirements, and iteration with new pathovars if needed.
interdependence
3/5 Steps are moderately coupled — the preliminary visual screen gates which concentrations and pathovars advance to the quantitative phase, but individual outcome interpretations are largely parallel and a failed pathovar just triggers a lateral branch rather than invalidating everything.
Justification: —
Step graph — 17 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #2, #4, #6, #8, #11 The solution is dominated by computational steps that operationalize a five-scenario infection assay framework, but the critical bottlenecks are the two upstream conceptual nodes (crop tool deficit, P. syringae nomination) that constrain all downstream experimental design. The graph has a funnel-then-fan structure: strategic framing converges into pathogen selection, which fans out into parallel symptom-score interpretations before reconverging at the final pathovar recommendation.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem: novel crop needs fast pathosystem complexity: 2/5
Recognize that Chenopodium pallidicaule is understudied, lacks established genetic tools, and requires rapid pathosystem establishment to study immunity.
#2 [conceptual] Identify crop's genetic tool deficit as key constraint complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Establish that because the crop is novel and genetically uncharacterized, the model pathogen must compensate by being genetically well-studied with pre-existing mutants and tools.
#3 [strategic] Decide to use genetically tractable model pathogen complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #2
Choose a strategy where the pathogen—not the crop—bears the genetic amenability burden, enabling mechanistic dissection of virulence despite limited host-side tools.
#4 [conceptual] Identify Pseudomonas syringae as candidate pathogen complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Nominate Pseudomonas syringae as the model pathogen based on its broad pathovar diversity, well-known virulence mechanisms, and relevance to foliar plant disease.
#5 [computational] Justify P. syringae with ≥3 specific criteria complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Enumerate at least three justifications: broad genetic diversity of pathovars, ease of acquisition (e.g., NCPPB), ease of handling/propagation, pre-established infection protocols, wealth of virulence knowledge, and availability of genetic mutants.
#6 [strategic] Design two-phase virulence screening protocol complexity: 4/5 ← #4
Plan a sequential screen: Phase 1 uses visual symptom scoring across an inoculum concentration gradient to identify virulent pathovars quickly; Phase 2 adds quantitative bacterial growth measurement to confirm and characterize virulence.
#7 [computational] Run Phase 1: visual screen with inoculum gradient complexity: 3/5 ← #6
Inoculate Chenopodium pallidicaule with each candidate pathovar at concentrations from 10^5 CFU/ml up to 10^8 CFU/ml and score visual symptoms within ≤3 days to identify virulence thresholds rapidly.
#8 [computational] Run Phase 2: combine visual scoring with colony counts complexity: 4/5 ← #7
For pathovars showing symptoms (or borderline results) in Phase 1, repeat the assay and additionally quantify bacterial growth over time via colony-forming unit counts to distinguish true virulence from artefactual responses.
#9 [computational] Trigger pathovar replacement if no virulence observed complexity: 2/5 ← #7, #8
If no pathovar induces virulence symptoms across the full 10^5–10^8 CFU/ml range in Phase 1, conclude those candidates are unsuitable and select new pathovars for re-screening.
#10 [verification] Ensure reliability with replicates and repeats complexity: 2/5 ← #7, #8
Confirm conclusions only after experiments are independently repeated with a minimum of n=6 biological replicates per treatment to control for biological variation and experimental error.
#11 [conceptual] Define five-scenario interpretation framework complexity: 4/5 ← #8
Establish the logical decision tree mapping combinations of symptom presence/absence and bacterial growth direction (increase vs. reduction) to biological interpretations of virulence, pathogenicity, or host resistance.
#12 [computational] Apply scenario: growth reduction → not virulent complexity: 1/5 ← #11
When bacterial CFU counts decline significantly over time with no symptoms, classify the pathovar as avirulent on this host.
#13 [computational] Apply scenario: growth increase → virulent complexity: 1/5 ← #11
When bacterial CFU counts increase significantly over time, classify the pathovar as virulent, regardless of whether visible symptoms are also present.
#14 [computational] Apply scenario: symptoms + growth increase → disease complexity: 2/5 ← #11
When both visual symptoms and significant bacterial proliferation are observed together, conclude the symptoms represent genuine disease caused by a virulent pathovar.
#15 [computational] Apply scenario: no symptoms + growth → investigate further complexity: 2/5 ← #11
When bacteria multiply without inducing visible symptoms, classify the pathovar as possibly non-pathogenic and extend the experiment duration before drawing conclusions.
#16 [computational] Apply scenario: symptoms + growth reduction → host resistance complexity: 3/5 ← #11
When visual symptoms appear but bacterial counts decline, interpret the symptoms as plant immune responses (hypersensitive response or resistance reaction) rather than disease.
#17 [strategic] Conclude and justify best pathovar(s) selection complexity: 4/5 ← #9, #10, #12, #13, #14, #15, #16
Synthesize Phase 1 and Phase 2 data with the five-scenario framework to identify and justify the pathovar(s) that combine confirmed virulence, reliable symptom induction, and available genetic tools as most suitable for dissecting Chenopodium pallidicaule immunity.
#20 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Expert (5) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 4 fs_1eca2503-b141-4f9f-ab10-e27d272c93b0 ▼
Problem
A hypothetical organism's cell has n sets of chromosomes. In the F₀ generation of one population of this organism, all individuals have genotype aAAAA... (only one 'a' allele). The organism self-crosses in the F₀ generation, and in all subsequent generations, it crosses with the immediate previous generation. If the minimum generation in which a genotype with no 'A' allele (i.e. aaa...) can appear…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: 35 (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires solid understanding of polyploidy, allele dosage in polyploid organisms, and gamete formation — well beyond introductory genetics.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 Solver must derive a recurrence for which genotypes are reachable each generation under the unusual crossing scheme (self → cross-with-parent), then invert it to find minimum n — a non-standard multi-generation combinatorial derivation.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 At least three non-trivial jumps: (1) correctly modeling gamete allele composition for an n-ploid, (2) encoding the parent×self-progeny crossing scheme as a reachability problem, (3) recognizing that the constraint 'first appears in gen 8' translates to a specific combinatorial threshold on n.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 6–9 distinct steps: define notation, set up allele-count model, compute gamete distributions per generation, track minimum 'a' count achievable, formulate inequality, solve for n.
interdependence
5/5 Each generation's reachable genotype set is built strictly from the previous one; a mis-step in any generation propagates and makes the final answer wrong.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #4, #5, #7 The solution is a single deep chain dominated by conceptual steps; the critical bottleneck is recognizing that the Fibonacci recurrence emerges from the generation-crossing scheme, which unlocks all downstream computation.
#1 [strategic] Frame as max 'a' allele accumulation problem complexity: 3/5
Recast the question as: what is the maximum number of 'a' alleles achievable in the best individual of each generation, since the all-recessive genotype requires exactly n copies of 'a'.
#2 [conceptual] Model the generation crossing scheme formally complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify that the crossing rule produces F₁ = F₀ × F₀, and for k ≥ 2, Fₖ = Fₖ₋₁ × Fₖ₋₂ — each generation mates with the one immediately before it.
#3 [conceptual] Determine max gamete 'a' contribution from n-ploid parent complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #2
For an n-ploid parent carrying m copies of 'a', the maximum number of 'a' alleles a gamete can receive is min(m, ⌊n/2⌋), since a gamete inherits ⌊n/2⌋ alleles at the locus.
#4 [computational] Write recurrence for M(k) with base cases complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Define M(k) = maximum 'a' alleles in any individual at generation k, with M(0)=1, M(1)=2, and recurrence M(k) = min(M(k−1), ⌊n/2⌋) + min(M(k−2), ⌊n/2⌋).
#5 [conceptual] Recognize Fibonacci structure in unconstrained regime complexity: 4/5 ← #4
When ⌊n/2⌋ ≥ M(k−1) for all relevant k, the ⌊n/2⌋ caps are never binding and the recurrence reduces to the Fibonacci relation M(k) = M(k−1) + M(k−2).
#6 [computational] Compute Fibonacci sequence M(0) through M(8) complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Evaluate the sequence starting from M(0)=1, M(1)=2: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34, 55, giving M(7)=34 and M(8)=55.
#7 [conceptual] Formulate condition for first appearance in generation 8 complexity: 3/5 ← #6
The all-'a' genotype first appears in generation 8 if and only if M(7) < n ≤ M(8), i.e., n cannot be reached in generation 7 but can be reached in generation 8.
#8 [computational] Solve inequality for minimum n complexity: 1/5 ← #7
From M(7) < n, we get 34 < n, so the minimum integer n satisfying the condition is n = 35.
#9 [verification] Verify n=35 satisfies both boundary conditions complexity: 2/5 ← #8, #6
Confirm M(7)=34 < 35 (all-'a' cannot appear in generation 7) and M(8)=55 ≥ 35 (all-'a' can appear in generation 8), validating that 8 is indeed the minimum generation for n=35.
#21 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Hard (4) nodes: 20 | bottlenecks: 5 fs_7e32b7a6-9340-4a41-8c1f-89a0a91a6d3f ▼
Problem
Context: There are various methods to tag proteins in the lab with many variations of fluorescent proteins, which is particularly advantageous for immunofluorescent staining. A relatively new tag, called HaloTag, works in a similar vein. By utilizing a plasmid containing a donor HaloTag sequence, it is possible to utilize CRISPR to tag a gene of interest. With the addition of a ligand that binds…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.25, Item: Part 1A: Give point if acknowledges the potential for this protein to be a homodimer due to the large shift in kDa, even considering species. Points: 1.25, Item: Part 1A: Give point if it acknowledges proper HaloTag expression and transfection. Points: 1.25, …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level knowledge of western blotting, CRISPR knock-in, HaloTag mechanics, allele heterozygosity, protein trafficking, and PAGE electrophoresis variants.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must integrate gel band patterns, allele dosage, species variation, gel-type artifacts, and subcellular localization into coherent multi-part conclusions.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 At least three non-obvious jumps are required: (1) double bands imply incomplete tagging/heterozygosity rather than degradation; (2) lanes 2 & 3 being same species but differing in allele tag number; (3) HaloTag antibody cannot distinguish allele tagging status, and gel type must be known for structural interpretation.
plan_length
3/5 Six distinct sub-questions (1A, 1B, 1C, 1D×2 facts, 2A, 2B) each requiring a separate analytical step, totalling roughly 7–9 meaningful reasoning actions.
interdependence
2/5 Parts 1A–1D are loosely coupled (each builds on the same gel data but can be answered semi-independently), while Part 2 is largely independent of Part 1.
Justification: —
Step graph — 20 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3, #6, #13, #15 The solution is dominated by conceptual and computational steps organized around interpreting western blot band patterns; the key bottlenecks are understanding HaloTag mass addition, recognizing the homodimer implication, and appreciating that anti-HaloTag blotting cannot reveal untagged alleles.
#1 [strategic] Frame HaloTag western blot interpretation problem complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the experiment involves CRISPR-mediated endogenous HaloTag insertion, single-cell sorting, and western blotting with an anti-HaloTag antibody, setting up the interpretive framework.
#2 [conceptual] Understand HaloTag mass contribution to bands complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that HaloTag adds ~33 kDa to any protein it is fused to, so each detected band represents an endogenous protein allele plus the HaloTag moiety.
#3 [conceptual] Interpret heterozygous vs homozygous tagging patterns complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Establish that one band per lane indicates homozygous HaloTag insertion (both alleles tagged or only one allele present), while two bands indicate heterozygous insertion (one tagged, one untagged allele).
#4 [computational] Classify each lane as hetero- or homozygous complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Apply the heterozygous/homozygous framework: Lane 1 (2 bands) = heterozygous; Lane 2 (1 band) = homozygous; Lane 3 (2 bands) = heterozygous; Lane 4 (2 bands) = heterozygous.
#5 [computational] Estimate untagged protein sizes from band data complexity: 3/5 ← #2, #4
Subtract ~33 kDa (HaloTag) from the lower band in each two-band lane to infer native protein size: Lane 1 lower band 73 kDa → ~40 kDa native; Lane 3 lower band 75 kDa → ~42 kDa native; Lane 4 lower band 80 kDa → ~47 kDa native.
#6 [conceptual] Recognize large kDa shift indicates homodimerization complexity: 4/5 ← #5
Note that tagged bands (106–113 kDa) are roughly double the untagged bands (~40–47 kDa), suggesting the protein may function as a homodimer, as the shift is too large to be explained by HaloTag alone.
#7 [computational] Answer Part 1A: protein properties from gel complexity: 3/5 ← #6
Conclude that (a) HaloTag was successfully expressed and transfection worked, and (b) the protein is likely a homodimer given the large apparent molecular weight of the tagged bands relative to untagged ones.
#8 [conceptual] Identify sources of kDa variation across cell lines complexity: 3/5 ← #3, #5
Recognize that kDa differences across lanes reflect both species-specific differences in protein sequence/size and incomplete tagging (only one allele tagged vs. both), explaining the band pattern diversity.
#9 [computational] Answer Part 1B: reasons for varying kDa values complexity: 2/5 ← #8
State that varying kDa values arise because (a) different species have slightly different protein sizes and (b) incomplete transfection means only one allele may be tagged, producing both tagged and untagged bands.
#10 [computational] Compare tagged-band sizes across lanes to group species complexity: 3/5 ← #4, #5
Compare the HaloTag-bearing band sizes: Lane 2 = 108 kDa, Lane 3 upper band = 108 kDa — identical sizes suggest the same species; Lanes 1 (106 kDa) and 4 (113 kDa) differ, indicating different species.
#11 [computational] Answer Part 1C: identify same-species lanes complexity: 3/5 ← #10
Conclude that Lanes 2 and 3 are likely from the same species because both show a tagged band at 108 kDa; Lane 3 is heterozygous (one untagged allele at 75 kDa), Lane 2 is homozygous (both alleles tagged).
#12 [conceptual] Identify missing experimental detail: gel type complexity: 4/5 ← #6
Recognize that knowing whether SDS-PAGE (denaturing, separates by MW) or native PAGE (non-denaturing, separates by MW + charge + shape) was used is critical to interpreting band sizes and native protein structure.
#13 [conceptual] Identify missing protein detail: allele tagging status complexity: 4/5 ← #3, #12
Recognize that because the antibody targets HaloTag (not the protein itself), it cannot reveal whether untagged alleles exist; knowing whether both alleles were successfully tagged is essential to determine experiment success.
#14 [computational] Answer Part 1D: critical missing details complexity: 3/5 ← #12, #13
State that the experiment requires knowing (a) the gel type (SDS-PAGE vs native PAGE) to interpret protein structure, and (b) whether both alleles were tagged, which cannot be determined by anti-HaloTag blotting alone.
#15 [conceptual] Interpret cytoplasmic puncta vs membrane localization complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Understand that the discrepancy between cytoplasmic puncta (endogenous HaloTag) and membrane localization (virus-transfected overexpression) implies the HaloTag fusion may impair protein trafficking to the membrane.
#16 [computational] Answer Part 2A: what cytoplasmic localization implies complexity: 3/5 ← #15
Conclude that the protein is likely structurally compromised by the HaloTag fusion, preventing proper membrane trafficking, and/or that prior overexpression studies produced artifactual membrane localization.
#17 [conceptual] Identify validation strategy for HaloTag fusion complexity: 4/5 ← #15
Recognize that validating the HaloTag requires demonstrating the tagged protein retains normal downstream function, e.g., via a functional assay measuring receptor signaling activity.
#18 [computational] Answer Part 2B: propose one validation step complexity: 2/5 ← #17
Propose a functional assay (e.g., measuring downstream signaling pathway activation) to confirm the HaloTag-fused receptor retains biological activity and is not structurally compromised by the tag.
#19 [verification] Cross-check band size arithmetic for consistency complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #10, #11
Verify that inferred native protein sizes and HaloTag-added sizes are internally consistent across all lanes and species groupings, catching arithmetic errors in kDa subtraction.
#20 [verification] Confirm homodimer hypothesis is consistent across lanes complexity: 3/5 ← #6, #7, #19
Check that the homodimer interpretation (double molecular weight in tagged bands) is consistent across all four lanes and not contradicted by any single-band lane data.
#22 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Expert (5) nodes: 21 | bottlenecks: 4 fs_1ca773ca-3f07-4426-8086-d1f3591cdf5f ▼
Problem
Context: Spectral cytometry is becoming a popular tool due to its ability to measure a higher number of fluorescently labeled antibodies, providing higher resolution of heterogeneous cell populations. ILCs and NK cells share a developmental trajectory that can be identified based on the expression of surface receptors and transcription factors. Being able to create complex flow panels that allow y…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: Each marker selected for the panel is assigned a specific peak channel that must be compatible with Cytek Aurora Spectral Cytometer. (i.e. peak channel UV11, UV16, V1, V10, etc.). Points: 1.0, Item: Includes transcription factors including NFIL3 and ID2 that a…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires research-frontier knowledge of ILC/NK developmental biology, spectral cytometry instrumentation, and commercial antibody availability across multiple vendors.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must simultaneously satisfy ~11 graded criteria spanning instrument compatibility, developmental hierarchy logic, transcription factor co-expression, and dump channel design.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two moderate non-obvious jumps: mapping developmental trajectory onto a single panel architecture, and resolving spectral overlap constraints of the Cytek Aurora across UV/V/B/YG/R laser lines.
plan_length
5/5 Panel design requires 21+ distinct decisions: selecting each marker, assigning peak channels, defining gating hierarchy for progenitors → precursors → mature subsets, plus dump channel and viability dye.
interdependence
4/5 Channel assignments constrain which fluorochrome-antibody conjugates are available; a poor early channel allocation cascades into unavailable commercial antibodies or unresolvable spectral overlap for downstream markers.
Justification: —
Step graph — 21 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #2, #17, #18 The solution is dominated by computational marker-selection steps that fan out from a critical conceptual bottleneck (the developmental hierarchy map) and converge at the fluorochrome channel-assignment node, which gates all downstream verification; the long parallel expansion across ILC/NK subsets followed by a single consolidation at node 18 is the defining structural feature.
#1 [strategic] Define panel goal and experimental scope complexity: 2/5
Establish that the panel must resolve NK cells and all ILC subsets across developmental stages from progenitor to mature cell, using cryopreserved peripheral blood and tonsil samples on the Cytek Aurora.
#2 [conceptual] Map NK/ILC developmental hierarchy complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Reconstruct the developmental trajectory from common lymphoid progenitor (CLP) → EILP → CILCP → NKP/ChILP → mature NK and ILC1/2/3 subsets to enumerate every population the panel must distinguish.
#3 [strategic] Choose spectral cytometry design strategy complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Decide to leverage the Cytek Aurora's full-spectrum unmixing to accommodate the large marker count required, and plan to assign each fluorochrome to a specific peak channel (e.g., UV11, V10, B3).
#4 [conceptual] Identify earliest progenitor populations and markers complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Determine that CLPs and EILPs are the earliest target populations and require surface markers CD34, integrin β7, and CD10 to distinguish them from downstream precursors.
#5 [conceptual] Identify innate lymphoid precursor populations complexity: 4/5 ← #2
Define the intermediate precursor populations to resolve: common innate lymphoid precursor (CILCP), natural killer precursor (NKP), and common helper innate lymphoid precursor (ChILP), each requiring PLZF alongside lineage-restricted markers.
#6 [conceptual] Identify mature NK cell subset markers complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Enumerate the three NK subsets — CD56bright, CD56dim, and CD57+ CD56dim — and the discriminating markers needed: CD56, CD94, CD16, CD57, TBET, and EOMES.
#7 [conceptual] Identify ILC1, ILC2, and ILC3 subset markers complexity: 4/5 ← #2
Enumerate the ILC subsets and their canonical defining markers: ILC1/ieILC1 (CD103, TBET, GATA3, CD127), ILC2 (CD127, CD117, CD294/CRTH2, GATA3), and ILC3 subsets NCR− ILC3, NCR+ ILC3, and LTi (CD127, CD117, RORγT, NKp44, CCR6).
#8 [conceptual] Design lineage exclusion dump channel complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Determine that T cells (CD3), B cells (CD19), myeloid cells (CD14), and stromal cells (CD31) must be excluded in a single dump channel to gate on the innate lymphoid compartment.
#9 [computational] Select antibodies for earliest progenitor markers complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Choose commercially available anti-CD34, anti-integrin β7, and anti-CD10 antibodies from BioLegend, BD Biosciences, or Miltenyi with fluorochromes suitable for Aurora spectral separation.
#10 [computational] Select transcription factor antibodies for progenitors and precursors complexity: 4/5 ← #4, #5
Choose antibodies against NFIL3, ID2, and PLZF — intracellular targets requiring fixation/permeabilization — with compatible fluorochromes (typically BV or PE family) from available vendors.
#11 [computational] Select antibodies for NK cell subset markers complexity: 3/5 ← #6
Choose anti-CD56, anti-CD94, anti-CD16, anti-CD57 (surface) and anti-TBET, anti-EOMES (intracellular) antibody-fluorochrome combinations that provide sufficient brightness to resolve dim/bright populations.
#12 [computational] Select antibodies for ILC1 and ieILC1 markers complexity: 3/5 ← #7
Choose anti-CD103, anti-CD127 (surface) and anti-TBET, anti-GATA3 (intracellular) antibodies, ensuring GATA3 fluorochrome does not conflict with the ILC2 channel assignments.
#13 [computational] Select antibodies for ILC2 gating markers complexity: 3/5 ← #7
Choose anti-CD127, anti-CD117 (c-Kit), anti-CD294 (CRTH2/PGDR2), and anti-GATA3 antibodies, confirming the ILC2 gate is defined as CD127+ CD117+ CD294+ GATA3+.
#14 [computational] Select antibodies for ILC3 gating markers complexity: 4/5 ← #7
Choose anti-CD127, anti-CD117, anti-NKp44, anti-CCR6, and anti-RORγT antibodies to distinguish NCR− ILC3 (RORγT+ NKp44−), NCR+ ILC3 (RORγT+ NKp44+), and LTi (RORγT+ CCR6+) populations.
#15 [computational] Select dump channel antibody cocktail complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Pool anti-CD3, anti-CD19, anti-CD14, and anti-CD31 antibodies onto a single bright fluorochrome (e.g., APC-Cy7 or BV785) to create one exclusion parameter.
#16 [computational] Include viability dye in panel complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Add a live/dead discriminator (e.g., DAPI for fresh cells, or Zombie Aqua / 7-AAD for cryopreserved samples) compatible with the Aurora UV or violet laser to exclude dead cells before gating.
#17 [conceptual] Map Aurora laser lines to detector peak channels complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Enumerate the Cytek Aurora laser configuration (UV 355 nm, violet 405 nm, blue 488 nm, yellow-green 561 nm, red 638 nm) and available peak detector channels (UV1–UV16, V1–V16, B1–B14, YG1–YG9, R1–R8) to create a fluorochrome placement grid.
#18 [computational] Assign each marker a specific Aurora peak channel complexity: 5/5 ← #9, #10, #11, #12, #13, #14, #15, #16, #17
Allocate every antibody-fluorochrome pair to a unique peak detector channel (e.g., DAPI→UV11, BV421→V4, FITC→B3, PE→YG3, APC→R4) ensuring no two markers share the same channel and that dim intracellular markers are on bright, spectrally distinct fluorochromes.
#19 [verification] Verify spectral compatibility across all fluorochromes complexity: 4/5 ← #18
Use Cytek's SpectroFlo or a spectral unmixing simulator to confirm that every fluorochrome pair can be reliably unmixed, with particular attention to dim intracellular TF channels adjacent to bright surface marker fluorochromes.
#20 [verification] Confirm commercial availability of all antibodies complexity: 2/5 ← #18
Cross-reference each antibody clone and conjugate against the BioLegend, BD Biosciences, and Miltenyi catalogs to verify every reagent is purchasable, including the viability dye.
#21 [verification] Confirm gating strategy resolves all target populations complexity: 4/5 ← #18, #19
Trace the complete hierarchical gating tree — from singlets → live → dump− → progenitors/precursors/NK/ILC1/ILC2/ILC3 — to confirm every scored population (CLP, EILP, CILCP, NKP, ChILP, CD56bright, CD56dim, CD57+CD56dim, ieILC1, ILC1, ILC2, NCR−ILC3, NCR+ILC3, LTi) is uniquely resolved.
#23 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Expert (5) nodes: 15 | bottlenecks: 4 fs_676e50c1-cea4-4361-81e1-d4409a23422c ▼
Problem
Context: The misfolding and aggregation of specific proteins are central to the pathology of many neurodegenerative diseases. While these proteins are often soluble and functional under normal physiological conditions, cellular stress can trigger their accumulation into aberrant assemblies. Biomolecular condensates, formed through liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS), are increasingly recognized …
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: Concludes that the hyper-stabilized RRM1 will substantially reduce the likelihood or kinetics of intra-condensate demixing by inhibiting disulfide bond formation. Points: 1.0, Item: Details that RRM1 domain local unfolding under oxidative conditions is necessa…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires research-frontier knowledge spanning LLPS biology, TDP-43 pathomechanisms, RRM domain redox chemistry, and lipid-protein interactions in condensates.
reasoning_complexity
5/5 Must integrate multiple molecular mechanisms (intra-condensate demixing, disulfide bonding, α-helix to β-sheet transition, fatty acid effects on condensate properties) into a coherent predictive framework.
conceptual_leaps
5/5 Requires non-obvious insights: that oxidative stress causes RRM1 local unfolding to expose cysteines, that demixing precedes aggregation, and that fatty acids alter condensate microenvironment to modulate these dynamics — none of which are derivable from first principles alone.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 6–8 distinct reasoning steps: describe SG recruitment, identify intra-condensate demixing, link oxidative stress to RRM1 unfolding, link cysteine exposure to disulfide bonding, analyze stabilized RRM1 variant, analyze fatty acid elevation, and synthesize combined effect.
interdependence
5/5 Each mechanistic step strictly depends on the prior: missing the demixing step or the cysteine-exposure mechanism entirely undermines the prediction about the RRM1 variant and the combined effect.
Justification: —
Step graph — 15 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #6, #13 The solution is dominated by computational and conceptual steps arranged in a deep sequential chain, reflecting the mechanistic cascade from LLPS recruitment through disulfide-driven demixing to solid-state transition; the perturbation analysis (nodes 8–14) branches off the canonical pathway at node 4 (RRM1 unfolding), making that node and the synergy-loss node (13) the critical bottlenecks.
#1 [strategic] Frame TDP-43 aggregation as two-stage LLPS problem complexity: 2/5
Decompose the solution into (a) the canonical SG-to-aggregate pathway and (b) perturbation analysis of two molecular alterations, establishing that both parts share the intra-condensate demixing mechanism as the pivot point.
#2 [conceptual] Identify oxidative stress as critical initiating trigger complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Pinpoint chronic oxidative stress as a distinct upstream event that drives SG formation and recruits TDP-43, setting the biochemical context for all downstream molecular changes.
#3 [computational] State TDP-43 up-concentration in SGs as initiating event complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Explain that liquid-liquid phase separation concentrates TDP-43 within SG condensates far above bulk cytoplasmic levels, making this local up-concentration the critical first step toward pathological aggregation.
#4 [conceptual] Identify RRM1 local unfolding exposing cysteine residues complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #3
Establish that oxidative stress destabilizes the RRM1 domain, causing local unfolding that exposes cysteine residues normally buried in the folded structure, which is a necessary prerequisite for subsequent intermolecular cross-linking.
#5 [computational] Describe intermolecular disulfide bond formation between cysteines complexity: 3/5 ← #4
Detail how the exposed RRM1 cysteines of adjacent up-concentrated TDP-43 molecules form intermolecular disulfide bonds under oxidizing conditions, creating covalent homotypic TDP-43 interactions.
#6 [conceptual] Link disulfide bonds to intra-condensate demixing complexity: 4/5 ← #5
Identify intra-condensate demixing as the key mechanistic step where disulfide-cross-linked TDP-43 clusters separate from the bulk SG liquid phase into a denser, TDP-43-enriched sub-phase, driven by combined disulfide and hydrophobic (HP) interactions.
#7 [computational] Describe liquid-to-solid transition via α-helix to cross-β-sheet change complexity: 4/5 ← #6
Explain that the demixed TDP-43 sub-phase undergoes a liquid-to-solid transition in which the α-helical conformation of the hydrophobic/prion-like (HP) region converts to a cross-β-sheet amyloid-like structure, producing the irreversible pathological aggregate.
#8 [conceptual] Analyze hyper-stabilized RRM1 resistance to local unfolding complexity: 4/5 ← #4
Reason that a genetic variant conferring significantly increased structural stability to RRM1 raises the energy barrier for local domain unfolding, thereby preventing the conformational change required to expose buried cysteine residues under oxidative stress.
#9 [computational] Predict reduced cysteine exposure from stabilized RRM1 complexity: 3/5 ← #8
Conclude that, because RRM1 remains stably folded even under oxidative conditions, its cysteine residues remain buried and inaccessible for intermolecular reactions, quantitatively reducing the pool of reactive cysteines relative to wild-type.
#10 [computational] Predict substantially reduced disulfide bond formation complexity: 3/5 ← #9
Project that the decrease in exposed cysteines directly and substantially reduces the rate and extent of intermolecular disulfide bond formation between TDP-43 molecules within the SG, weakening the primary covalent driver of intra-condensate demixing.
#11 [conceptual] Analyze role of elevated C6–C10 saturated fatty acids in HP interactions complexity: 4/5 ← #6
Identify that short-chain (C6–C10) saturated fatty acids can interact with hydrophobic/prion-like (HP) domains of TDP-43 or alter condensate membrane/lipid microenvironments, potentially modulating HP-driven TDP-43 self-association within SGs.
#12 [computational] Assess fatty acid contribution to condensate HP-driven interactions complexity: 4/5 ← #11
Evaluate whether the five-fold elevation in C6–C10 fatty acids amplifies HP-mediated TDP-43 self-interaction within the condensate; note that while this could promote some condensation, it cannot alone substitute for the disulfide-cross-linking arm of demixing.
#13 [computational] Predict loss of synergy between disulfide bonding and HP interactions complexity: 5/5 ← #10, #12
Combine the findings from nodes 10 and 12 to conclude that the combined alterations eliminate the critical mechanistic synergy: even if fatty acids slightly boost HP interactions, the absence of RRM1-mediated disulfide cross-links breaks the cooperative drive needed to trigger intra-condensate demixing.
#14 [computational] Conclude overall reduction in pathological aggregation via demixing pathway complexity: 3/5 ← #13
State the final prediction: the combined effect of a hyper-stabilized RRM1 and elevated fatty acids results in an overall net reduction in TDP-43 pathological aggregation specifically via the intra-condensate demixing pathway compared to wild-type neurons under identical oxidative stress.
#15 [verification] Verify mechanistic consistency of combined prediction complexity: 3/5 ← #14
Cross-check that each element of the prediction (reduced unfolding → fewer disulfide bonds → impaired demixing → reduced solid aggregation) is internally consistent and that no alternative pathway allows the elevated fatty acids alone to rescue full pathological aggregation.
#24 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Hard (4) nodes: 7 | bottlenecks: 2 fs_f7b49518-6e84-44e3-9480-4cf91d149702 ▼
Problem
In devising a drug delivery system for an in vitro experiment using cultured hepatic cells, a PhD student designed three different approaches: - Covalently linking the payload (hereby a small non-bioactive hydrophobic molecule) to a DNA origami structure - Conjugating the payload to a nanobody designed to target a currently unidentified surface orphan receptor. - Linking the payload to a DNA tetr…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Endosomal membrane protein recycling (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level knowledge spanning DNA nanotechnology, nanobody engineering, orphan receptor biology, and intracellular membrane trafficking.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Solver must chain: nanobody binds orphan receptor → receptor lacks canonical endocytic signal → payload still enters cell → therefore constitutive membrane protein recycling must be the mechanism.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-trivial jumps required: (1) recognize that surface proteins are constitutively internalized independent of signaling; (2) connect this to endosomal recycling pathways; (3) distinguish constitutive recycling from canonical receptor-mediated endocytosis.
plan_length
2/5 Solution involves roughly 3–5 reasoning steps; no lengthy computation, code, or multi-turn workflow.
interdependence
4/5 Each inferential step strictly depends on the previous one; misidentifying the mechanism at any stage derails the final answer.
Justification: —
Step graph — 7 nodes | bottlenecks: #4, #5 The solution is predominantly conceptual: the entire chain hinges on recalling that membrane proteins undergo constitutive endosomal recycling independent of canonical signaling, making nodes 4 and 5 the critical bottlenecks where biological knowledge gaps would derail the answer.
#1 [strategic] Frame problem as intracellular payload delivery complexity: 2/5
Identify that the core question is how a hydrophobic payload attached to a nanobody can enter the cell even when its target receptor does not canonically trigger endocytosis.
#2 [conceptual] Characterize nanobody as surface-receptor binder complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Establish that the nanobody binds a specific orphan receptor on the hepatic cell surface and remains extracellular until the receptor itself moves.
#3 [conceptual] Interpret PI's clue about non-canonical endocytosis complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Parse the PI's statement that the receptor's canonical signaling role is irrelevant, meaning the uptake mechanism must be independent of ligand-induced endocytosis.
#4 [conceptual] Recall constitutive internalization of membrane proteins complexity: 4/5 ← #2, #3
Apply knowledge that all plasma-membrane proteins, including orphan receptors, are continuously and constitutively internalized into endosomes as part of normal membrane homeostasis regardless of their signaling state.
#5 [conceptual] Identify endosomal membrane-protein recycling pathway complexity: 4/5 ← #4
Recognize that internalized receptor–nanobody complexes are trafficked into early endosomes and then recycled back to the cell surface via the endosomal membrane protein recycling route, transiently exposing the payload to the intracellular environment.
#6 [computational] Map nanobody-receptor complex to recycling route complexity: 3/5 ← #5
Trace the fate of the nanobody–payload conjugate: co-internalization with the orphan receptor into endosomes provides intracellular access for the hydrophobic payload without requiring any canonical endocytic signaling.
#7 [verification] Confirm mechanism satisfies PI's independence claim complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6
Check that endosomal membrane protein recycling operates constitutively and independently of whether the receptor induces endocytosis upon ligand binding, validating the PI's assertion that receptor identification is unnecessary.
#25 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Medium (3) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 fs_8b695bb3-ea3c-4372-9222-9f0b79a68b6c ▼
Problem
Biopsies of solid tumors from placebo patients and patients that have been given drug X were collected. Total RNA was reverse-transcribed and scRNA-seq was performed. A high-dimensional data matrix (6 patients x 100,000 cells x 9,000 genes) is collected and principal component analysis (PCA) was performed. Plotting a scatter plot of treated (T) and untreated samples (C) (6 patients each, numbered …
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Batch; UMAP (Full derivation not provided; annotator should infer solution steps from the problem structure and the known correct answer.)…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires graduate-level familiarity with scRNA-seq pipelines, PCA interpretation in genomics, batch effects, and dimensionality reduction methods.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must cross-reference PCA clustering patterns with experimental metadata to diagnose batch effects, then separately recall the canonical cell-type visualization tool.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious jumps: (1) recognizing that patient-wise co-clustering across treatment arms signals batch rather than drug effects, and (2) knowing UMAP specifically is the standard for qualitative cell-population visualization.
plan_length
2/5 Essentially a two-part short-answer question requiring ~4 distinct reasoning steps: parse PCA groupings, map to batch-effect concept, pivot to second question, recall UMAP.
interdependence
2/5 The two sub-answers (batch effects; UMAP) are largely independent—failing to identify batch effects does not block recalling UMAP as a visualization method.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #7 The solution has two parallel conceptual chains (batch-effect identification and UMAP selection) that converge only at the final verification node; conceptual steps dominate because both answers require domain knowledge recall rather than calculation.
#1 [strategic] Identify the two sub-questions in the problem complexity: 1/5
Recognize the problem asks (1) what the PCA clustering pattern implies and (2) what method visualizes cell-type diversity — solving them in sequence.
#2 [conceptual] Parse PCA cluster membership for T/C mixing complexity: 2/5 ← #1
List the five PCA clusters and note that each contains a mixture of treated (T) and control (C) samples rather than separating by condition.
#3 [conceptual] Distinguish biological signal from technical variation complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that if drug treatment were driving variance, T and C samples would segregate; mixed clusters indicate a non-biological systematic source of variance.
#4 [conceptual] Identify the source of variation as batch effects complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Map the patient-level co-clustering pattern (e.g., C1 with C3, T6 with C2/C4) to the concept of batch effects — technical artifacts from sample processing runs or patient covariates dominating PC1/PC2.
#5 [computational] State Part 1 answer: batch effects present complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Conclude that the PCA result suggests the presence of batch effects, completing the first part of the answer.
#6 [conceptual] Identify need for cell-population visualization in scRNA-seq complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Acknowledge that a 9,000-gene, 100,000-cell dataset requires a non-linear dimensionality reduction method to render discrete cell-type clusters visible to the eye.
#7 [conceptual] Select UMAP as standard scRNA-seq visualization method complexity: 2/5 ← #6
Recall that Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) is the field-standard technique for projecting high-dimensional scRNA-seq data to 2D, preserving local cluster structure to qualitatively reveal cell-type populations.
#8 [computational] State Part 2 answer: apply UMAP complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Conclude that UMAP is the method the researcher should apply to visualize and qualitatively assess cell types/populations.
#9 [verification] Confirm answers address both parts of the question complexity: 1/5 ← #5, #8
Check that 'batch effects' answers the PCA pattern question and 'UMAP' answers the cell-type visualization question, matching the expected two-part answer format.
#26 FrontierScience-Olympiad biology Expert (5) nodes: 29 | bottlenecks: 8 fs_dcfa830a-3f87-43eb-9ec7-6649fcdd0473 ▼
Problem
Context: In evolutionary developmental biology research, it is critical to understand the role of a gene during critical periods of growth and differentiation. A protein may play vastly different roles within development depending on the timing of expression. Here, I test the model on its ability to understand the role of certain proteins in both spatial and temporal variations. Question: 1. Chick…
Solution (excerpt)
Ground truth answer: Points: 1.0, Item: 1. Somatopleure and limbs Answer states: - Although the limbs are not visible on the embryo, cells are already committed to their participation in limb development. (0.33 points) - Experimental modification of HH11 somatopleure resulted in over half of the e…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
5/5 Requires graduate-level evo-devo knowledge spanning HOX gene cascades, retinoic acid signaling, limb field specification, somitic mesoderm lineages, and median fin evolution — well beyond any introductory or intermediate biology course.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 Each sub-question demands integrating multiple signaling pathways, developmental staging systems, and comparative evolutionary frameworks rather than simple recall or a linear procedure.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Several non-obvious connections must be made: linking CYP26A1 enzyme activity to RA gradient and positional identity, inferring that HOX9 at HH17 acts via HAND2/SHH rather than limb-field specification, and connecting median-fin paraxial mesoderm origin to fin-fold evolutionary hypotheses.
plan_length
4/5 Seven distinct sub-questions each requiring independent retrieval, reasoning, and integration steps amounts to roughly 14–20 discrete answer components.
interdependence
2/5 The sub-questions are largely independent of one another — a wrong answer on Q3 does not propagate errors into Q5 or Q6 — though shared conceptual vocabulary (HOX genes, RA signaling) runs across several questions.
Justification: —
Step graph — 29 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #2, #13, #14, #18, #21, #25, #26 The solution graph is a wide fan-out from a single strategic root into seven largely independent sub-problem branches, dominated by conceptual recall steps that gate downstream computational predictions; the median-fin axis sub-problem (Q7) forms the deepest chain, requiring the most inferential steps from shared signaling to evolutionary interpretation.
#1 [strategic] Frame multi-part evo-devo question set complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the seven sub-questions test spatiotemporal Hox gene knowledge, limb field specification, retinoic acid signaling, and fin evolution, requiring each sub-problem to be answered with stage- and gene-specific precision.
#2 [conceptual] Recall HH11 developmental context complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Identify HH11 (~2 dpc) as the beginning of somitogenesis, a stage at which limb fields are morphologically invisible but cell fate is already committed.
#3 [conceptual] Apply early cell-fate commitment principle complexity: 4/5 ← #2
Recognize that pre-committed limb progenitors in the somatopleure cannot compensate for spatial perturbation, so rotation disrupts positional information without morphological limbs being present.
#4 [computational] Predict somatopleure rotation outcome complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Conclude that rotating and re-grafting the somatopleure at HH11 produces limb malformations or transformations in over half of embryos because fated cells are repositioned before any compensatory mechanism can act.
#5 [verification] Verify Q1 consistency with fate-map data complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Confirm that the predicted malformation outcome is consistent with experimental somatopleure fate-mapping literature showing >50% limb abnormalities after HH11 rotation.
#6 [conceptual] Identify forelimb tracing stage and Hox genes complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Recall that forelimb-specific cells can be traced back to HH4–5 (1 dpc) and are marked by HOXB4 and HOXB7 expression for early identification.
#7 [conceptual] Identify interlimb tracing stage and Hox gene complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Recall that the interlimb region is specified at HH6–7 (1.25–1.5 dpc), slightly later than the forelimb, and is determined by HOX9 expression.
#8 [conceptual] Identify hindlimb tracing stage and Hox genes complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Recall that hindlimb cells are committed at HH8–9 (end of day 1 post-conception) and are specifically targeted by HOXB6 through HOXB9.
#9 [verification] Confirm temporal ordering of three limb fields complexity: 2/5 ← #6, #7, #8
Cross-check that the forelimb (HH4–5) → interlimb (HH6–7) → hindlimb (HH8–9) temporal sequence is internally consistent and matches known anterior-to-posterior developmental staging.
#10 [conceptual] Identify HOXB5 role at cervico-thoracic boundary complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Establish that HOXB5 is required for specifying the position of the forelimb at the cervico-thoracic transition zone, setting the stage for predicting deletion phenotypes.
#11 [computational] Determine anterior forelimb shift from HOXB5 deletion complexity: 3/5 ← #10
Apply HOXB5 positional function to predict that its deletion causes the forelimb to shift anteriorly (cranially) along the body axis in the murine model.
#12 [computational] Specify scapula and clavicle as affected structures complexity: 3/5 ← #11
Identify that the anterior shift of the forelimb manifests as malformations specifically in the scapula and clavicle, the shoulder girdle elements that mark the forelimb–axial boundary.
#13 [conceptual] Recall RA role in limb field specification complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Establish that retinoic acid (RA) is a critical morphogen for defining the anterior extent and identity of the limb field during gastrulation.
#14 [conceptual] Identify CYP26A1 as RA-degrading enzyme complexity: 3/5 ← #13
Recall that CYP26A1 is a cytochrome P450 enzyme responsible for RA catabolism, so its overexpression reduces local RA availability.
#15 [computational] Predict CYP26A1 overexpression limb phenotype complexity: 4/5 ← #14
Reason that CYP26A1 overexpression during gastrulation lowers RA levels, potentially causing the limb field to shift posteriorly or expand, and resulting in poorly formed limbs due to RA underexpression.
#16 [computational] Predict exogenous RA treatment limb phenotype complexity: 3/5 ← #14
Reason that excess exogenous RA would cause the limb field to shift anteriorly and may induce ectopic limb formation, producing the phenotypic opposite of CYP26A1 overexpression.
#17 [verification] Verify RA and CYP26A1 phenotypes are inverse complexity: 2/5 ← #15, #16
Confirm that the anterior-shift/ectopic-limb RA phenotype is indeed opposite to the posterior-shift/poor-formation CYP26A1 overexpression phenotype, validating the RA-axis logic.
#18 [conceptual] Identify HH17 as post-specification patterning stage complexity: 4/5 ← #1
Recognize that HH17 is past the initial limb-field specification window, so HOX9 function at this stage is no longer about forelimb/hindlimb identity but about downstream patterning via TBX-independent pathways.
#19 [conceptual] Recall HOX9–HAND2–SHH regulatory axis complexity: 4/5 ← #18
Recall that at HH17, HOX9 promotes SHH signaling through HAND2, a transcription factor that activates SHH in the zone of polarizing activity.
#20 [computational] Identify SHH as HOX9 deletion target at HH17 complexity: 3/5 ← #19
Conclude that deleting HOX9 at HH17 specifically impairs SHH signaling (via reduced HAND2 activity), not limb-identity specification, and note that TBX-gene-based answers are incorrect for this stage.
#21 [conceptual] Recall median fin origin from paraxial mesoderm complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Establish that in fish, median fins arise from paraxial (somitic) mesoderm, in contrast to paired appendages which arise from lateral plate mesoderm.
#22 [conceptual] Connect paraxial origin to fin-fold hypothesis complexity: 4/5 ← #21
Recall the fin-fold hypothesis, which proposes that a single ancestral unpaired fin gave rise to both median unpaired fins and eventually paired appendages.
#23 [computational] Assess evolutionary age of paraxial vs lateral plate origin complexity: 4/5 ← #22
Reason that paraxial/somitic mesoderm is evolutionarily older than lateral plate mesoderm, informing why basal taxa are used as reference points for appendage origin hypotheses.
#24 [computational] Apply median fin origin to support fin-fold hypothesis complexity: 3/5 ← #23
Conclude that modern fish median fins arising from paraxial mesoderm in basal taxa supports the fin-fold hypothesis by providing a mechanistic and evolutionary link between unpaired median fins and ancestral paired appendages.
#25 [conceptual] Identify shared FGF, SHH, and AEF signaling in median fins complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Recall that median fins express FGFs and SHH and form an apical ectodermal fold (AEF) that is functionally analogous to the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) of paired limbs.
#26 [conceptual] Identify absence of DV axis in median fins complexity: 4/5 ← #25
Recognize that median fins lack a dorsoventral (DV) axis comparable to that of paired appendages, despite expressing WNT7A across the entire fin ectoderm rather than only dorsally.
#27 [computational] Contrast WNT7A expression domain in fins vs limbs complexity: 4/5 ← #26
Compute the functional consequence: in limbs WNT7A is restricted to dorsal ectoderm to establish DV polarity, whereas in median fins WNT7A covers the entire ectoderm yet no DV axis forms, indicating ventralizing signals are absent.
#28 [computational] Conclude DV axis is evolutionarily derived complexity: 4/5 ← #27
Infer that the lack of DV axis in median fins despite WNT7A expression suggests that ventralizing signals are evolutionarily novel, making the DV axis younger than the proximo-distal and anterior-posterior axes seen in both median fins and paired appendages.
#29 [verification] Verify all three axes addressed for median fins complexity: 2/5 ← #28
Confirm that the answer covers proximo-distal (AEF/FGF), anterior-posterior (SHH), and dorsoventral (WNT7A/absent ventralization) axes, and that the comparison to mouse/chick limb development is explicit.
#27 SWE-bench Verified scikit-learn Trivial (1) nodes: 6 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_scikit-learn__scikit-learn-13779 ▼
Problem
Voting estimator will fail at fit if weights are passed and an estimator is None Because we don't check for an estimator to be `None` in `sample_weight` support, `fit` is failing`. ```python X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True) voter = VotingClassifier( estimators=[('lr', LogisticRegression()), ('rf', RandomForestClassifier())] ) voter.fit(X, y, sa…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: scikit-learn/scikit-learn Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sklearn/ensemble/voting.py b/sklearn/ensemble/voting.py --- a/sklearn/ensemble/voting.py +++ b/sklearn/ensemble/voting.py @@ -78,6 +78,8 @@ def fit(self, X, y, sample_weight=None): if sample_weight is no…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
2/5 Requires basic familiarity with scikit-learn's VotingClassifier and how None estimators are handled, but no advanced ML knowledge.
reasoning_complexity
2/5 The traceback and reproduction script point directly to the offending code path; minimal debugging required.
conceptual_leaps
1/5 No non-obvious leap — the fix is a straightforward null-check guard before the existing attribute check.
plan_length
1/5 Fix requires locating one loop in voting.py and inserting two lines (if step is None: continue).
interdependence
1/5 Single isolated change; no downstream steps depend on each other.
Justification: —
Step graph — 6 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3 The solution is shallow and linear: the fix itself is trivial (one guard clause), but the bottleneck lies in correctly locating the right loop and understanding the None-estimator convention. Verification steps branch in parallel after the single computational edit.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce the reported bug with sample code complexity: 1/5
Run the provided script to confirm AttributeError is raised when fitting a VotingClassifier with sample_weight after setting an estimator to None.
#2 [conceptual] Locate sample_weight validation logic in voting.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Find the fit method in sklearn/ensemble/voting.py and identify the loop that checks has_fit_parameter for sample_weight support.
#3 [conceptual] Understand how None estimators are handled elsewhere complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that VotingClassifier allows estimators to be set to None (to disable them) and that the fitting loop elsewhere already skips None estimators, but the sample_weight check loop does not.
#4 [computational] Add None guard before has_fit_parameter check complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Insert `if step is None: continue` immediately before the has_fit_parameter call inside the sample_weight loop in sklearn/ensemble/voting.py around line 80.
#5 [verification] Re-run reproduction script to confirm fix complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Execute the original failing script and verify no AttributeError is raised when fitting with sample_weight after setting an estimator to None.
#6 [verification] Run existing VotingClassifier test suite complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Execute sklearn's tests for the ensemble/voting module to ensure the patch introduces no regressions in normal (non-None estimator) sample_weight paths.
#28 SWE-bench Verified sphinx Hard (4) nodes: 17 | bottlenecks: 5 swe_sphinx-doc__sphinx-11510 ▼
Problem
source-read event does not modify include'd files source ### Describe the bug In [Yocto documentation](https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs), we use a custom extension to do some search and replace in literal blocks, see https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs/tree/documentation/sphinx/yocto-vars.py. We discovered (https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs/commit/?id=b7375ea4380e716a02c736e42…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: sphinx-doc/sphinx Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sphinx/directives/other.py b/sphinx/directives/other.py --- a/sphinx/directives/other.py +++ b/sphinx/directives/other.py @@ -8,6 +8,7 @@ from docutils.parsers.rst.directives.admonitions import BaseAdmonition from docuti…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires deep familiarity with both Sphinx's extension/event system and docutils internals (StateMachine, directive lifecycle, insert_input), well beyond general Python or web-dev knowledge.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 The solver must trace how BaseInclude delegates to StateMachine.insert_input(), realize that path bypasses the normal source-read emission, and then design an instance-level monkey-patch that does not recurse.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 Three non-obvious insights are needed: (1) included files do trigger source-read as documents but their text is injected via a different code path; (2) the fix requires patching the *instance* method to avoid tail-eating recursion; (3) docutils 0.18+ appends two sentinel lines that must be stripped before emitting the event and reattached afterward.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: reproduce the bug, locate Include in other.py, trace BaseInclude → insert_input, design the patch, handle sentinel lines, add imports, wire the conditional check, and verify the output.
interdependence
4/5 Each step's correctness feeds the next — misunderstanding the sentinel-line convention or choosing a class-level patch instead of an instance-level one invalidates the entire solution.
Justification: —
Step graph — 17 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #5, #6, #12, #14 The solution is dominated by computational steps implementing a tightly scoped monkey-patch, with the key bottlenecks being the correct identification of insert_input as the hook point and understanding docutils' marker-line convention, both of which gate all downstream implementation correctness.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce and confirm bug scope complexity: 2/5
Set up the minimal reproduction case to confirm that source-read fires for included files but their content is not modified in the final HTML output.
#2 [strategic] Decide to intercept Include directive content injection complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Choose the strategy of patching the Include directive's run() method rather than modifying Sphinx's event system, since the problem is that included content bypasses the source-read pipeline.
#3 [conceptual] Identify StateMachine.insert_input as injection point complexity: 4/5 ← #1, #2
Trace how docutils' BaseInclude directive injects included file content into the RST parser via StateMachine.insert_input(), which is called after the file is read but before the source-read event would normally apply.
#4 [conceptual] Locate Include class in sphinx/directives/other.py complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Find the Sphinx-specific Include subclass at sphinx/directives/other.py that wraps docutils' BaseInclude and SphinxDirective, where the patch must be applied.
#5 [conceptual] Plan instance-method monkey-patch approach complexity: 4/5 ← #3
Decide to override self.state_machine.insert_input at the instance level (not class level) so that the patch is scoped to each Include directive invocation and delegates to the class method to avoid recursion.
#6 [conceptual] Understand docutils marker lines structure complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Recognize that docutils 0.18+ appends two sentinel/marker lines to include_lines that must be preserved and excluded when reconstructing text for the source-read event.
#7 [computational] Add StateMachine import from docutils.statemachine complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Add 'from docutils.statemachine import StateMachine' to sphinx/directives/other.py so the class method can be called explicitly to avoid recursive instance dispatch.
#8 [computational] Add os_path import from sphinx.util.osutil complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Add 'from sphinx.util.osutil import os_path' to sphinx/directives/other.py to normalize file paths for docname resolution.
#9 [computational] Define _insert_input inner function skeleton complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #4
Inside Include.run(), define _insert_input(include_lines, path) as a closure that will intercept the StateMachine's content injection for included files.
#10 [computational] Reconstruct text from lines excluding markers complexity: 2/5 ← #9, #6
Inside _insert_input, join include_lines[:-2] with newlines to recover the original RST text, leaving the two docutils marker lines aside.
#11 [computational] Resolve docname from file path complexity: 2/5 ← #9, #8
Call self.env.path2doc(os_path(path)) to convert the included file's filesystem path to its Sphinx docname, needed as the argument to the source-read event.
#12 [computational] Emit source-read event and capture modifications complexity: 2/5 ← #10, #11
Call self.env.app.events.emit('source-read', docname, arg) with arg=[text], then read back arg[0] to get any subscriber-modified text.
#13 [computational] Rebuild include_lines from modified text and markers complexity: 2/5 ← #12, #6
Split the modified text back into lines with text.splitlines() and re-append include_lines[-2:] to restore the docutils sentinel lines.
#14 [computational] Delegate to StateMachine class method explicitly complexity: 3/5 ← #13, #7
Call StateMachine.insert_input(self.state_machine, include_lines, path) — the unbound class method — so the patched instance method does not call itself recursively.
#15 [computational] Conditionally apply patch only when listeners exist complexity: 2/5 ← #9
Guard the instance-method assignment with 'if self.env.app.events.listeners.get("source-read")' to avoid overhead and side effects when no extension listens to source-read.
#16 [verification] Confirm instance vs class method avoids recursion complexity: 3/5 ← #14
Verify that patching self.state_machine.insert_input (instance) and then calling StateMachine.insert_input (class) does not create an infinite call loop.
#17 [verification] Run sphinx-build and check included file output complexity: 2/5 ← #15, #16
Execute sphinx-build and grep the resulting HTML to confirm that &REPLACE_ME; is replaced in content originating from something-to-include.rst, not just the top-level index.rst.
#29 SWE-bench Verified matplotlib Easy (2) nodes: 7 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_matplotlib__matplotlib-22719 ▼
Problem
