[Bug]: Confusing deprecation warning when empty data passed to axis with category units ### Bug summary I'm seeing a `MatplotlibDeprecationWarning` when using calling axes methods on empty data structures for axes that are using string unit converters. I think this is either a false alarm or a non-actionable warning. ### Code for reproduction ```python import matplotlib.pyplot as plt f,…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: matplotlib/matplotlib Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/lib/matplotlib/category.py b/lib/matplotlib/category.py --- a/lib/matplotlib/category.py +++ b/lib/matplotlib/category.py @@ -58,7 +58,7 @@ def convert(value, unit, axis): is_numlike = all(units.Conversion…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with Matplotlib internals — unit converters, category handling, and the deprecation warning system.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the call chain from ax.plot([],[]) through unit conversion into category.py to identify where the empty-array guard is missing.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight: the deprecation warning fires before checking whether the array is empty, so a `values.size` guard must precede the `is_numlike` branch.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 3–5 actions: reproduce the bug, read category.py, identify the two unguarded conditions, apply the two one-line fixes.
interdependence
2/5 The two edits are largely independent of each other; fixing one does not depend on having fixed the other.
Justification: —
Step graph — 7 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3 The solution is diagnosis-heavy: the critical bottleneck is recognizing that Python's `all()` on an empty sequence returns True (vacuous truth), which then propagates the same fix pattern to two call sites. Once the root cause is understood, the code edits are trivial one-liners.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce and characterize the false warning complexity: 1/5
Run the minimal reproduction script to confirm the MatplotlibDeprecationWarning fires when empty lists are passed to an axis that uses category units.
#2 [conceptual] Trace call stack to warning origin complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Follow the traceback through axis.py convert_units → category.py convert() to identify that the deprecation warning is raised inside the category converter.
#3 [conceptual] Identify vacuous-truth cause in convert() complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that `all(...)` over an empty iterable returns True, making `is_numlike=True` for an empty array and incorrectly triggering the deprecation warning.
#4 [computational] Guard warning with values.size check complexity: 1/5 ← #3
In category.py convert(), change `if is_numlike:` to `if values.size and is_numlike:` so the deprecation warning is suppressed when the values array is empty.
#5 [conceptual] Locate analogous issue in update() complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Inspect the StrCategoryConverter.update() method and recognize the same vacuous-truth pattern: `convertible` defaults to True and the info log fires even when data is empty.
#6 [computational] Guard info log with data.size check complexity: 1/5 ← #5
In category.py update(), change `if convertible:` to `if data.size and convertible:` so the misleading log message is suppressed for empty data.
#7 [verification] Verify no warning on empty data complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #6
Re-run the reproduction script and confirm no MatplotlibDeprecationWarning or spurious log is emitted, while also checking that non-empty numeric data still triggers the expected warning.
#30 SWE-bench Verified xarray Medium (3) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_pydata__xarray-6744 ▼
Problem
"center" kwarg ignored when manually iterating over DataArrayRolling ### Discussed in https://github.com/pydata/xarray/discussions/6738 <div type='discussions-op-text'> <sup>Originally posted by **ckingdon95** June 29, 2022</sup> Hello, I am trying to manually iterate over a DataArrayRolling object, as described [here ](https://docs.xarray.dev/en/stable/user-guide/computation.html#rolling-w…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pydata/xarray Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/xarray/core/rolling.py b/xarray/core/rolling.py --- a/xarray/core/rolling.py +++ b/xarray/core/rolling.py @@ -267,16 +267,21 @@ def __init__( # TODO legacy attribute self.window_labels = self.obj[self.dim[0]]…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with xarray's rolling window internals, numpy slicing semantics, and the center-offset calculation pattern.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace how stops/starts are computed in __iter__, understand why center is ignored, and derive the correct offset formula.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious insights needed: (1) recognizing the offset formula `(window + 1) // 2` for centering, and (2) adjusting the starts clamp index accordingly.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 4–5 actions: reproduce the bug, locate __iter__ in rolling.py, derive correct offset math, apply the patch, verify.
interdependence
3/5 The offset derivation must be correct before the starts/stops arrays can be fixed; an error in the formula cascades into both the clamp and the slice.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #7 The solution is dominated by computational edits, but the critical bottleneck is the mid-level insight (nodes 3–4) about how centering should shift the stop-index range; an error there propagates through all window-boundary calculations. The boundary clamp fix (node 7) is the trickiest atomic step.
#1 [strategic] Identify bug: __iter__ ignores center kwarg complexity: 2/5
Recognize that manual iteration over DataArrayRolling yields right-aligned windows regardless of center=True, mismatching the .mean() shortcut.
#2 [conceptual] Locate __iter__ in xarray/core/rolling.py complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Find the DataArrayRolling.__iter__ method (around line 267) where stops/starts are computed without consulting self.center.
#3 [conceptual] Model centered vs right-aligned window slicing complexity: 3/5 ← #1, #2
Understand that right-aligned windows use stops = 1..N and starts = stops - window, while centered windows must shift stops forward by an offset so the label falls in the middle of the window.
#4 [computational] Derive centering offset formula complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Compute offset = (window + 1) // 2 when self.center[0] is True, else offset = 1, mirroring xarray's existing half-window shift convention.
#5 [computational] Recompute stops array with offset complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Replace stops = np.arange(1, len(window_labels)+1) with stops = np.arange(offset, obj.sizes[dim0] + offset) to shift all window end-points forward.
#6 [computational] Recompute starts as stops minus window size complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Replace starts = stops - int(self.window[0]) identically in form but now operating on the shifted stops, so each window still spans window0 elements.
#7 [computational] Fix boundary clamp for leading partial windows complexity: 3/5 ← #4, #6
Replace starts[:int(self.window[0])] = 0 with starts[:window0 - offset] = 0 so only the correct number of leading indices are clamped given the new offset.
#8 [computational] Extract dim0 and window0 as local variables complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Introduce dim0 = self.dim[0] and window0 = int(self.window[0]) to de-duplicate repeated attribute lookups and improve readability throughout __iter__.
#9 [verification] Confirm iterated windows match .mean(center=True) output complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6, #7, #8
Check that [window.mean() for _, window in my_data.rolling(x=3, center=True)] now produces [nan, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, nan], matching the non-iterative path.
#31 SWE-bench Verified scikit-learn Easy (2) nodes: 7 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_scikit-learn__scikit-learn-12585 ▼
Problem
clone fails for parameters that are estimator types #### Description `clone` fails when one or more instance parameters are estimator types (i.e. not instances, but classes). I know this is a somewhat unusual use case, but I'm working on a project that provides wrappers for sklearn estimators (https://github.com/phausamann/sklearn-xarray) and I'd like to store the wrapped estimators as thei…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: scikit-learn/scikit-learn Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sklearn/base.py b/sklearn/base.py --- a/sklearn/base.py +++ b/sklearn/base.py @@ -48,7 +48,7 @@ def clone(estimator, safe=True): # XXX: not handling dictionaries if estimator_type in (list, tuple, set, fr…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires understanding sklearn's clone mechanism, get_params convention, and Python's class-vs-instance distinction.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the traceback into base.py, understand why get_params() fails when called on a type object, and infer the correct guard condition.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight: a class object (type) that happens to have get_params as an unbound method must be excluded from the estimator-cloning branch.
plan_length
2/5 Steps are: reproduce the error, locate the relevant line in base.py, understand the conditional, apply the one-line patch — roughly 3–5 actions.
interdependence
3/5 Understanding the bug correctly is a bottleneck; the fix is a direct consequence and cannot be derived without that understanding.
Justification: —
Step graph — 7 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #5 The solution is a shallow, linear chain dominated by conceptual diagnosis steps; the single computational edit is trivial once the root cause (hasattr returning True for unbound class methods) is correctly understood, making node 3 the critical bottleneck.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce bug and trace call path in clone complexity: 1/5
Run the minimal reproducer to confirm the TypeError and decide to fix it by tracing how `clone` recurses over estimator parameters.
#2 [conceptual] Locate clone function in sklearn/base.py complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Find the `clone` function (around line 48) in `sklearn/base.py` to read its branching logic for handling different parameter types.
#3 [conceptual] Diagnose why class objects trigger TypeError complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that when a parameter is a class (type) rather than an instance, `hasattr(estimator, 'get_params')` returns True (the method exists on the class itself), so `clone` recurses and calls `estimator.get_params(deep=False)` as an unbound method, causing the TypeError.
#4 [conceptual] Identify isinstance(estimator, type) as the correct guard complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Determine that adding `isinstance(estimator, type)` to the existing `not hasattr` condition correctly routes class objects to the `deepcopy` branch without affecting normal estimator instances.
#5 [computational] Edit elif condition in clone to add type check complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Change line 51 of `sklearn/base.py` from `elif not hasattr(estimator, 'get_params'):` to `elif not hasattr(estimator, 'get_params') or isinstance(estimator, type):`, applying the minimal one-line patch.
#6 [verification] Verify fix resolves the reported failing case complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Confirm that `clone(StandardScaler(with_mean=StandardScaler))` now executes without error and that the class parameter is deep-copied correctly.
#7 [verification] Check no regression for normal estimator cloning complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Verify that cloning estimators with standard instance parameters (e.g., `clone(StandardScaler())`) still follows the `get_params` branch and produces correct clones.
#32 SWE-bench Verified django Easy (2) nodes: 12 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_django__django-13212 ▼
Problem
Make validators include the provided value in ValidationError Description It is sometimes desirable to include the provide value in a custom error message. For example: “blah” is not a valid email. By making built-in validators provide value to ValidationError, one can override an error message and use a %(value)s placeholder. This placeholder value matches an example already in the docs: ​https…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: django/django Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/django/core/validators.py b/django/core/validators.py --- a/django/core/validators.py +++ b/django/core/validators.py @@ -48,7 +48,7 @@ def __call__(self, value): regex_matches = self.regex.search(str(value)) …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
2/5 Requires basic Django familiarity: knowing how ValidationError works and that params dict maps to message placeholders.
reasoning_complexity
2/5 Once the mechanism is understood, the fix is a repetitive, mechanical substitution across a single file with no branching logic.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One small insight needed: recognizing that params={'value': value} is the correct kwarg to thread the invalid value into ValidationError.
plan_length
3/5 Requires locating the file, identifying all relevant raise ValidationError sites (7 in the patch), and editing each one.
interdependence
1/5 Each edit site is independent — missing or mis-editing one does not cascade into failures at the others.
Justification: —
Step graph — 12 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #4 The solution is dominated by mechanical, low-complexity computational edits (7 of 12 nodes) that are all parallel once the audit step identifies every raise site. Node 4 (enumerating all raise sites) is the critical bottleneck: missing any site leaves that validator without the fix.
#1 [strategic] Decide to propagate value via ValidationError params complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the fix requires passing `params={'value': value}` to every `ValidationError` raised in built-in validators so callers can use `%(value)s` in custom messages.
#2 [conceptual] Locate django/core/validators.py as target file complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Identify that all built-in validators live in `django/core/validators.py`, which is the sole file requiring changes.
#3 [conceptual] Understand ValidationError params keyword argument complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Confirm that `ValidationError.__init__` accepts a `params` dict whose keys are interpolated into the message string, making `%(value)s` a valid placeholder.
#4 [conceptual] Enumerate all ValidationError raise sites in validators complexity: 3/5 ← #2, #3
Audit `validators.py` to find every `raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)` call across `RegexValidator`, `URLValidator`, and `EmailValidator` that lacks a `params` argument.
#5 [computational] Add params to RegexValidator raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `RegexValidator.__call__`, change `raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)` to include `params={'value': value}`.
#6 [computational] Add params to URLValidator non-string type raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `URLValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` raised when the input is not a string instance.
#7 [computational] Add params to URLValidator invalid scheme raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `URLValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` raised when the URL scheme is not in the allowed set.
#8 [computational] Add params to URLValidator urlsplit ValueError raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `URLValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` raised inside the `except ValueError` block for malformed URLs such as invalid IPv6.
#9 [computational] Add params to URLValidator invalid IPv6 address raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `URLValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` re-raised after `validate_ipv6_address` fails.
#10 [computational] Add params to URLValidator netloc-too-long raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `URLValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` raised when the netloc portion exceeds 253 characters.
#11 [computational] Add params to EmailValidator missing-at-sign raise complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `EmailValidator.__call__`, add `params={'value': value}` to the `ValidationError` raised when the value is empty or lacks an `@` character.
#12 [verification] Confirm all raise sites consistently updated complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #6, #7, #8, #9, #10, #11
Review the full diff to ensure no `raise ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code)` call without `params` remains in the file, and that `params={'value': value}` is uniform across all changed sites.
#33 SWE-bench Verified django Trivial (1) nodes: 5 | bottlenecks: 1 swe_django__django-9296 ▼
Problem
Paginator just implement the __iter__ function Description (last modified by Alex Gaynor) Right now, when you want to iter into all the pages of a Paginator object you to use the page_range function. It would be more logical and naturel to use the normal python of doing that by implementing the iter function like that: def __iter__(self): for page_num in self.page_range: yield self.page…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: django/django Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/django/core/paginator.py b/django/core/paginator.py --- a/django/core/paginator.py +++ b/django/core/paginator.py @@ -34,6 +34,10 @@ def __init__(self, object_list, per_page, orphans=0, self.orphans = int(orphans) …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
2/5 Requires basic Python knowledge (iterators, generators, yield) and familiarity with Django's Paginator API.
reasoning_complexity
1/5 The issue description directly provides the exact implementation to add — no debugging or inference needed.
conceptual_leaps
1/5 No insight required; the solution is spelled out verbatim in the problem statement.
plan_length
1/5 One action: insert the 4-line __iter__ method into the correct location in paginator.py.
interdependence
1/5 Single isolated edit with no cascading dependencies on other steps.
Justification: —
Step graph — 5 nodes | bottlenecks: #3 The solution is a shallow, linear pipeline with minimal complexity; the dominant challenge is correctly identifying the existing page_range and page() APIs as the right building blocks, making node 3 the key bottleneck.
#1 [strategic] Decide to add __iter__ to Paginator class complexity: 1/5
Recognize that the feature request asks for a standard Python iteration protocol on Paginator, achievable by implementing __iter__ using the existing page_range API.
#2 [conceptual] Locate Paginator class in codebase complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Identify that the Paginator class is defined in django/core/paginator.py and find the appropriate insertion point after __init__.
#3 [conceptual] Understand page_range and page() APIs complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Confirm that self.page_range yields valid 1-based page numbers and self.page(page_number) returns the corresponding Page object, making them the correct primitives to use in __iter__.
#4 [computational] Implement __iter__ as a generator method complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Insert the __iter__ method after __init__ that iterates over self.page_range and yields self.page(page_number) for each value.
#5 [verification] Verify iteration yields correct Page objects complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Confirm that calling list() or a for-loop on a Paginator instance produces the same pages as manually calling self.page() for each number in page_range.
#34 SWE-bench Verified seaborn Easy (2) nodes: 6 | bottlenecks: 1 swe_mwaskom__seaborn-3187 ▼
Problem
Wrong legend values of large ranges As of 0.12.1, legends describing large numbers that were created using `ScalarFormatter` with an offset are formatted without their multiplicative offset value. An example: ```python import seaborn as sns import seaborn.objects as so penguins = sns.load_dataset("Penguins") penguins["body_mass_mg"] = penguins["body_mass_g"]*1000 ( so.Plot( p…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: mwaskom/seaborn Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/seaborn/_core/scales.py b/seaborn/_core/scales.py --- a/seaborn/_core/scales.py +++ b/seaborn/_core/scales.py @@ -378,6 +378,14 @@ def spacer(x): axis.set_view_interval(vmin, vmax) locs = axis.major…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with matplotlib's formatter internals (ScalarFormatter, set_useOffset, set_scientific) and seaborn's scale/legend pipeline.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace from visual symptom → formatter offset behavior → two distinct code sites, combining matplotlib API knowledge with seaborn internals.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight: legend generation has no mechanism to display the offset notation, so the formatter must be configured to suppress it — the issue description partially telegraphs this.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 6–8 steps: read issue, locate scales.py and utils.py, understand ScalarFormatter API, make edits in both files, verify fix.
interdependence
2/5 The two file edits are largely independent code paths (new-style Plot API vs. legacy scatterplot), so one can be done without the other, though both are needed for a complete fix.
Justification: —
Step graph — 6 nodes | bottlenecks: #1 The solution is shallow and parallel: after one strategic decision, two independent file locations are identified and patched concurrently, then joined at a single verification step. Computational edits are simple once the correct API calls (set_useOffset, set_scientific) are identified.
#1 [strategic] Decide to suppress formatter offset in legends complexity: 3/5
Determine that the correct fix is to disable offset and scientific notation on the formatter before generating legend tick labels, rather than trying to display the offset somewhere in the legend.
#2 [conceptual] Locate legend label generation in scales.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify the code block in seaborn/_core/scales.py (around line 377-382) where axis tick locations are computed and formatted into legend labels via format_ticks.
#3 [conceptual] Locate ScalarFormatter instantiation in utils.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify the code in seaborn/utils.py where a ScalarFormatter is created for the non-log-scale path and attached to a dummy axis, which is the entry point for the scatterplot legend path.
#4 [computational] Disable offset/scientific on formatter in scales.py complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Insert guarded calls (using hasattr) to set_useOffset(False) and set_scientific(False) on axis.major.formatter before the format_ticks call, so that offset and scientific notation are suppressed for legend labels.
#5 [computational] Disable offset/scientific on ScalarFormatter in utils.py complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Call formatter.set_useOffset(False) and formatter.set_scientific(False) immediately after constructing the ScalarFormatter in utils.py, before it is attached to the dummy axis.
#6 [verification] Verify legend labels correct for large-range values complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #5
Reproduce the original example with body_mass_mg (~1E6 scale) and confirm that legend tick labels now show the full numeric values without a missing offset multiplier, for both so.Plot and scatterplot code paths.
#35 SWE-bench Verified sympy Hard (4) nodes: 12 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_sympy__sympy-20438 ▼
Problem
`is_subset` gives wrong results @sylee957 Current status on `master`, ```python >>> a = FiniteSet(1, 2) >>> b = ProductSet(a, a) >>> c = FiniteSet((1, 1), (1, 2), (2, 1), (2, 2)) >>> b.intersection(c) == c.intersection(b) True >>> b.is_subset(c) >>> c.is_subset(b) True >>> Eq(b, c).simplify() Traceback (most recent call last): File "/usr/lib/python3.6/code.py", line 91, in runcode …
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: sympy/sympy Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sympy/core/relational.py b/sympy/core/relational.py --- a/sympy/core/relational.py +++ b/sympy/core/relational.py @@ -302,9 +302,12 @@ def equals(self, other, failing_expression=False): def _eval_simplify(self, **kwargs):…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
4/5 Requires deep familiarity with SymPy's internal architecture: the multipledispatch system, the set hierarchy (ProductSet, FiniteSet, Complement), and how relational simplification pipelines work.
reasoning_complexity
4/5 The solver must trace two distinct but related bugs across three files — the missing ProductSet→FiniteSet subset dispatch and the unsafe arithmetic attempted on non-Expr set operands in _eval_simplify — and reason about why the generic Set,Set equality handler needed rethinking.
conceptual_leaps
4/5 At least three non-obvious insights are required: (1) `is_subset` silently returns None due to a missing dispatch case, (2) `_eval_simplify` blindly subtracts lhs−rhs without checking that operands are algebraic Exprs, and (3) the FiniteSet,Interval False-shortcut handler is wrong and must be removed.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 8–10 distinct steps: reproduce bugs, locate dispatch tables in issubset.py and comparison.py, trace _eval_simplify in relational.py twice (Relational and Equality), write four separate edits across three files, and verify with tests.
interdependence
3/5 The two bugs are superficially independent, but diagnosing the Eq.simplify crash depends on first understanding that is_subset returns None (Complement appears because equality falls back to lhs−rhs on a set difference), creating a moderate chain of error propagation.
Justification: —
Step graph — 12 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #5 The solution addresses two independent bugs (missing subset dispatch and unsafe arithmetic on Sets) that share a common diagnosis phase; computational edits dominate and fan out in parallel from the file-location bottleneck at node 5.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce bugs and understand failure modes complexity: 2/5
Run the reported code to confirm two distinct failures: `b.is_subset(c)` returns None instead of True, and `Eq(b, c).simplify()` raises AttributeError on Complement.
#2 [conceptual] Identify missing ProductSet/FiniteSet subset dispatch complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Determine that `is_subset` returns None because no multipledispatch handler exists for the (ProductSet, FiniteSet) type pair in issubset.py.
#3 [conceptual] Identify simplify crash from non-Expr Set arithmetic complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Trace the AttributeError to `_eval_simplify` in relational.py, which computes `r.lhs - r.rhs` assuming both sides are `Expr` subclasses, but Set objects are not.
#4 [conceptual] Identify Set/Set equality handler returns None complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Recognize that the fallback `@dispatch(Set, Set)` handler in comparison.py always returns None, preventing equality from being inferred via mutual subset checks.
#5 [conceptual] Locate the three relevant source files complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3, #4
Pin down the three files requiring edits: `sympy/sets/handlers/issubset.py`, `sympy/sets/handlers/comparison.py`, and `sympy/core/relational.py`.
#6 [computational] Add ProductSet/FiniteSet dispatch in issubset.py complexity: 3/5 ← #2, #5
Implement `@dispatch(ProductSet, FiniteSet)` handler that returns `fuzzy_and(b_fs.contains(x) for x in a_ps)`, iterating all elements of the ProductSet.
#7 [computational] Update Set/Set equality handler to use subset checks complexity: 3/5 ← #4, #5
Replace the `return None` body of `@dispatch(Set, Set)` with `return tfn[fuzzy_and(a.is_subset(b) for a, b in [(lhs, rhs), (rhs, lhs)])]` so mutual subset membership implies equality.
#8 [computational] Remove incorrect FiniteSet/Interval equality handler complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #5
Delete the `@dispatch(FiniteSet, Interval)` handler that hard-codes `return False`, since a FiniteSet can equal a degenerate Interval and the general Set/Set handler now handles this correctly.
#9 [computational] Add Expr guard in Relational._eval_simplify complexity: 2/5 ← #3, #5
Insert `if not isinstance(r.lhs, Expr) or not isinstance(r.rhs, Expr): return r` before the `r.lhs - r.rhs` subtraction to short-circuit when operands are non-algebraic (e.g., Sets).
#10 [computational] Add Expr guard in Equality._eval_simplify complexity: 2/5 ← #3, #5
Insert the same `isinstance(e.lhs, Expr) / isinstance(e.rhs, Expr)` check in `Equality._eval_simplify` before the linear coefficient solver path to prevent the same crash for Equality subclass.
#11 [verification] Verify is_subset returns correct True/True complexity: 1/5 ← #6, #7, #8
Confirm that both `b.is_subset(c)` and `c.is_subset(b)` now return True (not None) after the new ProductSet/FiniteSet handler and updated Set equality dispatcher are in place.
#12 [verification] Verify Eq(b, c).simplify() no longer crashes complexity: 1/5 ← #9, #10
Confirm that `Eq(b, c).simplify()` returns a valid result without AttributeError now that the Expr type guards in `_eval_simplify` prevent arithmetic on Set objects.
#36 SWE-bench Verified pylint Easy (2) nodes: 5 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_pylint-dev__pylint-4970 ▼
Problem
Setting `min-similarity-lines` to `0` should stop pylint from checking duplicate code ### Current problem Setting `min-similarity-lines` to `0` in the rcfile doesn't disable checking for duplicate code, it instead treats every line of code as duplicate and raises many errors. ### Desired solution Setting `min-similarity-lines` to `0` should disable the duplicate code check. It works that way…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pylint-dev/pylint Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/pylint/checkers/similar.py b/pylint/checkers/similar.py --- a/pylint/checkers/similar.py +++ b/pylint/checkers/similar.py @@ -390,6 +390,8 @@ def append_stream(self, streamid: str, stream: TextIO, encoding=None) -> None: …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with pylint's internal checker architecture to know where the similarity check entry point lives.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must read the bug report, navigate the codebase to find `similar.py`, trace the `run()` call path, and identify where to insert a guard.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One small insight: rather than changing threshold semantics, use an early-return guard at the `run()` entry point when `min_lines == 0`.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 4–5 actions: clone/navigate repo, locate `pylint/checkers/similar.py`, find `run()`, add 2-line guard, verify.
interdependence
3/5 Steps are moderately sequential — the correct file and method must be identified before the fix can be written, but the fix itself is self-contained.
Justification: —
Step graph — 5 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3 The solution is a shallow, linear chain dominated by conceptual navigation — finding the right class, method, and attribute — followed by a trivially simple one-line code edit. The bottleneck is correctly mapping the config option to the in-memory attribute before writing any code.
#1 [strategic] Frame bug as missing zero-value guard complexity: 2/5
Recognize that the fix requires an early-exit convention (value 0 = disable) rather than a change to similarity computation logic.
#2 [conceptual] Locate duplicate-code checker entry point complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify that `pylint/checkers/similar.py` contains the `Similar` class whose `run()` method drives the duplicate-code check.
#3 [conceptual] Map `min_lines` attribute to `min-similarity-lines` complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Confirm that `self.min_lines` in the `Similar` class corresponds to the `min-similarity-lines` config option, making it the correct predicate for the guard.
#4 [computational] Insert early-return guard in `run()` complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Add `if self.min_lines == 0: return` at the top of `Similar.run()`, before the call to `_display_sims`, so the entire check is skipped when the threshold is zero.
#5 [verification] Verify zero disables check, nonzero still works complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Test that a project with `min-similarity-lines=0` produces no R0801 errors, and that a positive value still detects duplicates as before.
#37 SWE-bench Verified sympy Medium (3) nodes: 10 | bottlenecks: 4 swe_sympy__sympy-16792 ▼
Problem
autowrap with cython backend fails when array arguments do not appear in wrapped expr When using the cython backend for autowrap, it appears that the code is not correctly generated when the function in question has array arguments that do not appear in the final expression. A minimal counterexample is: ```python from sympy.utilities.autowrap import autowrap from sympy import MatrixSymbol im…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: sympy/sympy Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sympy/utilities/codegen.py b/sympy/utilities/codegen.py --- a/sympy/utilities/codegen.py +++ b/sympy/utilities/codegen.py @@ -695,6 +695,11 @@ def routine(self, name, expr, argument_sequence=None, global_vars=None): arg…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with SymPy's codegen internals, Cython wrapping, and how array symbols are represented (IndexedBase, MatrixSymbol).
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the bug from a TypeError through C signature generation back to the codegen routine's argument-sequence handling path.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious insights: (1) unused array arguments fall through to a KeyError catch that creates bare InputArgument without dimensions, and (2) MatrixSymbol needs the same dimensions treatment as Indexed arrays.
plan_length
2/5 Fix requires reading ~50 lines of codegen.py, extracting a small helper, and modifying two code sites — roughly 4–5 meaningful actions.
interdependence
3/5 The helper function must be defined before both usage sites; both edits are needed together since the bug manifests only when argument_sequence is provided with unused array args.
Justification: —
Step graph — 10 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #4, #5, #7 The solution is diagnosis-heavy: most complexity sits in understanding two separate code paths in `routine` before a small but precise fix is applied. The bottleneck is correctly identifying that the `argument_sequence` fallback never propagates dimension metadata for array-typed symbols.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce and understand the reported bug complexity: 2/5
Run the minimal counterexample to confirm the TypeError and inspect the generated C function signature to understand the root cause.
#2 [conceptual] Identify codegen.py as the root cause location complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Trace the incorrect C signature (`double x` instead of `double *x`) back to how `InputArgument` metadata is assigned in the `routine` method of `codegen.py`.
#3 [conceptual] Understand array symbol resolution in `routine` complexity: 4/5 ← #2
Analyze how `array_symbols` is built from `Indexed` atoms in expressions, and recognize that array arguments absent from the expression are never entered into `array_symbols`.
#4 [conceptual] Identify the fallback path for extra argument_sequence symbols complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Locate the loop over `argument_sequence` that appends symbols not in `name_arg_dict`, and see that it calls `InputArgument(symbol)` with no metadata, losing dimension info for array/matrix symbols.
#5 [computational] Extract dimension logic into a reusable `dimensions` helper complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Refactor the inline `dims` construction loop into a local helper function `dimensions(s)` returning `[(S.Zero, dim - 1) for dim in s.shape]` to avoid duplication.
#6 [computational] Replace inline dims loop with helper call for known array symbols complexity: 1/5 ← #5
In the existing `array_symbols` lookup branch, replace the three-line `dims` construction with a single call to `dimensions(array)` for clarity and consistency.
#7 [computational] Add isinstance check and metadata in fallback argument path complexity: 3/5 ← #4, #5
In the `argument_sequence` fallback loop, detect `IndexedBase` or `MatrixSymbol` symbols and pass `dimensions(symbol)` as metadata to `InputArgument`, so absent array args get correct pointer-type signatures.
#8 [verification] Test with original failing counterexample complexity: 1/5 ← #6, #7
Run `autowrap(1.0, args=(x,), backend='cython')` with a `MatrixSymbol` arg and confirm it returns `1.0` without TypeError.
#9 [verification] Test with expression-dependent array argument complexity: 1/5 ← #6, #7
Run the variant where `expr = x[0,0]` to confirm the previously-working case is not regressed by the refactor.
#10 [verification] Verify generated C signature uses pointer type complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Inspect the generated C code to confirm the argument is now `double *x` rather than `double x` for array/matrix symbols.
#38 SWE-bench Verified xarray Trivial (1) nodes: 6 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_pydata__xarray-4629 ▼
Problem
merge(combine_attrs='override') does not copy attrs but instead references attrs from the first object <!-- Please include a self-contained copy-pastable example that generates the issue if possible. Please be concise with code posted. See guidelines below on how to provide a good bug report: - Craft Minimal Bug Reports: http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2018/02/28/minimal-bug-reports - M…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pydata/xarray Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/xarray/core/merge.py b/xarray/core/merge.py --- a/xarray/core/merge.py +++ b/xarray/core/merge.py @@ -501,7 +501,7 @@ def merge_attrs(variable_attrs, combine_attrs): if combine_attrs == "drop": return {} eli…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
2/5 Requires basic Python knowledge (reference vs copy semantics) and minimal xarray familiarity.
reasoning_complexity
2/5 Bug report already diagnoses the root cause and points to the exact line; solver just needs to verify and apply.
conceptual_leaps
1/5 No non-obvious jump needed — the fix (dict() copy) is explicitly stated in the bug report.
plan_length
1/5 Single one-character edit: change `return variable_attrs[0]` to `return dict(variable_attrs[0])`.
interdependence
1/5 Single isolated change with no downstream coupling to other steps.
Justification: —
Step graph — 6 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #4 The solution is shallow and nearly linear: correctly diagnosing reference aliasing (node 2) is the key insight that unlocks the trivial one-line fix (node 4). Verification steps branch independently after the fix.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce the bug with minimal example complexity: 1/5
Run the provided MCVE to confirm that mutating xds3.attrs also mutates xds1.attrs, establishing the bug is real.
#2 [conceptual] Identify root cause as reference aliasing complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that returning variable_attrs[0] directly yields a reference to the original dict rather than a copy, causing shared mutation.
#3 [conceptual] Locate merge_attrs function in merge.py complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Find the merge_attrs function in xarray/core/merge.py, specifically the combine_attrs == 'override' branch at line ~504.
#4 [computational] Change return to return dict(variable_attrs[0]) complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Edit line 504 in xarray/core/merge.py from `return variable_attrs[0]` to `return dict(variable_attrs[0])` to return a shallow copy instead of a reference.
#5 [verification] Re-run MCVE to confirm fix complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Execute the original minimal example again and verify that mutating xds3.attrs no longer affects xds1.attrs.
#6 [verification] Check other combine_attrs branches for consistency complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Confirm that all other combine_attrs cases (drop, no_conflicts, identical) already return new dicts, ensuring the fix is consistent with the existing pattern.
#39 SWE-bench Verified astropy Medium (3) nodes: 10 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_astropy__astropy-7606 ▼
Problem
Unit equality comparison with None raises TypeError for UnrecognizedUnit ``` In [12]: x = u.Unit('asdf', parse_strict='silent') In [13]: x == None # Should be False --------------------------------------------------------------------------- TypeError Traceback (most recent call last) <ipython-input-13-2486f2ccf928> in <module>() ----> 1 x == None # Should …
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: astropy/astropy Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/astropy/units/core.py b/astropy/units/core.py --- a/astropy/units/core.py +++ b/astropy/units/core.py @@ -728,7 +728,7 @@ def __eq__(self, other): try: other = Unit(other, parse_strict='silent') …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid understanding of Python's rich comparison protocol, specifically when __eq__ should return NotImplemented vs False — an intermediate but non-obvious Python nuance.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the exception through two call sites, understand the semantic difference between NotImplemented and False in comparison methods, and identify that both UnitBase.__eq__ and UnrecognizedUnit.__eq__ need fixes.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious insights required: (1) returning NotImplemented rather than False is the correct protocol to let Python fall back to the reflected operation; (2) a second, subtler fix is needed — isinstance(other, UnrecognizedUnit) should be isinstance(other, type(self)) for correctness.
plan_length
2/5 Around 4–5 discrete actions: read the traceback, locate both __eq__ methods in core.py, apply the try/except wrapping with NotImplemented returns, and fix the isinstance check.
interdependence
2/5 The two hunks are largely independent patches; missing one leaves a partial fix but does not break the other.
Justification: —
Step graph — 10 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4 The solution hinges on a conceptual insight (nodes 3–4): returning NotImplemented rather than False is the semantically correct response when __eq__ cannot interpret its argument. Once that is understood, the computational edits are straightforward two-line changes in two locations.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce bug and confirm failure mode complexity: 1/5
Create an UnrecognizedUnit via parse_strict='silent' and compare with None to confirm TypeError is raised instead of returning False.
#2 [conceptual] Locate both __eq__ methods in core.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify that both UnitBase.__eq__ (~line 728) and UnrecognizedUnit.__eq__ (~line 1710) are relevant and must be changed.
#3 [conceptual] Understand Python's NotImplemented comparison protocol complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Recognize that returning NotImplemented (not False) from __eq__ tells Python to try the reflected operation, which ultimately yields False for None comparisons without raising TypeError.
#4 [conceptual] Determine that both sites need NotImplemented fix complexity: 3/5 ← #2, #3
Conclude that both UnitBase.__eq__ (already catches exceptions but returns False) and UnrecognizedUnit.__eq__ (no exception handling at all) must be updated to return NotImplemented on TypeError/ValueError/UnitsError.
#5 [computational] Fix UnitBase.__eq__: return NotImplemented instead of False complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In the except clause at ~line 731, change `return False` to `return NotImplemented` so invalid comparisons defer to Python's default equality fallback.
#6 [computational] Wrap Unit() call in UnrecognizedUnit.__eq__ with try/except complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Surround `other = Unit(other, parse_strict='silent')` with a try/except catching ValueError, UnitsError, and TypeError to prevent the None TypeError from propagating.
#7 [computational] Return NotImplemented in UnrecognizedUnit.__eq__ except handler complexity: 1/5 ← #6
In the newly added except block, return NotImplemented so Python can apply its default comparison fallback (yielding False when comparing with None).
#8 [computational] Fix isinstance check to use type(self) instead of UnrecognizedUnit complexity: 2/5 ← #6
Change `isinstance(other, UnrecognizedUnit)` to `isinstance(other, type(self))` to correctly handle potential subclasses and make the equality check more precise.
#9 [verification] Verify x == None returns False without TypeError complexity: 1/5 ← #5, #7, #8
Confirm that after both patches, comparing an UnrecognizedUnit with None returns False and that UnitBase comparisons with None also return False via the NotImplemented fallback.
#10 [verification] Verify __ne__ and symmetric equality still work complexity: 2/5 ← #9
Check that x != None returns True, x == x returns True, and x == y (same name) returns True, ensuring the NotImplemented change did not break normal equality semantics.
#40 SWE-bench Verified matplotlib Easy (2) nodes: 7 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_matplotlib__matplotlib-24627 ▼
Problem
cla(), clf() should unset the `.axes` and `.figure` attributes of deparented artists mpl2.0b3: Removing an artist from its axes unsets its `.axes` attribute, but clearing the axes does not do so. ``` In [11]: f, a = plt.subplots(); l, = a.plot([1, 2]); l.remove(); print(l.axes) None In [12]: f, a = plt.subplots(); l, = a.plot([1, 2]); a.cla(); print(l.axes) Axes(0.125,0.11;0.775x0.77) ```
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: matplotlib/matplotlib Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py b/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py --- a/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py +++ b/lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py @@ -1315,7 +1315,9 @@ def __clear(self): self._get_patches_for_fill = _process_…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with matplotlib's artist/axes ownership model and internal `_children` list management.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Must trace the inconsistency between `remove()` (which nulls back-references) and `__clear()` (which doesn't), then adapt the fix accordingly.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight: reassigning `self._children = []` orphans the list without notifying the artists, so their back-references go stale.
plan_length
2/5 About 4–5 steps: reproduce the bug, locate `__clear` in `_base.py`, understand the current reset, write the cleanup loop, verify.
interdependence
3/5 Locating the correct method is a prerequisite for the fix; a wrong location search would waste effort but is recoverable.
Justification: —
Step graph — 7 nodes | bottlenecks: #4, #5 The solution is a shallow, linear bug-fix chain dominated by conceptual steps that locate the deficient code path; the single computational bottleneck is capturing `old_children` before the list is replaced, without which the null-out loop is impossible.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce and frame the inconsistency complexity: 1/5
Confirm that `artist.remove()` correctly nullifies `.axes` but `cla()` does not, establishing the expected invariant to enforce.
#2 [conceptual] Identify the invariant: departed artists must lose parent refs complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that any code path that removes artists from axes must null out `.axes` and `.figure` to maintain a consistent object graph.
#3 [conceptual] Trace `cla()` execution to `__clear` in `_base.py` complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Follow the call chain from `Axes.cla()` into the private `__clear` method in `lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py` to find where `_children` is reset.
#4 [conceptual] Understand `_children` list replacement pattern complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Observe that `__clear` replaces `self._children` with a new empty list, discarding all prior artist references without touching their back-pointers.
#5 [computational] Capture old children before list reset complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Replace `self._children = []` with `old_children, self._children = self._children, []` so the deparented artists are accessible for cleanup.
#6 [computational] Null out `.axes` and `.figure` on each old child complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #5
Iterate `old_children` and set `chld.axes = chld.figure = None` to mirror the cleanup already performed by `Artist.remove()`.
#7 [verification] Verify `cla()` output matches `remove()` behavior complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Re-run both original REPL snippets to confirm that `l.axes` is `None` after `a.cla()`, consistent with the behavior after `l.remove()`.
#41 SWE-bench Verified astropy Easy (2) nodes: 11 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_astropy__astropy-8707 ▼
Problem
Header.fromstring does not accept Python 3 bytes According to [the docs](http://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/_modules/astropy/io/fits/header.html#Header.fromstring), the method `Header.fromstring` "...creates an HDU header from a byte string containing the entire header data." By "byte string" here it really means the `str` type which on Python 2 could be raw binary data, but on Python 3 explicit…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: astropy/astropy Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/astropy/io/fits/card.py b/astropy/io/fits/card.py --- a/astropy/io/fits/card.py +++ b/astropy/io/fits/card.py @@ -554,6 +554,13 @@ def fromstring(cls, image): """ card = cls() + if isinstance(image…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid understanding of Python 2/3 str/bytes semantics and basic familiarity with the FITS file format standard.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must read existing code, diagnose the str/bytes mismatch, choose an appropriate encoding (latin-1 vs. ASCII/UTF-8), and propagate the fix to two related classes.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 The one non-obvious insight is why latin-1 is the correct decoding choice — it is byte-transparent, preserving all 256 byte values without raising decode errors, unlike ASCII or UTF-8.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 discrete actions: locate both files, read Card.fromstring and Header.fromstring, add bytes-decoding guards to each, update docstrings/examples, and adjust __doctest_skip__.
interdependence
2/5 The card.py and header.py edits are mostly independent; the docstring/example additions are also loosely coupled to the functional fix.
Justification: —
Step graph — 11 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #3 The solution has two parallel fix tracks (Card and Header) that both depend on the shared latin-1 codec decision (node 3); the Header track is longer because it also requires documentation and doctest updates. Computational steps dominate but the critical intellectual work is concentrated in the codec choice.
#1 [strategic] Extend both fromstring methods to accept bytes complexity: 2/5
Decide to modify Card.fromstring and Header.fromstring to handle both str and bytes inputs rather than adding new methods, keeping the fix minimal and backward-compatible.
#2 [conceptual] Understand Python 2/3 str vs bytes split complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Recognize that Python 2's str was a byte string, so legacy code passing raw bytes now breaks on Python 3 where str is unicode; both types must be accepted.
#3 [conceptual] Choose latin-1 as the decode codec for FITS bytes complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Select latin-1 (ISO-8859-1) as the decode scheme because FITS only allows ASCII, but latin-1 is a superset that maps every byte value 0–255 to the same code point, preserving malformed data without loss.
#4 [conceptual] Locate Card.fromstring in card.py complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Find the Card.fromstring classmethod in astropy/io/fits/card.py and identify the entry point where the raw image string is first consumed, before _pad is called.
#5 [computational] Add bytes guard and latin-1 decode to Card.fromstring complexity: 1/5 ← #3, #4
Insert an isinstance(image, bytes) check immediately after card = cls() in Card.fromstring; if True, decode image via image.decode('latin1') before assigning to card._image.
#6 [conceptual] Locate Header.fromstring in header.py complexity: 1/5 ← #1
Find the Header.fromstring classmethod in astropy/io/fits/header.py and identify where the raw data string is consumed before card-level parsing begins.
#7 [computational] Add bytes guard and latin-1 decode to Header.fromstring complexity: 1/5 ← #3, #6
Insert an isinstance(data, bytes) check at the top of the Header.fromstring body; if True, decode data via data.decode('latin1') so downstream card-splitting logic always sees a str.
#8 [computational] Update Header.fromstring docstring parameter section complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Change the data parameter type annotation from 'str' to 'str or bytes' and add a prose explanation of why latin-1 is used and what it means for malformed FITS files.
#9 [computational] Add doctest examples to Header.fromstring docstring complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Write two doctest blocks: one round-tripping a Header through tostring/fromstring and one showing human-readable newline-separated input with sep='\n', to validate and document both usage patterns.
#10 [computational] Narrow __doctest_skip__ to exclude new examples complexity: 2/5 ← #9
Replace the broad 'Header.*' entry in __doctest_skip__ with specific method names (Header.comments, Header.fromtextfile, Header.totextfile, Header.set, Header.update) so the new Header.fromstring doctests are actually executed by the test runner.
#11 [verification] Confirm both methods accept str and bytes inputs complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #7, #10
Run the new doctests and any existing regression tests to verify that passing either a unicode str or a bytes object to Card.fromstring and Header.fromstring produces identical, correct results.
#42 SWE-bench Verified pylint Medium (3) nodes: 9 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_pylint-dev__pylint-8898 ▼
Problem
bad-names-rgxs mangles regular expressions with commas ### Bug description Since pylint splits on commas in this option, instead of taking a list of strings, if there are any commas in the regular expression, the result is mangled before being parsed. The config below demonstrates this clearly by causing pylint to crash immediately. ### Configuration ```ini [tool.pylint.basic] # capture…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pylint-dev/pylint Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/pylint/config/argument.py b/pylint/config/argument.py --- a/pylint/config/argument.py +++ b/pylint/config/argument.py @@ -114,7 +114,7 @@ def _regex_transformer(value: str) -> Pattern[str]: def _regexp_csv_transfomer(valu…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with pylint's config/argument parsing pipeline, Python regex syntax, and how CSV-style option transformers work internally.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace a crash traceback across several abstraction layers (config_initialization → arguments_manager → argparse → transformer) to pinpoint the naive comma-split as root cause.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious insights needed: (1) the comma in `{1,3}` quantifiers collides with CSV splitting, and (2) the fix must track brace-open state rather than using a simple string split.
plan_length
3/5 Approximately 7–8 distinct steps: reproduce crash, trace call stack, locate _regexp_csv_transfomer, write brace-aware _check_regexp_csv, update the transformer call, re-export from __init__.py, verify tests pass.
interdependence
4/5 Steps are tightly sequential: the new helper must be written before the transformer can reference it, the export must be added before the import resolves, and each layer depends on the previous being correct.
Justification: —
Step graph — 9 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #6 The solution is a linear pipeline: diagnosis leads to a design decision (brace-aware splitting), which drives four implementation edits across three files. Computational steps dominate but the true leverage point is the conceptual design of the algorithm (node 4), since a flawed split strategy would propagate incorrectly through all downstream code.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce crash and confirm root cause complexity: 2/5
Run pylint with a config containing a regex quantifier like `{1,3}` to confirm the crash and establish that naive comma-splitting is to blame.
#2 [conceptual] Trace crash to _regexp_csv_transfomer call site complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Follow the traceback through config_initialization → arguments_manager into `_regexp_csv_transfomer` in `pylint/config/argument.py`, which delegates to `_csv_transformer` for splitting.
#3 [conceptual] Identify naive comma-split as the defect complexity: 2/5 ← #2
Recognize that `_csv_transformer` splits on every comma, so a pattern like `(foo{1,3})` is broken into `(foo{1` and `3})`, producing invalid regexps downstream.
#4 [conceptual] Design brace-aware CSV split algorithm complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Decide that commas inside `{…}` quantifier braces must be skipped, and plan a single-pass character scanner that tracks whether the cursor is inside open braces.
#5 [computational] Add deque and Iterable imports to utils.py complexity: 1/5 ← #4
In `pylint/utils/utils.py`, add `from collections import deque` and extend the `collections.abc` import to include `Iterable`, since the new function uses both.
#6 [computational] Implement _check_regexp_csv in utils.py complexity: 4/5 ← #4, #5
Write the new generator function that passes through list/tuple values unchanged and for strings iterates character-by-character, tracking `open_braces`, splitting only on commas outside braces, and yielding stripped pattern strings.
#7 [computational] Export _check_regexp_csv from pylint/utils/__init__.py complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Add `_check_regexp_csv` to both the import line and the `__all__` list in `pylint/utils/__init__.py` so the symbol is reachable as `pylint_utils._check_regexp_csv`.
#8 [computational] Replace _csv_transformer with _check_regexp_csv in argument.py complexity: 1/5 ← #7
In `_regexp_csv_transfomer` in `pylint/config/argument.py`, change the loop to iterate over `pylint_utils._check_regexp_csv(value)` instead of `_csv_transformer(value)`.
#9 [verification] Verify fix with comma-quantifier regex config complexity: 2/5 ← #8
Re-run pylint with `bad-name-rgxs = "(foo{1,3})"` to confirm no crash and that the pattern is parsed as a single valid regexp; also check that plain comma-separated patterns still split correctly.
#43 SWE-bench Verified sphinx Medium (3) nodes: 8 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_sphinx-doc__sphinx-8120 ▼
Problem
locale/<language>/LC_MESSAGES/sphinx.po translation ignored **Describe the bug** I read [1] as it should be possible to add a file ``locale/<language>/LC_MESSAGES/sphinx.mo`` to the source dir (same dir as the ``Makefile``) and through that change translations or add additional translation to <language>. When I add ``locale/da/LC_MESSAGES/sphinx.po``, with updated entries for ``Fig. %s`` and …
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: sphinx-doc/sphinx Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/sphinx/application.py b/sphinx/application.py --- a/sphinx/application.py +++ b/sphinx/application.py @@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ from collections import deque from io import StringIO from os import path -from typing import Any, …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with Python, GNU gettext/i18n conventions, and how Sphinx initializes its translation subsystem.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the locale-loading path across two files, infer that list ordering determines lookup priority, and connect that to the user-reported symptom.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One core insight: the built-in package locale dir was placed before user-supplied dirs, so it always won — reordering the list fixes the priority.
plan_length
3/5 Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: reproduce, locate _init_i18n, read locale/__init__.py, confirm priority logic, edit application.py, update type annotation in locale/__init__.py, verify.
interdependence
3/5 Understanding the locale-loading order is a bottleneck; both file edits depend on that insight, and the type-annotation change is a minor follow-on that must stay consistent.
Justification: —
Step graph — 8 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #4, #5 The solution is shallow in strategic reasoning but precise in locating and reordering a single list construction; the dominant steps are conceptual (understanding search-order semantics) and computational (the targeted edits), with the reorder step (node 5) being the critical bottleneck.
#1 [strategic] Frame bug as locale search-order priority issue complexity: 3/5
Recognize that user-supplied translations are silently overridden because the built-in package locale directory is searched before the project's locale directories.
#2 [conceptual] Locate `_init_i18n` in `sphinx/application.py` complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify `_init_i18n` as the method that assembles `locale_dirs` and calls `locale.init()`, making it the site of the ordering defect.
#3 [conceptual] Understand `locale.init()` uses first-match-wins ordering complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Determine that `locale.init()` iterates `locale_dirs` in order and returns the first catalog found, so list position directly controls translation priority.
#4 [computational] Read `locale_dirs` construction at `application.py:293` complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3
Inspect the existing line `locale_dirs = [None, path.join(package_dir, 'locale')] + list(repo.locale_dirs)` to confirm package locale precedes repo locale dirs.
#5 [computational] Reorder `locale_dirs`: repo dirs before package dir complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Replace the single-line construction with three statements so the order becomes `[None] + repo.locale_dirs + [package_dir/locale]`, giving user-provided dirs higher priority.
#6 [conceptual] Recognize `None` entry requires `Optional[str]` type complexity: 2/5 ← #5
After restructuring, `locale_dirs` is explicitly typed as `List[Optional[str]]` (since `None` is a valid sentinel), requiring a matching update to the `locale.init()` signature.
#7 [computational] Update `init()` signature in `sphinx/locale/__init__.py` complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Change the `locale_dirs` parameter annotation from `List[str]` to `List[Optional[str]]` and add `Optional` to the import in `sphinx/locale/__init__.py` to maintain type correctness.
#8 [verification] Verify user locale now overrides package translations complexity: 2/5 ← #5, #7
Confirm that after the reorder, the user's `locale/da/LC_MESSAGES/sphinx.mo` is found first by `locale.init()` and produces the expected custom caption labels.
#44 SWE-bench Verified pytest Medium (3) nodes: 7 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_pytest-dev__pytest-7521 ▼
Problem
pytest 6.0.0rc1: capfd.readouterr() converts \r to \n I am testing pytest 6.0.0rc1 with Fedora packages. This is the first failure I get, from borgbackup 1.1.13. ``` ______________________ test_progress_percentage_sameline _______________________ capfd = <_pytest.capture.CaptureFixture object at 0x7f9bd55e4d00> monkeypatch = <_pytest.monkeypatch.MonkeyPatch object at 0x7f9bcbbced60> …
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pytest-dev/pytest Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/src/_pytest/capture.py b/src/_pytest/capture.py --- a/src/_pytest/capture.py +++ b/src/_pytest/capture.py @@ -388,6 +388,7 @@ def __init__(self, targetfd: int) -> None: TemporaryFile(buffering=0), # type:…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires familiarity with Python I/O, text encoding, newline translation, and pytest's capture internals.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace from a regression symptom ( → conversion) through pytest's capture layer to a specific TextIOWrapper constructor argument.
conceptual_leaps
3/5 Two non-obvious jumps: (1) recognizing newline translation is the culprit, and (2) knowing that newline='' disables it in TextIOWrapper.
plan_length
2/5 Steps are: reproduce bug, locate capture.py, identify TextIOWrapper construction, add newline='' — roughly 4–5 actions.
interdependence
4/5 Each step depends on the prior: reproducing pinpoints the layer, locating the file enables inspection, understanding TextIOWrapper semantics leads to the fix.
Justification: —
Step graph — 7 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #5 The solution is a shallow, linear chain dominated by conceptual steps: the hard part is tracing the symptom to Python's TextIOWrapper universal-newline behavior and knowing the newline='' parameter disables it; the actual code change is a single-line insertion.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce bug and confirm regression in pytest 6 complexity: 2/5
Establish a minimal reproducer to confirm that capfd.readouterr() silently converts \r to \n only in pytest 6, not pytest 5, isolating it as a regression.
#2 [conceptual] Locate capture machinery changed in pytest 6 complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify src/_pytest/capture.py as the file responsible for fd-level capture, which was modified between pytest 5 and 6 and is the likely source of the regression.
#3 [conceptual] Trace \r→\n conversion to TextIOWrapper universal newlines complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that Python's TextIOWrapper, when wrapping the temporary file used for capture, applies universal newlines translation by default, converting \r to \n on read.
#4 [computational] Locate TextIOWrapper instantiation in capture.py ~line 388 complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Find the specific TextIOWrapper constructor call in EncodedFile.__init__ (around line 388) where the temporary capture file is wrapped, confirming it lacks a newline argument.
#5 [conceptual] Identify newline="" as fix to disable newline translation complexity: 3/5 ← #3
Recognize that passing newline="" to TextIOWrapper disables universal newline translation, preserving raw \r bytes in the captured output as required.
#6 [computational] Add newline="" to TextIOWrapper constructor call complexity: 1/5 ← #4, #5
Insert the newline="" keyword argument into the TextIOWrapper instantiation at src/_pytest/capture.py line ~391, between errors="replace" and write_through=True.
#7 [verification] Run reproducer test to confirm \r is preserved complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Execute the minimal reproducer test (assert out.endswith('\r')) and the borgbackup test_progress_percentage_sameline to confirm the fix restores correct carriage-return behavior.
#45 SWE-bench Verified requests Easy (2) nodes: 8 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_psf__requests-1724 ▼
Problem
Unicode method names cause UnicodeDecodeError for some requests in Python 2.7.2 The following example works fine: ``` files = {u'file': open(u'/usr/bin/diff', u'rb')} response = requests.request(method='POST', url=u'http://httpbin.org/post', files=files) ``` But the following example (using `method=u'POST'` instead of `method='POST'`) produces a UnicodeDecodeError: ``` files = {u'file': open(u'…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: psf/requests Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/requests/sessions.py b/requests/sessions.py --- a/requests/sessions.py +++ b/requests/sessions.py @@ -12,7 +12,7 @@ from collections import Mapping from datetime import datetime -from .compat import cookielib, OrderedDict, …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires knowledge of Python 2/3 unicode handling, httplib internals, and the requests library's compat module.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the error from user-facing API through sessions, adapters, urllib3, and into stdlib httplib to identify where the unicode string causes a failure.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One key insight: the fix must happen early in the call stack (at the session.request entry point) by coercing the unicode method to a native str before it propagates.
plan_length
2/5 Solution requires 3–4 actions: reproduce/read the traceback, identify the entry point, locate the compat utility, insert one line.
interdependence
3/5 Each step depends on the previous — correctly reading the traceback informs which layer to patch, and knowing the compat module exists is required to write the correct fix.
Justification: —
Step graph — 8 nodes | bottlenecks: #3, #4, #5 The solution is a shallow, linear chain: the dominant cognitive work is conceptual (understanding the Python 2 str/unicode boundary and locating the right abstraction), while the actual code change is trivially small (two lines). The bottleneck lies in correctly diagnosing that httplib is the failure point and choosing the session entry point as the normalization site.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce bug and examine full traceback complexity: 1/5
Run the failing example with method=u'POST' to obtain the full traceback and determine where in the call stack the error originates.
#2 [conceptual] Identify httplib as the failure point complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Read the traceback to see that the UnicodeDecodeError surfaces inside Python 2's httplib when it tries to encode the unicode method string into a byte-string HTTP request line.
#3 [conceptual] Understand Python 2 str/unicode mismatch in httplib complexity: 3/5 ← #2
Recognize that Python 2's httplib.request expects a native byte str for the HTTP method; passing a unicode object causes implicit ASCII encoding which fails when combined with binary body data.
#4 [strategic] Decide to normalize method at session entry point complexity: 2/5 ← #3
Choose to coerce the method argument to a native str as early as possible—at the top of sessions.Session.request—so all downstream code (models, adapters, urllib3, httplib) receives a safe byte string.
#5 [conceptual] Locate builtin_str utility in compat module complexity: 2/5 ← #4
Find that requests.compat already exposes builtin_str, which maps to Python 2's str (byte string) and Python 3's str (unicode), making it the correct cross-version coercion function.
#6 [computational] Add builtin_str to compat import in sessions.py complexity: 1/5 ← #5
Edit the existing import line in sessions.py to add builtin_str alongside the other names imported from .compat.
#7 [computational] Insert method = builtin_str(method) conversion complexity: 1/5 ← #6
Add the single line `method = builtin_str(method)` at the start of the sessions.Session.request body, before the Request object is constructed, to convert any unicode input to a native str.
#8 [verification] Confirm fix resolves both str and unicode inputs complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Re-run both the working (method='POST') and previously failing (method=u'POST') examples to confirm no UnicodeDecodeError occurs and that the coercion is a no-op for already-byte-str inputs.
#46 SWE-bench Verified seaborn Medium (3) nodes: 11 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_mwaskom__seaborn-3069 ▼
Problem
Nominal scale should be drawn the same way as categorical scales Three distinctive things happen on the categorical axis in seaborn's categorical plots: 1. The scale is drawn to +/- 0.5 from the first and last tick, rather than using the normal margin logic 2. A grid is not shown, even when it otherwise would be with the active style 3. If on the y axis, the axis is inverted It probably ma…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: mwaskom/seaborn Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/seaborn/_core/plot.py b/seaborn/_core/plot.py --- a/seaborn/_core/plot.py +++ b/seaborn/_core/plot.py @@ -25,7 +25,7 @@ from seaborn._stats.base import Stat from seaborn._core.data import PlotData from seaborn._core.moves…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid familiarity with seaborn's objects API internals, matplotlib axis/scale machinery, and the `_finalize_figure` lifecycle.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace the codebase to locate the right insertion point, understand how `self._scales` maps axis keys to Scale objects, and wire three distinct behaviors correctly.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 The issue itself names `_finalize_figure` as the target and describes all three behaviors, leaving only one non-trivial jump: realizing the y-axis inversion is achieved by swapping lo/hi rather than calling `invert_yaxis`.
plan_length
3/5 ~8 meaningful actions: read plot.py, find existing axis-limit block, understand scale lookup pattern, move the Nominal import to the top-level, remove the inner import, add grid-off call, add limit-setting logic with y-inversion swap, verify no test regressions.
interdependence
3/5 The import refactor and the new `_finalize_figure` block are loosely coupled to each other, but within the new block the three behaviors share the same `isinstance` guard and axis-key lookup, so a mistake in the condition propagates to all three.
Justification: —
Step graph — 11 nodes | bottlenecks: #1, #4, #7 The solution is dominated by low-complexity computational edits once the strategic decision and import restructuring are settled; the critical bottlenecks are the strategic framing, the module-level import promotion (which unlocks isinstance checks), and the isinstance guard block that gates all three behavioral changes.
#1 [strategic] Decide to mirror categorical axis behavior for Nominal scales complexity: 3/5
Frame the solution as: detect Nominal scales in _finalize_figure and apply the three categorical axis behaviors (limits, grid suppression, y-inversion) there.
#2 [conceptual] Identify _finalize_figure as correct implementation site complexity: 3/5 ← #1
Determine that _finalize_figure is the right hook because it runs after all scales and ticks are resolved, making tick count and scale type both available.
#3 [conceptual] Recognize Nominal must be module-level import complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Identify that the existing Nominal import is local to _setup_scales and must be promoted to module level so _finalize_figure can use isinstance checks against it.
#4 [computational] Move Nominal import to top of plot.py complexity: 1/5 ← #3
Edit the import line at the top of plot.py to add Nominal alongside Scale so it is accessible throughout the module.
#5 [computational] Remove redundant local Nominal import from _setup_scales complexity: 1/5 ← #4
Delete the now-redundant `from seaborn._core.scales import Nominal` line inside _setup_scales to avoid shadowing and keep code clean.
#6 [computational] Add axis_obj variable in _finalize_figure loop complexity: 1/5 ← #2
Introduce `axis_obj = getattr(ax, f"{axis}axis")` inside the per-axis loop so subsequent code can call axis-level methods (grid, get_major_ticks) without repetition.
#7 [computational] Add isinstance guard block for Nominal scales complexity: 2/5 ← #4, #6
Write `if isinstance(self._scales.get(axis_key), Nominal):` to gate all three categorical behaviors so they only apply when the scale is Nominal.
#8 [computational] Suppress grid for Nominal axes complexity: 1/5 ← #7
Call `axis_obj.grid(False, which="both")` inside the Nominal guard to disable both major and minor grid lines, matching categorical plot behavior.
#9 [computational] Compute and set ±0.5 tick-based axis limits complexity: 2/5 ← #7
Count ticks with `len(axis_obj.get_major_ticks())`, derive lo=-.5, hi=nticks-.5, and call set_xlim/set_ylim with auto=None only when the user has not set explicit limits.
#10 [computational] Invert y-axis by swapping lo and hi complexity: 2/5 ← #9
For axis=="y", swap lo and hi before calling set_lim so that the y-axis is inverted, matching seaborn's legacy categorical y-axis orientation.
#11 [verification] Verify all three Nominal axis behaviors render correctly complexity: 2/5 ← #8, #10
Confirm that a Nominal-scaled axis shows no grid, limits at ±0.5 from the outermost ticks, and that a y-axis Nominal scale is inverted — matching legacy categorical plot output.
#47 SWE-bench Verified pytest Easy (2) nodes: 11 | bottlenecks: 3 swe_pytest-dev__pytest-8399 ▼
Problem
Starting v6.2.0, unittest setUpClass fixtures are no longer "private" <!-- Thanks for submitting an issue! Quick check-list while reporting bugs: --> Minimal example: ``` import unittest class Tests(unittest.TestCase): @classmethod def setUpClass(cls): pass def test_1(self): pass ``` ``` ~$ pytest --fixtures ... unittest_setUpClass_fixture_Tests …
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: pytest-dev/pytest Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/src/_pytest/python.py b/src/_pytest/python.py --- a/src/_pytest/python.py +++ b/src/_pytest/python.py @@ -528,7 +528,7 @@ def _inject_setup_module_fixture(self) -> None: autouse=True, scope="modul…
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
3/5 Requires solid familiarity with pytest internals — fixture registration, naming conventions, and unittest compatibility shims — beyond typical user-level knowledge.
reasoning_complexity
3/5 Solver must trace a behavioral regression (fixtures appearing in --fixtures output) back to a naming-convention change across multiple fixture-injection helpers in the codebase.
conceptual_leaps
2/5 One non-obvious jump: recognizing that 'private' visibility in pytest --fixtures is controlled solely by a leading underscore in the fixture name, and that this convention was broken in multiple symmetric injection sites.
plan_length
2/5 Roughly 4–6 actions: reproduce/understand the issue, locate the fixture-name construction in python.py and unittest.py, apply the underscore prefix in all five affected f-strings.
interdependence
2/5 Each of the five edits is independent; missing one leaves a subset of generated fixtures still exposed, but the others remain correct and the fix is locally recoverable.
Justification: —
Step graph — 11 nodes | bottlenecks: #2, #3, #4 The solution is dominated by repetitive low-complexity computational edits (adding a single `_` prefix in five places across two files); the only real intellectual work is recognizing the underscore-private-fixture convention (node 2), which is the sole bottleneck gating all five edits.
#1 [strategic] Reproduce and understand the regression complexity: 2/5
Run `pytest --fixtures` on a minimal unittest example to confirm that xunit/unittest fixture names appear publicly starting v6.2.0, when they should be hidden.
#2 [conceptual] Identify underscore-prefix convention for private fixtures complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Recognize that pytest treats fixture names beginning with `_` as private, hiding them from `--fixtures` output unless `-v` is also supplied.
#3 [conceptual] Locate xunit fixture injection in python.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Find the four `_inject_setup_*_fixture` methods in `src/_pytest/python.py` (lines ~528, ~557, ~809, ~838) where xunit-style fixture names are constructed and registered.
#4 [conceptual] Locate unittest fixture injection in unittest.py complexity: 2/5 ← #1
Find the single fixture registration call in `src/_pytest/unittest.py` (line ~144) where unittest setUp/tearDown fixture names are constructed.
#5 [computational] Prefix setup_module fixture name with underscore complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3
In `_inject_setup_module_fixture` (python.py:528), change the `name` f-string from `xunit_setup_module_fixture_{...}` to `_xunit_setup_module_fixture_{...}`.
#6 [computational] Prefix setup_function fixture name with underscore complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3
In `_inject_setup_function_fixture` (python.py:557), change the `name` f-string from `xunit_setup_function_fixture_{...}` to `_xunit_setup_function_fixture_{...}`.
#7 [computational] Prefix setup_class fixture name with underscore complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3
In `_inject_setup_class_fixture` (python.py:809), change the `name` f-string from `xunit_setup_class_fixture_{...}` to `_xunit_setup_class_fixture_{...}`.
#8 [computational] Prefix setup_method fixture name with underscore complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #3
In `_inject_setup_method_fixture` (python.py:838), change the `name` f-string from `xunit_setup_method_fixture_{...}` to `_xunit_setup_method_fixture_{...}`.
#9 [computational] Prefix unittest fixture name with underscore complexity: 1/5 ← #2, #4
In `src/_pytest/unittest.py` (line ~144), change the `name` f-string from `unittest_{setup_name}_fixture_{...}` to `_unittest_{setup_name}_fixture_{...}`.
#10 [verification] Verify fixtures hidden from plain --fixtures output complexity: 1/5 ← #5, #6, #7, #8, #9
Run `pytest --fixtures` on the minimal example and confirm none of the renamed fixtures appear in the output.
#11 [verification] Verify fixtures still visible with --fixtures -v complexity: 1/5 ← #5, #6, #7, #8, #9
Run `pytest --fixtures -v` and confirm the underscore-prefixed fixture names are still listed, ensuring no functional regression.
#48 SWE-bench Verified requests Trivial (1) nodes: 6 | bottlenecks: 2 swe_psf__requests-1766 ▼
Problem
quote qop options in Digest Auth Based on RFC2617 (http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2617), the value of 'qop-options' directive should be quoted with double quotes: ``` qop-options This directive is optional, but is made so only for backward compatibility with RFC 2069 [6]; it SHOULD be used by all implementations compliant with this version of the Digest scheme. If present, it i…
Solution (excerpt)
Repository: psf/requests Correct patch (git diff): ```diff diff --git a/requests/auth.py b/requests/auth.py --- a/requests/auth.py +++ b/requests/auth.py @@ -105,7 +105,7 @@ def sha_utf8(x): A1 = '%s:%s:%s' % (self.username, realm, self.password) A2 = '%s:%s' % (method, path) - …
Difficulty dimensions
domain_depth
2/5 Requires basic familiarity with HTTP Digest Auth and RFC2617, but the relevant spec section is explicitly cited in the problem.
reasoning_complexity
2/5 Solver reads the RFC excerpt, understands a quoting requirement, locates the relevant string format in code, and applies a one-character fix.
conceptual_leaps
1/5 No non-obvious insight needed — the problem statement directly identifies the bug and the spec that mandates the fix.
plan_length
1/5 Effectively two actions: locate the offending format string in requests/auth.py and add quotes around 'auth'.
interdependence
2/5 The single code change is self-contained; finding the file is a prerequisite but failure there is easily recoverable.
